# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme** End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. They follow the *Module-2 internal-choice* format, with sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set A]`
**Differentiate between Common Cathode (CC) and Common Anode (CA) seven-segment displays. Give the segment code (in hex) for the digit '7' in both configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Common Cathode (CC) | Common Anode (CA) |
|---|---|---|
| Common pin tied to | **GND (0 V)** | **Vcc (+5 V)** |
| Segment ON when anode is | **HIGH** | **LOW** |
| Current direction | MCU **sources** current | MCU **sinks** current |
| Bit value for 'ON' segment | **1** | **0** |

For digit '7', segments **a, b, c** are ON; all others OFF.
- CC code: `a b c d e f g = 1 1 1 1 0 0 0` → **0xF8**
- CA code: bit-inverted of CC → `0 0 0 0 1 1 1` → **0x07**

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 for table differentiation + 1 for CC code + 1 for CA code.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]`
**Why is a flyback diode connected across a relay coil? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**

A relay coil is an **inductor**. When the driving transistor switches OFF, the current through the coil cannot change instantaneously. The collapsing magnetic field induces a **high reverse-EMF** (back-EMF) given by $V = -L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$, which can reach **hundreds of volts** and destroy the BJT.

The **flyback diode** (1N4007) is connected in **reverse-bias** across the coil. When the transistor turns OFF, the diode becomes **forward-biased** by the induced EMF and provides a safe circulating path for the coil current, dissipating the stored magnetic energy as heat.

If the diode is **omitted**, the reverse-EMF can:
1. Punch through the BJT's collector-base junction, permanently damaging the transistor.
2. Cause erratic MCU resets due to voltage spikes on the supply rail.
3. Generate electromagnetic interference (EMI) affecting nearby circuits.

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 for back-EMF explanation + 1 for diode function + 1 for consequences of omission.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a **4-digit multiplexed common-cathode seven-segment display** with the 8051 microcontroller. List the segment codes (in hex) for digits 0, 1, 5, 9 in CC configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the current-limiting resistor for each segment, given $V_{CC} = 5$ V, LED forward voltage $V_F = 2.0$ V, desired segment current $I_F = 15$ mA. Also compute the multiplex refresh rate if the per-digit on-time is 5 ms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit and Codes (7 marks):**
- Connect the 7 segment lines (a–g, dp) of all 4 digits in **parallel** to PORT 0 of the 8051 through current-limiting resistors.
- Connect the **common cathode of each digit** to a separate PNP transistor (or p-MOSFET) driven by PORT 2 bits P2.0–P2.3.
- Only one digit's transistor is ON at a time, enabling that digit; the other digits are OFF (blanked).
- The 8051 rapidly cycles through digits 1→2→3→4→1 at a rate >60 Hz, exploiting persistence of vision.

**Segment Codes (CC, 'ON' = 1):**

| Digit | g f e d c b a | Hex Code |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 | **0x3F** |
| 1 | 0 0 0 0 1 1 0 | **0x06** |
| 5 | 1 0 1 1 0 1 1 | **0x6D** |
| 9 | 1 1 0 1 1 1 1 | **0x6F** |

**(b) Design Calculations (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Voltage across the resistor:

$$V_R = V_{CC} - V_F = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

Step 2 — Resistance value:

$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{3.0}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$$

Step 3 — Refresh rate from per-digit on-time:

$$f_{refresh} = \frac{1}{N \cdot t_{digit}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 5 \text{ ms}} = \frac{1}{20 \text{ ms}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** Since 50 Hz < 60 Hz, there will be perceptible flicker. **Recommended:** reduce $t_{digit}$ to ≤ 4 ms to achieve ≥ 62.5 Hz.

**Final Answer:** $R = 200 \text{ }\Omega$, $f_{refresh} = 50$ Hz (recommend ≤ 4 ms per digit).

---

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Alternative]`

**(a)** Describe the **HD44780-based 16×2 LCD** in 4-bit mode interfacing with an 8051. List the pins used and write the initialization sequence with correct command codes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4×4 matrix keypad is connected to PORT 1 of the 8051 (rows on P1.0–P1.3, columns on P1.4–P1.7). During a scan, the row pattern sent is `0xFD` and the column pattern read back is `0xBE`. Identify the pressed key using the lookup table. Justify the need for software debounce. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LCD 4-bit Mode Interface (7 marks):**
- **Pins used:** RS, R/W, E, D4, D5, D6, D7 (total 7 GPIOs).
- D0–D3 of the LCD are tied to GND (unused in 4-bit mode).
- A 10 kΩ potentiometer is connected to VEE for contrast adjustment.

**Initialization Sequence:**

| Step | Command | Hex | Delay |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power-on wait | — | 15 ms |
| 2 | Function set (8-bit init) | 0x03 | 4.1 ms |
| 3 | Function set (8-bit init) | 0x03 | 100 µs |
| 4 | Function set (8-bit init) | 0x03 | 100 µs |
| 5 | Switch to 4-bit mode | 0x02 | 100 µs |
| 6 | Function Set: 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 | 0x28 | 37 µs |
| 7 | Display ON, Cursor OFF | 0x0C | 37 µs |
| 8 | Entry Mode: Increment, no shift | 0x06 | 37 µs |
| 9 | Clear Display | 0x01 | 1.53 ms |

To send a command in 4-bit mode: upper nibble is sent first (E pulse), then lower nibble (E pulse).

**(b) Keypad Identification & Debounce (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Decode row pattern `0xFD = 1111\,1101_2`. Bit 1 (P1.1) is LOW → **Row 1** is active.

Step 2 — Decode column pattern `0xBE = 1011\,1110_2`. Reading bits 7, 6, 5, 4: `C3=1, C2=0, C1=1, C0=1`. Bit 5 (C1) is LOW → **Column 1** is active.

Step 3 — Identify the key at **(R1, C1)** from the standard lookup table:

|  | C0 | **C1** | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | 4 | **5** | 6 | B |

The pressed key is **'5'**.

Step 4 — Justification for software debounce: Mechanical key contacts physically *bounce* (open/close rapidly) for **5–20 ms** before settling into a stable state. Without debounce, the MCU would interpret a single keypress as multiple presses, causing erroneous data entry. A software delay of **~10–20 ms** after first detection filters out the bounce and confirms a valid, single keypress.

**Final Answer:** Key **'5'** is pressed; debounce delay = 10–20 ms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CA and CC codes:** Many students write the *CA code* when the question asks for *CC*. Always re-read carefully and verify the common-pin voltage.
> 2. **Forgetting the flyback diode:** The single most common omission. If your relay circuit lacks a diode, you may lose 2–3 marks outright.
> 3. **Wrong Enable timing on the LCD:** Students often forget that the E pin is **active on the HIGH-to-LOW transition**. Set E HIGH → wait ≥ 230 ns → set E LOW. Sending data on the LOW-to-HIGH edge is wrong.
> 4. **Insufficient debounce delay:** 1 ms is **not enough**. Always use 10–20 ms and *justify* it.
> 5. **Not showing the base resistor calculation in relay problems:** Examiner expects KVL/Ohm's Law, not just a "guessed" value.
> 6. **Multiplex refresh rate < 60 Hz:** This produces visible flicker. Always cross-check the rate at the end of your design.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a **last-minute revision checklist** before the KTU examination.

- [ ] **7-Segment Display:** Know CC vs CA; memorize hex codes for 0–9; formula $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$.
- [ ] **Multiplexing:** $f_{refresh} \geq 60$ Hz; $I_{peak} = I_{avg} / D$ where $D = 1/N$.
- [ ] **LCD HD44780:** 4-bit mode uses 7 pins; E pin is latched on HIGH-to-LOW; key commands are 0x01, 0x06, 0x0C, 0x28.
- [ ] **LCD Initialization:** 15 ms + 4.1 ms + 100 µs (×3) + standard commands ≈ 21 ms total.
- [ ] **LCD Enable pulse width:** $t_{PWE} \geq 230$ ns (non-negotiable per datasheet).
- [ ] **Matrix Keypad 4×4:** 8 wires, active-low scanning, debounce 10–20 ms.
- [ ] **Key identification:** Row LOW + Column LOW = intersection = pressed key (lookup table).
- [ ] **Relay coil current:** ~70–100 mA at 5 V — **cannot be driven directly** by MCU GPIO.
- [ ] **Relay driver:** Use BC547 NPN BJT; base resistor $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE})/I_B$ with $\beta = 100$.
- [ ] **Flyback diode:** 1N4007, reverse-biased across coil, cathode to Vcc — **mandatory, never omit**.
- [ ] **Galvanic isolation:** Relay provides isolation between low-voltage MCU and high-voltage load.
- [ ] **KTU exam priority order (typical):** 7-Segment (highest) > LCD > Relay > Keypad, but all four carry marks every year.
- [ ] **Mandatory diagrams in your answer sheet:** Circuit diagram (not just a block diagram), pin connections, and a timing diagram (especially for LCD).
- [ ] **Show all calculation steps** — KTU awards step marks, not just final-answer marks.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Interfacing: Seven-Segment Display, LCD Display, Matrix Keypad, and Relay**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog to Digital Conversion: Potentiometer, Temperature Sensor, LDR, and Microphone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analog to Digital Conversion: Potentiometer, Temperature Sensor, LDR, and Microphone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** in a microcontroller context is the process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal (voltage) into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code that the CPU can process. In the **PEADT418 syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, this involves interfacing four fundamental analog transducers — the **Potentiometer**, **Temperature Sensor (LM35/TMP36)**, **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)**, and **Microphone** — with the on-chip ADC of a target microcontroller (e.g., ATmega328P, STM32, or LPC2148).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Analog-to-Digital Conversion is the translation of real-world physical quantities — mechanical displacement, thermal energy, luminous intensity, and acoustic pressure — into a proportional analog voltage, which is then quantized into a binary representation by the microcontroller's successive-approximation or sigma-delta ADC peripheral.*

### 1.2 The Four Transducers — A Quick Glance

| Transducer | Physical Quantity | Output Nature | Output Range (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | Mechanical Rotation / Position | Resistive (Voltage Divider) | 0 V to V_ref (e.g., 0–5 V) |
| **Temperature Sensor (LM35)** | Heat | Linear Voltage | 0 V to 1.5 V (for 0–150 °C) |
| **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** | Luminous Intensity | Resistive (Voltage Divider) | 0 V to V_ref (logarithmic mapping) |
| **Microphone (Electret + Amplifier)** | Acoustic Pressure | AC + DC Voltage | 0 V to V_ref (with V_ref/2 bias) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Language Translator"

Imagine you are an English-only person (**microcontroller/digital CPU**) sitting in a room with a French-only guest (**the physical world of analog signals**). You literally cannot understand each other.

The **ADC** is like a **simultaneous interpreter** sitting between you. The interpreter does not just translate — they also:
1. **Listen for a fixed duration** (this is **sampling**, governed by the Sampling Theorem).
2. **Round the speaker's words to the nearest "level"** the listener can count on their fingers (this is **quantization**, governed by the ADC's bit resolution).
3. **Write down a number** (e.g., 0, 1, 2 … 1023) that represents what was said (this is the **binary digital output**).

The four sensors (Potentiometer, LM35, LDR, Microphone) are simply **"microphones for different realities"** — each one converts a unique physical phenomenon into the same universal language: an **analog voltage between 0 V and V_ref**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these four sensors grouped together in Module 2?**
> KTU groups them because they are the canonical teaching examples of **unipolar single-ended analog inputs** — every engineering student must master their signal-conditioning circuits, since 80% of embedded sensor design uses this exact pattern.

---

### 1.4 Block-Level Functional Overview

The complete signal chain is identical for all four sensors:

$$ \text{Physical Quantity} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Analog Voltage} \xrightarrow{\text{Signal Conditioning}} V_{in} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Digital Code} \xrightarrow{\text{CPU}} \text{Action} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem):**
> To faithfully reconstruct an analog signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the ADC sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> For the human voice microphone (~4 kHz bandwidth), $f_s \geq 8$ kHz is mandatory (hence CD-quality audio uses 44.1 kHz).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ADC — Operational Breakdown

Modern microcontroller ADCs (e.g., the ATmega328P's 10-bit SAR ADC) execute the conversion in **four disciplined stages**:

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** A track/hold capacitor charges to $V_{in}$ and freezes it during conversion. Without this, mid-conversion voltage changes would corrupt the result.
2. **Quantization:** The held voltage is compared against $2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ is the ADC's bit resolution.
3. **Encoding:** The matched level is assigned a binary code from $0$ to $2^n - 1$.
4. **Output Latching:** The result is loaded into the `ADC` data register (e.g., `ADCL`/`ADCH` on AVR) for CPU reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind SAR (Successive Approximation Register):**
> SAR ADCs are the dominant topology in KTU-targeted microcontrollers because they offer an excellent trade-off between **speed (typically 15 µs conversion on AVR)**, **resolution (10–12 bits)**, and **silicon area / cost**. They work by binary-searching the correct voltage level using an internal DAC.

### 2.2 Step Size (Resolution Voltage) — The Most Important Equation

The **step size (LSB voltage)** $\Delta V$ is the smallest voltage difference the ADC can distinguish:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

where:
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (typically 5.0 V, 3.3 V, or 1.1 V internal)
- $n$ = number of ADC bits (8, 10, 12, or 16 bits)

The **digital output code** $D$ for an input $V_{in}$ is:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Conversion (Engineering Use):** To recover the analog voltage from a digital reading $D$:
> $$V_{in} = D \cdot \Delta V = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$$
> This equation appears in nearly every KTU Part A and Part B question on this topic — memorize it.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC step size / LSB voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}$ | Digital code from input voltage | Unitless integer $[0, 2^n-1]$ |
| 3 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Input voltage from digital code | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage divider output | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $T(^{\circ}C) = V_{out} \times 100$ | LM35 linear conversion | Celsius (°C) |
| 6 | $T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{out} - 0.5}{0.01}$ | TMP36 conversion | Celsius (°C) |
| 7 | $R_{LDR} = \dfrac{R_{fixed} \cdot (2^n - D)}{D}$ | LDR resistance from divider | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR of ideal ADC | Decibels (dB) |
| 10 | $I_{ADC} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{ref}^-}{V_{ref}^+ - V_{ref}^-}$ | Generalized ADC input mapping | Normalized $[0, 1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The table above uses `\dfrac` and `\cdot` instead of the `|` pipe character for fractions. This preserves the Markdown table parser — never use `|V_ref|` style notation inside table cells.

### 2.4 Sensor-Specific Theoretical Insights

**A) Potentiometer:**
A potentiometer is a **3-terminal variable resistor** acting as an adjustable voltage divider. Wiper voltage $V_{w}$ is:

$$V_{w} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{w \rightarrow GND}}{R_{total}}$$

It is the **simplest ADC input** because it requires no signal conditioning.

**B) LM35 Temperature Sensor:**
- Output is **linearly proportional** to Celsius: **10 mV / °C**
- Calibration offset: **0 V at 0 °C** (no offset subtraction needed — major advantage over TMP36)
- Does **not require calibration or external trimming**
- Operating range: **0 °C to +150 °C** (for the basic LM35)

**C) LDR (Light Dependent Resistor):**
- Resistance **decreases** as light intensity **increases** (photoconductivity)
- Forms a voltage divider with a fixed pull-down resistor (typically 10 kΩ)
- Response is **logarithmic**, not linear — a common exam pitfall

**D) Microphone (Electret Condenser with Amplifier):**
- Outputs a **small AC signal** (~mV) superimposed on a **DC bias** (typically $V_{cc}/2$)
- Requires an **amplifier** (e.g., MAX9814, LM358) to bring signal within the ADC's 0–5 V range
- The DC bias must be **AC-coupled** (via a capacitor) or digitally subtracted in firmware

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Sensor Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Volume / Brightness Knobs (TVs, Phones) | Potentiometer (digital or analog) | Cheap, infinite resolution, no power needed |
| HVAC Systems, Medical Thermometers | LM35 / TMP36 | Linear, calibrated, no ADC offset math |
| Automatic Street Lights, Cameras | LDR | Low cost, robust, sufficient for on/off decisions |
| Voice Recorders, Smart Speakers, Alexa | Electret / MEMS Microphone | Wide bandwidth, low noise after amplification |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Digital Output $D$ from Analog Input $V_{in}$

We start from the **fundamental quantization principle**: the ADC partitions the reference range $[0, V_{ref}]$ into $2^n$ equal intervals of width $\Delta V$.

**Step 1 — Define the step size.**
By definition of uniform quantization, the smallest distinguishable voltage increment is:
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Map the input to a level index.**
If the input voltage $V_{in}$ falls in the $k$-th quantization interval, the number of full $\Delta V$ steps that fit below $V_{in}$ is the digital code:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta V$.**
Substituting the expression from Step 1 into Step 2:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref} / 2^n} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**
$$\boxed{D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor}$$
The floor function $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ enforces integer truncation (a real ADC always rounds **down** the bin index).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 10-bit ADC with Potentiometer

> **Problem:** A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V reads a potentiometer wiper at $V_{in} = 2.43$ V. Find (a) the step size, (b) the digital output, and (c) the percentage of full scale.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $n = 10 \Rightarrow 2^{10} = 1024$
- $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V
- $V_{in} = 2.43$ V

**Step 2 — Compute the step size $\Delta V$.**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 0.0048828125 \text{ V} = 4.883 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the digital code $D$.**
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{2.43 \times 1024}{5.0} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2488.32}{5.0} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 497.664 \right\rfloor = 497$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of full scale.**
$$\% \text{FS} = \frac{D}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \frac{497}{1023} \times 100 \approx 48.58\%$$

**Step 5 — Verification (reverse conversion).**
$$V_{in,recovered} = \frac{497 \times 5.0}{1024} = 2.4268 \text{ V}$$
Quantization error: $|2.43 - 2.4268| = 3.2$ mV, which is less than one $\Delta V$ (4.88 mV). ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All Four Sensor Conversions

For an **n-bit ADC** with reference $V_{ref}$ reading digital code $D$:

| Sensor | Engineering Quantity $Q$ as a Function of $D$ | Inverse Engineering Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | $V_{w} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $D = \dfrac{V_w \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}}$ |
| **LM35 (Celsius)** | $T = \dfrac{V_{w} \cdot 100}{1} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref} \cdot 100}{2^n}$ | $D = \dfrac{T \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref} \cdot 100}$ |
| **TMP36 (Celsius)** | $T = \dfrac{V_{w} - 0.5}{0.01}$ (in firmware) | Combine with $V_w$ formula |
| **LDR** | $R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - D}{D}$ | $D = \dfrac{R_{fixed} \cdot 2^n}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$ |
| **Microphone** | $V_{peak} = \dfrac{D_{peak} \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n} - \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2}$ | $D_{peak} = \dfrac{V_{peak} + V_{ref}/2}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n$ |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Temperature in °C from LM35 ADC Reading

> **Problem:** ATmega328P ($V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, 10-bit ADC) returns $D = 214$ from an LM35. Find the temperature in °C.

**Step 1 — Convert $D$ to analog voltage using the reverse ADC formula.**
$$V_{in} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{214 \times 5.0}{1024} = 1.0449 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LM35 transfer function (10 mV/°C).**
$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01 \text{ V/°C}} = \frac{1.0449}{0.01} = 104.49 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single firmware-friendly expression.**
$$T = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 0.01} = \frac{D \cdot 5.0}{1024 \times 0.01} = \frac{D}{2.048}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with $D = 214$:**
$$T = \frac{214}{2.048} = 104.49 \text{ °C} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation — LDR Resistance from Digital Code

> **Setup:** LDR forms a voltage divider with $R_{fixed} = 10$ kΩ (pull-down to GND). The divider output goes to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Reading is $D = 768$.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider equation.**
$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ADC voltage formula $V_{out} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$.**
$$\frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

**Step 3 — Assume $V_{cc} = V_{ref} = 5.0$ V (typical design) and solve for $R_{LDR}$.**
$$D \cdot R_{fixed} = 2^n \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{V_{cc}} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed} \cdot R_{LDR} \cdot V_{cc}}{(R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}) \cdot V_{ref}}$$

Simplifying (with $V_{ref} = V_{cc}$):
$$D = \frac{2^n \cdot R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and isolate $R_{LDR}$.**
$$D \cdot (R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}) = 2^n \cdot R_{fixed}$$
$$D \cdot R_{LDR} = 2^n \cdot R_{fixed} - D \cdot R_{fixed} = R_{fixed} \cdot (2^n - D)$$
$$\boxed{R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \cdot \frac{2^n - D}{D}}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numbers.**
$$R_{LDR} = 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{1024 - 768}{768} = 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{256}{768} \approx 3333 \, \Omega \approx 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Interpretation:** $R_{LDR} = 3.33$ kΩ indicates a **bright environment** (low LDR resistance), consistent with the high digital reading of 768/1023 ≈ 75% of full scale.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the term **resolution of an ADC**. For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, calculate the smallest voltage change that can be detected.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Resolution is the smallest change in analog input voltage that an ADC can detect, equal to one LSB (Least Significant Bit) step size.
- **Formula (1 Mark):** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096}$
- **Result (1 Mark):** $\Delta V \approx 0.0008057 \text{ V} = 805.66 \, \mu\text{V}$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** An LM35 temperature sensor outputs **650 mV** at a certain ambient temperature. Determine the temperature in °C.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Given (1 Mark):** LM35 scale factor = 10 mV/°C
- **Formula (1 Mark):** $T(^\circ C) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{0.01 \, \text{V/°C}}$
- **Result (1 Mark):** $T = \dfrac{0.650}{0.01} = 65 \, ^\circ\text{C}$

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. State one advantage and one disadvantage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram (3 Marks):**
   - S/H → Comparator → SAR logic → N-bit register → Internal DAC (binary-weighted resistor or R-2R ladder)
   - The SAR register holds the current approximation. The DAC converts it back to analog. The comparator tests $V_{in}$ against $V_{DAC}$. Based on the result, the SAR sets or clears bits MSB-first.

2. **Working steps (3 Marks):**
   - **Step 1:** MSB is set to 1; DAC output becomes $V_{ref}/2$. If $V_{in} \geq V_{ref}/2$, MSB remains 1; else it is reset to 0.
   - **Step 2:** Next bit is set to 1, producing $V_{ref}/4$ (or $3V_{ref}/4$). Test again.
   - **Step 3:** Repeat for all $n$ bits. After $n$ clock cycles, the SAR contains the binary equivalent of $V_{in}$.

3. **Advantages (0.5 Mark):** Moderate speed, low power, high resolution (up to 18 bits).
4. **Disadvantages (0.5 Mark):** Requires a precise internal DAC; slower than flash ADC.

**(b)** An **8-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V gives an output of **0xB2**. Compute the input voltage, the step size, and the quantization error. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Step size (2 Marks):**
   $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^8} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

2. **Convert hex to decimal (1 Mark):**
   $$0xB2 = (11 \times 16) + 2 = 176 + 2 = 178$$

3. **Compute input voltage (2 Marks):**
   $$V_{in} = D \cdot \Delta V = 178 \times 0.01953 = 3.477 \text{ V}$$

4. **Quantization error (2 Marks):**
   $$Q_e = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{19.53}{2} = \pm 9.77 \text{ mV}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram how a **Potentiometer, an LDR, and an LM35 temperature sensor** can be interfaced to the ADC of an ATmega328P microcontroller. Show the necessary formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Potentiometer (2 Marks):**
   - Connect the two end terminals to **VCC (5 V)** and **GND**. Connect the **wiper** to **ADC0 (PC0)**.
   - Formula: $V_{w} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{bottom}}{R_{total}}$. The wiper voltage linearly maps rotation 0–100% to 0–5 V.

2. **LDR Voltage Divider (2 Marks):**
   - LDR between VCC and ADC1 (PC1). A fixed **10 kΩ** resistor between ADC1 and GND.
   - $R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - D}{D}$

3. **LM35 Interfacing (2 Marks):**
   - LM35 **VCC** to +5 V, **GND** to ground, **Vout** to **ADC2 (PC2)**. Add a **100 nF** decoupling capacitor between Vout and GND for noise filtering.
   - $T(^\circ C) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{0.01} = \dfrac{D \cdot 5.0}{1024 \times 0.01}$

4. **Firmware (1 Mark):** Use `analogRead(A0)`, `analogRead(A1)`, `analogRead(A2)` and apply the formulas above.

**(b)** A microphone module outputs a peak-to-peak AC signal of **200 mV** centered on **2.5 V DC bias**. Design the signal conditioning chain (gain, coupling, clamping) before feeding it to a **3.3 V, 12-bit ADC**. Compute the expected digital range of the output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **DC blocking (1.5 Marks):** Insert a **series capacitor (10 µF)** at the microphone output to remove the 2.5 V DC bias. After the cap, the signal swings between **+100 mV and −100 mV** about 0 V.

2. **Amplifier design (2 Marks):** Use a **non-inverting op-amp** with gain:
   $$G = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 1 + \frac{49 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 50$$
   Peak signal becomes $100 \text{ mV} \times 50 = 5 \text{ V}$ (too high — so reduce to $G = 16$ for a clean ±1.6 V swing).

3. **DC bias restoration (1 Mark):** Add a **2.5 V reference** to the amplified signal at the op-amp's non-inverting input to re-center the waveform within the ADC's 0–3.3 V range.

4. **Clamping (1 Mark):** Use two Schottky diodes (BAT54S) — anode to GND, cathode to signal, and the reverse — to clamp the signal between 0 V and 3.3 V, protecting the ADC from overvoltage.

5. **Digital range calculation (1.5 Marks):** With peak swing ±1.6 V around 1.65 V (midpoint of 3.3 V):
   $$V_{min} = 1.65 - 1.6 = 0.05 \text{ V} \Rightarrow D_{min} = \frac{0.05 \times 4096}{3.3} \approx 62$$
   $$V_{max} = 1.65 + 1.6 = 3.25 \text{ V} \Rightarrow D_{max} = \frac{3.25 \times 4096}{3.3} \approx 4034$$
   The ADC will produce codes spanning **62 to 4034** for full-volume audio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{ref}$ value** in the step-size formula (0.5 mark penalty).
> 2. **Mixing up TMP36 vs LM35** — TMP36 has a 0.5 V offset at 0 °C, LM35 has 0 V offset. Many students apply $T = V \times 100$ to both, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Not applying the floor function** in the digital output formula — examiners look for `floor( )` notation.
> 4. **LDR voltage divider direction** — students often place the fixed resistor on top, which inverts the response. Always put the **LDR at the top** (connected to VCC) when you want brightness ∝ $D$.
> 5. **Forgetting AC coupling for microphones** — feeding the DC bias directly into the ADC saturates the input. Always use a series capacitor.
> 6. **In Part B, writing code instead of circuit diagrams** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **hardware diagrams, formulas, and derivation steps**; raw code is acceptable only when paired with these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU Exams:**

- [ ] **Step size equation:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ — this is the single most tested formula in Module 2.
- [ ] **Reverse formula:** $V_{in} = (D \cdot V_{ref}) / 2^n$ — used in every sensor reading.
- [ ] **LM35 scale:** 10 mV per °C, **no offset**.
- [ ] **TMP36 scale:** 10 mV per °C, **0.5 V offset** (subtract 0.5 V before dividing by 0.01).
- [ ] **LDR behavior:** Resistance **decreases** with light; place LDR at the **top** of the voltage divider for a positive correlation with brightness.
- [ ] **Microphone chain:** Mic → **Series Capacitor (AC coupling)** → **Amplifier (gain stage)** → **DC bias restore** → **Clamping diodes** → ADC.
- [ ] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — voice needs at least 8 kHz sampling.
- [ ] **SAR ADC advantages:** Low power, 10–18 bit resolution, moderate speed (µs range).
- [ ] **Quantization error** is $\pm \Delta V / 2$, **not** $\pm \Delta V$.
- [ ] **SNR formula** for ideal ADC: $SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ (10-bit ADC ≈ 61.96 dB).
- [ ] **ADC clock prescaler** on AVR must be between **50 kHz and 200 kHz** for full 10-bit accuracy.
- [ ] **Microphone peak detection** in firmware: track the maximum value over a moving window, then divide by 2 for RMS approximation.
- [ ] **Voltage divider direction matters** — verify which terminal is at the ADC input to avoid inverted readings.
- [ ] **Resolution is NOT accuracy** — a 10-bit ADC can still be inaccurate due to noise, reference drift, and INL/DNL errors.
- [ ] **Always use a 100 nF decoupling capacitor** on ADC reference pins and sensor supply lines for clean readings.

> **Final Note:** For KTU 2024 Scheme exams, structure your answers as: **(1) Diagram → (2) Formula → (3) Substitution → (4) Numerical Answer → (5) Physical Interpretation.** This 5-step pattern is exactly what KTU examiners award full marks to.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital to Analog Conversion: Simple DAC Output, Sine Wave Generation, and Audio Signal Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital to Analog Conversion: Simple DAC Output, Sine Wave Generation, and Audio Signal Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)?

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic circuit that converts **discrete digital binary codes** (typically from a microcontroller) into a **continuous analog voltage or current** proportional to the digital input value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A DAC is a device that reconstructs an analog signal from a digital input word. The output analog quantity $V_{out}$ is directly proportional to the digital input code $D$, scaled by the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ and the resolution $2^n$.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Digital Volume Knob" 🎛️

Imagine a high-precision volume knob in a music studio that **cannot be turned by hand**. Instead, you press **"up" or "down" buttons**, and the knob moves to **fixed, predetermined positions** (e.g., 0, 1, 2, 3... up to 1023). Each press changes the volume by a tiny, fixed step.

This is exactly how a DAC works:
- The **buttons** are the digital bits coming from the microcontroller.
- The **fixed positions** of the knob represent the **quantization levels** ($2^n$ levels for an *n*-bit DAC).
- The **final position** of the knob is the **analog output voltage**.

The more "buttons" (bits) you have, the smoother the volume change — just like a **1024-step** knob feels smoother than a **4-step** knob.

---

## 1.2 Why Do We Need DACs in Microcontrollers?

Microcontrollers are inherently **digital devices** — they understand only `0`s and `1`s. However, the real world is **analog**:
- Speakers need a **continuously varying voltage** to produce sound.
- Motors need an **analog control voltage** to vary their speed.
- Sensors output **analog signals** that we may need to *simulate* during testing.

A DAC bridges this **digital-to-physical** gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the LPC2148 microcontroller (ARM7TDMI), DAC is implemented as a **10-bit DAC peripheral** with two output channels (AOUT). The DAC output range is **0 V to VREF (typically 3.3 V)**, giving a resolution of $3.3/1024 \approx 3.22$ mV per LSB step.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters of a DAC

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value (LPC2148) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **Resolution** | $n$ | Number of bits in the digital input | **10 bits** |
| **Number of Levels** | $2^n$ | Discrete output steps | **1024 levels** |
| **Step Size (LSB)** | $\Delta V$ | Smallest voltage change per LSB | **3.22 mV** |
| **Reference Voltage** | $V_{ref}$ | Full-scale output reference | **3.3 V** |
| **Settling Time** | $t_s$ | Time to reach $\pm 0.5$ LSB of final value | **1 µs** |
| **Conversion Rate** | $f_{DAC}$ | Updates per second | **up to 1 MHz** |
| **Monotonicity** | — | Output never decreases when input increases | **Guaranteed** |

> [!TIP]
> **Higher resolution ($n$) = Smoother analog output.** Each additional bit **doubles** the number of output levels and **halves** the step size $\Delta V$, dramatically reducing quantization noise.

---

## 1.4 Applications in Embedded Systems

1. **Sine Wave Generation** — for power inverters, signal generators, and tone production.
2. **Audio Signal Generation** — for music playback, voice prompts, and sound effects.
3. **Function Generation** — producing triangular, sawtooth, and arbitrary waveforms.
4. **Motor Speed Control** — providing variable DC voltage to DC motor drivers.
5. **Analog Sensor Simulation** — emulating sensor outputs during system testing.
6. **Closed-Loop Control** — outputting control signals in PID-based systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental DAC Output Equation

The **core equation** governing every DAC (whether R-2R, weighted resistor, or PWM-based) is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{D}{2^n} \times V_{ref}$$

Where:
- $V_{out}$ = Analog output voltage
- $D$ = Decimal equivalent of the digital input word (range: 0 to $2^n - 1$)
- $n$ = Number of DAC bits (resolution)
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage (the maximum possible output)

**Expanded form for clarity:**

$$V_{out} = \left( \frac{b_{n-1}}{2^1} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2^2} + \frac{b_{n-3}}{2^3} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^n} \right) \times V_{ref}$$

Where $b_{n-1}$ is the **Most Significant Bit (MSB)** and $b_0$ is the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**.

---

## 2.2 Resolution & Step Size Derivation Logic

The **step size** (also called **LSB voltage** or **quantization step**) represents the smallest possible change in output voltage when the digital code increments by 1.

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

Where $V_{FS}$ is the **Full-Scale Voltage** (the output when all bits are 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A 10-bit DAC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$ has $\Delta V = 3.22$ mV. This means the output is a **staircase waveform**, not a perfectly smooth curve. The finer the steps, the closer the output approximates a true analog signal.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $V_{out} = (D/2^n) \cdot V_{ref}$ | General DAC output equation | Volts | All DAC problems |
| $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Step size (LSB voltage) | Volts | Resolution calculations |
| $D = (V_{out}/V_{ref}) \cdot 2^n$ | Digital code for desired output | Integer | Code generation |
| $V_{out(MSB)} = V_{ref}/2$ | Output when only MSB is high | Volts | Quick verification |
| $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz | Sine wave gen |
| $T_{table} = 1/f_{s}$ | Time between DAC updates | Seconds | Lookup table design |
| $D(t) = 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1}) \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$ | Digital code for sine wave | Integer | Waveform synthesis |
| $f_{PWM} = f_{clk} / (PR+1)$ | PWM frequency formula | Hz | PWM-based DAC |
| $V_{out(PWM)} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{ref}$ | PWM-as-DAC output (with filter) | Volts | PWM DAC |
| $\text{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR of ideal DAC | dB | Audio quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always specify the **units** in your final answer. A common mistake is writing $V_{out} = 1.65$ without mentioning **Volts**. Also, remember to **mention assumptions** (e.g., "assuming ideal DAC with no offset error").

---

## 2.4 Types of DAC Architectures (Conceptual Map)

### A) Weighted Resistor DAC
- Each bit controls a switch connected to either $V_{ref}$ or GND through a **different-valued resistor**.
- MSB uses $R$, next bit uses $2R$, then $4R$, $8R$, etc.
- **Drawback:** Requires a huge range of resistor values (e.g., 1:1024 for 10-bit), making it impractical for high resolution.

### B) R-2R Ladder DAC (Industry Standard ⭐)
- Uses only **two resistor values**: $R$ and $2R$.
- Each bit is fed through a switch to either $V_{ref}$ or GND.
- **Advantages:** Easy to fabricate in IC form, monotonic, high accuracy, low cost.
- **Used in:** LPC2148 internal DAC, audio equipment, function generators.

### C) PWM-Based DAC (Software Approach)
- A **PWM signal** is generated by the microcontroller and passed through a **low-pass RC filter**.
- The filter's average DC value = $D_{PWM} \times V_{ref}$.
- **Advantage:** Available on *any* microcontroller with PWM (no dedicated DAC pin needed).
- **Disadvantage:** Requires external filter, lower bandwidth.

### D) Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma$-$\Delta$) DAC
- Oversamples data using a **noise-shaping modulator** to push quantization noise out of the audio band.
- Used in **high-fidelity audio** (16/24-bit audio DACs in smartphones).

---

## 2.5 Sine Wave Generation Theory — The "Lookup Table" Approach

To generate a **pure sine wave** using a DAC, the microcontroller follows this conceptual pipeline:

> **Step 1: Pre-compute** digital values for one full cycle of the sine wave at the desired resolution.
> **Step 2: Store** these values in a lookup table (array) in memory.
> **Step 3: Output** each value to the DAC sequentially at a fixed rate (controlled by a timer interrupt).
> **Step 4: Repeat** indefinitely.

The digital value at any point in time is:

$$D(t) = \text{round}\left[ 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1} - 1) \cdot \sin(2\pi f t) \right]$$

**Breaking this down:**
- $2^{n-1}$ = **DC offset** (midpoint of DAC range) — centers the sine wave at $V_{ref}/2$.
- $(2^{n-1} - 1)$ = **Amplitude scaling** — maximizes swing without clipping.
- $\sin(2\pi f t)$ = **Pure sine function** oscillating between -1 and +1.
- `round()` = **Quantization** to nearest integer DAC code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the midpoint offset?** A sine wave has both positive and negative excursions. Since most microcontrollers (like LPC2148) have **unipolar DACs** (0 V to 3.3 V), we must add a DC offset of $V_{ref}/2$ to shift the negative half-cycle into the positive range.

---

## 2.6 Audio Signal Generation — Sampling Theory

For **audio signal generation**, the digital samples of a sound waveform are stored in memory and played back through a DAC at a precise rate.

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the audio signal.

| Audio Quality | Bit Depth ($n$) | Sample Rate ($f_s$) | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Telephone Voice | **8 bits** | 8 kHz | Intercom, telephony |
| FM Radio | **16 bits** | 32 kHz | Voice broadcast |
| CD Audio | **16 bits** | 44.1 kHz | Music CD |
| DVD Audio | **24 bits** | 96 kHz | Studio mastering |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Insight:** The human ear can hear up to about **20 kHz**. The CD standard uses 44.1 kHz (slightly above $2 \times 20$ kHz = 40 kHz) to satisfy the Nyquist criterion with a small safety margin — this is why **CDs are sampled at 44.1 kHz**.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems

| Engineering Field | DAC Application | Typical DAC Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Audio Electronics** | Music players, speakers | Sigma-Delta, 16-24 bit |
| **Industrial Control** | PLC analog output, motor drives | 12-16 bit R-2R |
| **Telecommunications** | Signal generators, modulators | 10-14 bit high-speed |
| **Medical Equipment** | ECG simulators, ultrasound | 12-16 bit precision |
| **Test & Measurement** | Function generators | 12-14 bit arbitrary |
| **Automotive** | Infotainment, sensor simulation | 10-12 bit integrated |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DAC Output Voltage for an n-Bit Converter

### Starting Point
We want to find the **analog output voltage** $V_{out}$ produced by a digital input word $D$ consisting of $n$ bits.

### Step 1: Express the Digital Word in Binary
Let the digital input be represented as an $n$-bit binary number:

$$D = b_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1} + b_{n-2} \cdot 2^{n-2} + b_{n-3} \cdot 2^{n-3} + \cdots + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0$$

Where each $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the value of the $i^{th}$ bit (from MSB to LSB).

### Step 2: Define the Maximum Possible Digital Value
The largest digital value occurs when **all bits are 1**:

$$D_{max} = 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-2} + \cdots + 2^1 + 2^0 = 2^n - 1$$

This is the **sum of a finite geometric series** with first term 1, common ratio 2, and $n$ terms.

### Step 3: Define the Full-Scale Voltage
When $D = D_{max} = 2^n - 1$, the output reaches its **maximum voltage** (just below $V_{ref}$):

$$V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n} \approx V_{ref}$$

> *Note:* The output is $V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n)$, which is *technically* $V_{ref}$ minus one LSB step. In most KTU problems, we approximate $V_{FS} \approx V_{ref}$ for simplicity.

### Step 4: Form the Proportionality Relationship
Since $V_{out}$ is **linearly proportional** to $D$ (this is a fundamental DAC property):

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} = \frac{D}{2^n}$$

### Step 5: Final Output Equation

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{D}{2^n} \times V_{ref}}$$

**Verification with boundary conditions:**
- When $D = 0$ (all bits OFF): $V_{out} = 0$ V. ✓
- When $D = 2^n - 1$ (all bits ON): $V_{out} \approx V_{ref}$. ✓
- When only MSB is ON ($D = 2^{n-1}$): $V_{out} = V_{ref}/2$. ✓ (half of full scale)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Size (LSB Voltage)

### Starting Point
The step size $\Delta V$ is the change in output voltage when the digital code increments by **exactly 1 LSB**.

### Step 1: Calculate Output at Code $D+1$

$$V_{out}(D+1) = \frac{D+1}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Output at Code $D$

$$V_{out}(D) = \frac{D}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$$

### Step 3: Subtract to Find the Step

$$\Delta V = V_{out}(D+1) - V_{out}(D) = \frac{D+1}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref} - \frac{D}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$$

### Step 4: Simplify Algebraically

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot (D + 1 - D) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**
For a 10-bit DAC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V:
- $\Delta V = 3.3 / 2^{10} = 3.3 / 1024 = 3.2227$ mV
- A 12-bit DAC: $\Delta V = 3.3 / 4096 = 0.806$ mV (4× finer)
- An 8-bit DAC: $\Delta V = 3.3 / 256 = 12.89$ mV (4× coarser)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sine Wave Lookup Table Generation

### Goal
Generate digital values for **one complete cycle** of a sine wave using an *n*-bit DAC, suitable for storing in a microcontroller's memory array.

### Step 1: Define the Pure Mathematical Sine Function
A sine wave oscillates between **-1 and +1**:

$$x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 2: Scale to the DAC's Full Range
A unipolar DAC can only output voltages from **0 to $V_{ref}$**. We must map $[-1, +1]$ to $[0, V_{ref}]$:

$$V_{out}(t) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} + \frac{V_{ref}}{2} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 3: Convert to Digital Codes
Using the inverse DAC equation $D = (V_{out}/V_{ref}) \cdot 2^n$:

$$D(t) = \frac{1}{V_{ref}} \cdot V_{out}(t) \cdot 2^n$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

$$D(t) = \frac{2^n}{V_{ref}} \cdot \left[ \frac{V_{ref}}{2} + \frac{V_{ref}}{2} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t) \right]$$

$$D(t) = 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-1} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 5: Quantize to Integer Values
Since DACs only accept **integer codes**, we apply rounding:

$$\boxed{D(k) = \text{round}\left[ 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1} - 1) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) \right]}$$

Where:
- $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ is the sample index
- $N$ is the **total number of samples per cycle** (table size)
- $n$ is the DAC resolution in bits

### Step 6: Worked Example (10-bit DAC, 36 samples per cycle)

| $k$ | Angle $\theta = 2\pi k/N$ | $\sin(\theta)$ | $D(k)$ (decimal) | $D(k)$ (hex) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0° | 0.000 | 512 | 0x200 |
| 3 | 30° | 0.500 | 768 | 0x300 |
| 6 | 60° | 0.866 | 956 | 0x3BC |
| 9 | 90° | 1.000 | 1023 | 0x3FF |
| 12 | 120° | 0.866 | 956 | 0x3BC |
| 18 | 180° | 0.000 | 512 | 0x200 |
| 27 | 270° | -1.000 | 1 | 0x001 |
| 35 | 350° | -0.174 | 423 | 0x1A7 |

**Note:** Due to symmetry, we only need to store **N/4** values and exploit the four-fold symmetry of the sine function (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th quadrants) — saving **75% of memory**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Audio Sampling Rate Calculation

### Problem
Generate a 1 kHz sine wave audio tone using a DAC with a lookup table of 100 samples. Calculate the required DAC update frequency.

### Step 1: Relate Output Frequency to Update Rate
The output frequency $f_{out}$ is determined by how fast we cycle through the table:

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_{update}}{N}$$

Where $N$ = number of samples in one cycle, $f_{update}$ = DAC update rate.

### Step 2: Solve for $f_{update}$

$$f_{update} = f_{out} \times N = 1000 \text{ Hz} \times 100 = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Time Between Updates

$$T_{update} = \frac{1}{f_{update}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000} = 10 \text{ µs}$$

### Step 4: Verify with Nyquist Theorem
The highest frequency component of a 1 kHz sine wave is 1 kHz. So:

$$f_s = 100 \text{ kHz} \gg 2 \times 1 \text{ kHz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

✅ Nyquist criterion is **easily satisfied** (50× oversampling).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Problem Pattern:** "A DAC updates every 50 µs. The lookup table has 64 samples for one cycle. Find the output frequency."
> **Solution:** $f_{update} = 1/50\mu s = 20$ kHz; $f_{out} = 20{,}000 / 64 = 312.5$ Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Microcontroller DAC-Based Signal Generation System

This diagram shows the complete data flow from **stored digital samples** to a **reconstructed analog output**, including the external signal conditioning stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LUT_SAMPLES\nLookup Table\nin ROM/RAM] --> B[DAC_CTRL\nDAC Controller\n10-bit Register]
    B --> C[DAC_LPC2148\nInternal R-2R\nLadder DAC]
    C --> D[OPAMP_BUF\nOutput Buffer\nOp-Amp Follower]
    D --> E[LPF_FILTER\nLow Pass Filter\nRC or Active]
    E --> F[ANALOG_OUT\nReconstructed\nAnalog Output]
    G[TMR_ISR\nTimer Interrupt\nSets Sample Rate] -.-> B
    H[REF_3V3\nVoltage Reference\n3.3V Supply] -.-> C

    classDef logicStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef analogStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,G logicStyle
    class C,D,E,H analogStyle
    class F ioStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **orange block** = digital processing (microcontroller side).
- The **blue block** = analog hardware (DAC + signal conditioning).
- The **green block** = final output to the external world.
- Dashed lines (`.->`) represent **control signals** (timer triggers, reference voltage) rather than data flow.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: Sine Wave Generation Algorithm

This flowchart represents the **software algorithm** that a microcontroller executes in a timer interrupt service routine (ISR) to produce a continuous sine wave.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([System Power On]) --> init[INIT: Configure DAC,\nEnable Timer ISR]
    init --> load[Load Sample Index\nk = 0]
    load --> wait{Timer Overflow\nInterrupt?}
    wait -- No --> wait
    wait -- Yes --> read[Read LUT Value\nval = sineTable k]
    read --> write[Write val to DAC Register\nDACR = val LSB 6]
    write --> inc[Increment Index\nk = k + 1]
    inc --> check{k = N\nCycle Complete?}
    check -- No --> wait
    check -- Yes --> reset[Reset k = 0\nStart New Cycle]
    reset --> wait

    classDef processStyle fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class init,load,read,write,inc,reset processStyle
    class start ioStyle
    class wait,check decisionStyle
```

**Key observations:**
- The **yellow diamond shapes** are decision points.
- The **loop is infinite** — the sine wave plays continuously.
- The **timer ISR rate** determines the audio output frequency.

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram: PWM-Based DAC for Audio

This shows an alternative DAC implementation using **Pulse Width Modulation** + **External Filter** — useful when no dedicated DAC hardware is available.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MCU_PWM\nPWM Module\nVariable Duty Cycle] --> B[PWM_WAVE\nPWM Pulse Train\nat PWM Frequency]
    B --> C[RC_LPF\nRC Low Pass Filter\nR and C Components]
    C --> D[DC_ANALOG\nFiltered DC Output\nProportional to Duty]
    E[OVERSAMPLE\nOversampling to\nMove Noise Out of Band] -.-> A

    classDef mcuStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filterStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,E mcuStyle
    class B,C,D filterStyle
```

**Design constraints:**
- PWM frequency $f_{PWM}$ must be **at least 10× the highest audio frequency** to allow the RC filter to work effectively.
- The **cutoff frequency** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ must be slightly above the highest audio frequency (e.g., 20 kHz for full audio).
- Trade-off: Higher $f_{PWM}$ → cleaner audio, but more CPU overhead.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Complete Audio Player Architecture

This represents a **full audio playback system** that reads digital audio samples (e.g., from flash memory) and plays them through a speaker.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FLASH_MEM\nWAV/MP3 Data\nStored Samples] --> B[MCU_DECODE\nMicrocontroller\nReads & Decodes]
    B --> C[BUFFER\nDMA or RAM\nBuffer]
    C --> D[DAC_OUT\nDAC Module\nLPC2148 or PWM]
    D --> E[OPAMP_AMP\nAudio Amplifier\nLM386 / TPA2005]
    E --> F[SPEAKER\n4 Ohm / 8 Ohm\nLoudspeaker]
    G[VREF_SUPPLY\nStable Voltage\nReference Source] -.-> D

    classDef storageStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef digitalStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef analogStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C storageStyle,digitalStyle
    class D,E,G analogStyle
    class F outputStyle
```

**Signal path summary:**
1. **Storage** (yellow) → 2. **Digital Processing** (blue) → 3. **Analog Conversion** (orange) → 4. **Sound Output** (green)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks Total)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC). List any two important specifications of a DAC.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic device that converts a digital binary input code into a proportional analog output voltage or current.

**Key Specifications of a DAC:**

1. **Resolution (n):** The number of bits in the digital input word. It determines the smallest change in output voltage (LSB) for a 1-bit change in input. A higher resolution yields a finer, smoother analog output.

2. **Settling Time ($t_s$):** The time required for the DAC output to settle and remain within $\pm 0.5$ LSB of its final value after a digital code change. A shorter settling time allows the DAC to be updated more rapidly.

3. *(Optional third — can be added for completeness):* **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The maximum output voltage span, equal to $V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n)$.

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Specification 1 (1 mark) + Specification 2 (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the role of a lookup table in sine wave generation using a DAC? Why is a DC offset added to the sine samples?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of a Lookup Table (LUT):**
A **lookup table** is a pre-computed array of digital values (in ROM or RAM) that represents one complete cycle of a sine wave, sampled at regular angular intervals. During playback, the microcontroller reads successive values from the LUT and writes them to the DAC at a fixed rate. This approach **eliminates real-time floating-point sine calculations**, which are computationally expensive for microcontrollers.

**Why a DC Offset is Added:**
Most microcontrollers (including LPC2148) have **unipolar DACs** that can only output voltages in the range **0 V to $V_{ref}$**. A pure sine wave, however, has both **positive and negative** excursions ($-1$ to $+1$).

To accommodate this, the sine values are **offset by $V_{ref}/2$** (i.e., a digital value of $2^{n-1}$), so that:
- A $\sin = +1$ maps to a digital code of $2^n - 1$ (≈ $V_{ref}$)
- A $\sin = 0$ maps to a digital code of $2^{n-1}$ (= $V_{ref}/2$)
- A $\sin = -1$ maps to a digital code of $0$ (= 0 V)

This ensures the entire sine wave fits within the DAC's output range without **clipping**.

**Mark distribution:** LUT role (1.5 marks) + DC offset explanation (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — DAC Fundamentals & Sine Wave Generation**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Explain the working of an R-2R ladder DAC with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for its output voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
The **R-2R Ladder DAC** is the most widely used DAC architecture in integrated circuits due to its use of only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$), making it easy to manufacture on silicon.

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Circuit Topology**
A 4-bit R-2R ladder consists of:
- **Vertical (shunt) resistors** of value $2R$ connected from each bit node to ground.
- **Horizontal (series) resistors** of value $R$ between successive bit nodes.
- A **terminating resistor** of value $2R$ at the end of the ladder.
- A **CMOS switch** at each bit input that connects the bit node to either $V_{ref}$ (if bit = 1) or ground (if bit = 0).

**Step 2: Thevenin Equivalent Analysis (Key Insight)**
At any node looking to the right, the equivalent resistance is always $2R$. This is the **fundamental recursive property** of the R-2R ladder:
- Looking right from any node, the resistance is $R + (2R \Vert 2R) = R + R = 2R$.

**Step 3: Voltage Divider Action**
When the MSB ($b_3$) is switched to $V_{ref}$ and all other bits to ground:
- The voltage at the MSB node is $V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2R}{R + 2R} = \frac{2V_{ref}}{3}$ (this is then halved at the next node).

**Step 4: Contribution of Each Bit**
- The MSB's contribution to $V_{out}$ is $V_{ref}/2$ (voltage is halved at each subsequent node).
- The next bit contributes $V_{ref}/4$.
- The next bit contributes $V_{ref}/8$, and so on.
- The LSB contributes $V_{ref}/2^n$.

**Step 5: General Output Equation**
Summing all bit contributions (with superposition):

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left( \frac{b_{n-1}}{2^1} + \frac{b_{n-2}}{2^2} + \frac{b_{n-3}}{2^3} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^n} \right)$$

Combining the binary weights into a single digital value $D$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{D}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}}$$

**Step 6: Verification**
- All bits = 0: $V_{out} = 0$ V ✓
- All bits = 1: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^n \approx V_{ref}$ ✓
- Only MSB = 1: $V_{out} = V_{ref}/2$ ✓

**Mark distribution:** Diagram (2 marks) + Step-by-step explanation (3 marks) + Derivation (2 marks).

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**With the help of a block diagram, explain the generation of a sine wave using a DAC and a microcontroller. Mention the role of a timer interrupt.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (described in text):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
   │  Sine Lookup │ ──▶ │  Micro-      │ ──▶ │  DAC         │ ──▶ │  Op-Amp      │ ──▶ OUTPUT
   │  Table (ROM) │     │  controller  │     │  (10-bit)    │     │  Buffer +    │
   └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     │  LPF         │
            ▲                  ▲                                    └──────────────┘
            │                  │
            └──── Timer ───────┘
                 Interrupt
```

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

**Step 1: Pre-computation of Lookup Table**
The sine wave is sampled at $N$ equally spaced points over one complete cycle ($0$ to $2\pi$ radians). For each point $k$, the digital value is computed as:

$$D(k) = \text{round}\left[ 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1} - 1) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) \right]$$

These $N$ values are stored in an array (e.g., `uint16_t sineTable[N]`) in the microcontroller's flash memory.

**Step 2: Timer Configuration**
A **hardware timer** (e.g., Timer0 in LPC2148) is configured to generate an interrupt at a precise frequency:

$$f_{timer} = f_{audio} \times N$$

For example, to generate a 1 kHz tone with $N = 100$ samples/cycle: $f_{timer} = 100{,}000$ Hz (every 10 µs).

**Step 3: Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**
Each time the timer overflows, the ISR executes the following steps:
1. **Read** the next value from the lookup table: `val = sineTable[index]`.
2. **Write** it to the DAC register: `DACR = (val << 6)`.
3. **Increment** the index: `index = (index + 1) % N`.

**Step 4: DAC Output**
The DAC continuously reconstructs the analog voltage corresponding to each digital code. Since the codes change in a sinusoidal pattern, the output forms a **staircase approximation** of a sine wave.

**Step 5: Smoothing Filter**
A **low-pass filter** (RC or active op-amp based) is connected at the DAC output to **smooth the staircase** and remove high-frequency harmonics, producing a clean sine wave.

**Role of Timer Interrupt:**
- **Precision:** The timer interrupt ensures that each DAC update occurs at a **precisely regular interval**, producing a constant output frequency.
- **Non-blocking:** The main CPU is free to perform other tasks; the DAC updates happen "in the background."
- **Frequency control:** The output frequency is **directly proportional** to the timer rate, allowing software-controlled pitch variation (e.g., for melody generation).

**Mark distribution:** Block diagram (2 marks) + Steps 1-3 (2 marks) + Timer role (1.5 marks) + Smoothing filter (1.5 marks).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Audio Signal Generation**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of audio signal generation using a DAC. Discuss the significance of the sampling theorem and bit depth in determining audio quality.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Audio Signal Generation:**
Audio signal generation is the process of synthesizing **sound waveforms** (speech, music, tones) using a DAC from digitally stored samples. The digital samples represent the **instantaneous amplitude** of the audio signal at regular time intervals. When played back at the correct rate, the DAC reconstructs the original waveform, which is then amplified and sent to a speaker to produce sound.

**System Pipeline:**
1. **Sampling:** An analog audio signal is sampled at regular intervals to create discrete digital values.
2. **Storage:** The digital samples are stored in memory (Flash, SD card, or ROM).
3. **Playback:** A microcontroller reads the samples sequentially and writes them to the DAC.
4. **Amplification:** The DAC output is amplified to drive a speaker.

**Significance of the Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon):**

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that a bandlimited analog signal can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least **twice the maximum frequency component** $f_{max}$ present in the signal:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

- If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ → **Aliasing** occurs (false low-frequency artifacts appear).
- The human ear hears up to **20 kHz**, so CD audio uses $f_s = 44.1$ kHz.
- A higher sampling rate captures more **high-frequency detail** but requires more memory and faster DAC.

**Significance of Bit Depth (Resolution):**

The bit depth $n$ determines the **amplitude resolution** — the number of distinct voltage levels available to represent the signal.

- The **quantization step size** is: $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$
- The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** for an ideal DAC is:

$$\text{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

| Bit Depth | Levels | SNR (theoretical) | Use Case |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 8-bit | 256 | 49.9 dB | Telephone |
| 16-bit | 65,536 | 98.1 dB | CD audio |
| 24-bit | 16,777,216 | 146.2 dB | Studio mastering |

- **Higher bit depth = lower quantization noise = better dynamic range.**
- CD quality (16-bit, 44.1 kHz) provides 98 dB SNR and captures 20 kHz bandwidth — more than adequate for human hearing.

**Conclusion:** For high-fidelity audio, both **high sampling rate** (to capture high frequencies) and **high bit depth** (to capture amplitude detail) are essential. The choice depends on the **application's quality requirements**, **memory constraints**, and **DAC capabilities**.

**Mark distribution:** Concept (2 marks) + Sampling theorem (2.5 marks) + Bit depth (2.5 marks).

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Describe the generation of audio signals using PWM as a DAC. Mention its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of PWM-based DAC:**
A **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** signal is a square wave with a fixed frequency but a **variable duty cycle**. When this signal is passed through a **low-pass filter** (RC network), the filter's output is a **DC voltage** equal to the **average value** of the PWM signal, which is directly proportional to the duty cycle.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

The average output voltage of a PWM signal is:

$$V_{out} = D_{PWM} \times V_{ref}$$

Where:
- $D_{PWM}$ = Duty cycle (ratio of ON time to total period), ranging from 0 to 1
- $V_{ref}$ = Supply voltage (e.g., 3.3 V or 5 V)

By **rapidly changing the duty cycle** in a pattern that mimics an audio waveform, and using a low-pass filter, we can synthesize an audio signal.

**Generation Process (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Configure PWM Module**
- Set the PWM frequency: $f_{PWM} = f_{clock} / (PR + 1)$
- For audio, $f_{PWM}$ should be **at least 10× the highest audio frequency** (e.g., 200 kHz for 20 kHz audio).

**Step 2: Update Duty Cycle in ISR**
A timer interrupt (e.g., at 44.1 kHz for CD quality) executes:
1. Read the next audio sample from memory.
2. Convert it to a duty cycle value: `duty = (sample / 2^n) × (PR + 1)`.
3. Update the PWM compare register: `PWMMR = duty`.

**Step 3: External Low-Pass Filter**
Connect an **RC filter** (or active op-amp filter) to the PWM output:
- Cutoff frequency: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- Choose $f_c$ slightly above the highest audio frequency (e.g., 22 kHz).

**Step 4: Audio Amplification**
The filtered signal is weak (low current). Amplify it using an **audio amplifier IC** (e.g., LM386) before driving a speaker.

**Advantages of PWM-as-DAC:**

1. **No dedicated DAC hardware required** — available on virtually every microcontroller.
2. **Low cost** — only an external RC filter and amplifier are needed.
3. **Flexible resolution** — bit depth is determined by the timer's PWM register width.
4. **Easy to interface** with software — just update one register per sample.
5. **No precision components** required (unlike R-2R ladder, which needs matched resistors).

**Limitations of PWM-as-DAC:**

1. **Requires external filter** — adds cost, board space, and design complexity.
2. **Lower bandwidth** — limited by the PWM frequency and filter response.
3. **Quantization noise** — PWM introduces significant high-frequency noise that must be filtered.
4. **Lower audio fidelity** — not suitable for **high-fidelity** (hi-fi) audio; better for **voice prompts and tones**.
5. **CPU overhead** — requires frequent ISR updates, consuming CPU cycles.
6. **Glitches and jitter** — slight variations in PWM timing can introduce audible artifacts.

**Comparison with Hardware DAC:**

| Feature | PWM DAC | Hardware DAC (R-2R) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Hardware needed | RC filter | None (integrated) |
| Audio quality | Low-Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher |
| Setup complexity | Medium | Low |

**Mark distribution:** Concept + equation (1.5 marks) + Steps 1-3 (2 marks) + Advantages (1.5 marks) + Limitations (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing $V_{out} = 1.65$ without "Volts" costs **0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Off-by-one error:** Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ for the maximum digital code. Remember: the maximum code is $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$.
> 3. **DC offset confusion:** Forgetting to add $2^{n-1}$ offset when generating sine waves on a unipolar DAC. This causes the sine wave to **clip** at 0 V.
> 4. **Confusing sampling theorem:** Writing "$f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$" instead of "$\geq$". The theorem says the sampling rate must be **at least** twice the signal frequency.
> 5. **Missing labels in diagrams:** Drawing a block diagram without labeling "DAC," "LUT," or "Timer" will be penalized. Always label **every block**.
> 6. **No mention of smoothing filter:** When explaining DAC output for audio, students often forget the **low-pass filter** that removes staircase artifacts. This is a **must-mention** for full marks.
> 7. **Confusing PWM frequency and audio frequency:** These are two **different** concepts. PWM frequency is the carrier; audio frequency is the modulating signal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **DAC Definition:** Converts digital codes to proportional analog voltages.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $V_{out} = (D / 2^n) \cdot V_{ref}$
- [x] **Step Size (LSB):** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$
- [x] **Resolution:** Number of bits ($n$); higher = smoother output.
- [x] **R-2R Ladder:** Uses only $R$ and $2R$ resistors; most common IC architecture.
- [x] **PWM-as-DAC:** Uses a low-pass filter to extract the average DC value.

### Sine Wave Generation
- [x] **Lookup Table (LUT):** Pre-computed digital sine samples stored in memory.
- [x] **DC Offset:** $2^{n-1}$ added to fit sine wave into unipolar DAC range.
- [x] **Sine Formula:** $D(k) = 2^{n-1} + (2^{n-1} - 1) \cdot \sin(2\pi k / N)$
- [x] **Quadrant Symmetry:** Use N/4 values to save **75% memory**.
- [x] **Output Frequency:** $f_{out} = f_{update} / N$ (where $N$ = samples per cycle).

### Audio Signal Generation
- [x] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (mandatory for perfect reconstruction).
- [x] **CD Audio Standard:** 16-bit, 44.1 kHz sampling.
- [x] **SNR Formula:** $\text{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB (theoretical maximum).
- [x] **Bit Depth Trade-off:** More bits = better dynamic range, more memory, slower conversion.

### LPC2148-Specific (KTU Favourite ⭐)
- [x] DAC is **10-bit**, with output range **0 V to 3.3 V**.
- [x] **Resolution** = 3.22 mV per LSB step.
- [x] DAC register is at address `0xE006C000`, with output on pin **AOUT** (P0.26).
- [x] The DAC value must be left-shifted by **6 bits** before writing (bits 6-15 are valid).
- [x] **Settling time:** typically 1 µs; **maximum update rate:** 1 MHz.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Shortcuts
- [x] For a 10-bit DAC: $2^{10} = 1024$. Always use this constant in calculations.
- [x] "Find $V_{out}$ for $D = X$" → Use $V_{out} = (X / 1024) \cdot 3.3$ V.
- [x] "Find $D$ for $V_{out} = Y$" → Use $D = (Y / 3.3) \cdot 1024$, then round to nearest integer.
- [x] "Find output frequency" → $f_{out} = f_{timer} / N_{samples}$.

### Exam Golden Rules
- [x] **Always draw labeled diagrams** for 7-mark questions (block diagrams preferred).
- [x] **Mention units** (V, Hz, ms, etc.) in all numerical answers.
- [x] **State assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming ideal DAC with no offset error").
- [x] **Show step-by-step calculation** — never skip algebra.
- [x] **Add a brief conclusion** to long-answer questions (1-2 lines summarizing the result).

---

**End of Notes — All the Best for Your KTU 2024 Exam! 🎓**
