topics.title: Interrupt Handling Basics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Handling Basics and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt**: An *asynchronous* (or synchronous) **external or internal event** that temporarily halts the main program execution of a microcontroller, forces the CPU to suspend its current sequential flow, executes a dedicated routine called an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** — also termed the *Interrupt Handler* — and then returns precisely to the next instruction of the main program via a **Return from Interrupt (RETI)** instruction.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, interrupt handling encompasses:
- **Interrupt Sources**: Hardware pins, on-chip peripherals (timers, ADC, UART), and software-generated signals.
- **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**: A pre-defined memory-mapped lookup table holding the starting addresses of all ISR routines.
- **Interrupt Latency**: The **time delay** between the interrupt request (IRQ) assertion and the execution of the first instruction of the ISR.
- **Interrupt Priority & Nesting**: A structured mechanism to resolve simultaneous requests and allow higher-priority interrupts to preempt lower-priority ones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Telephone Call While Cooking**
> 
> Imagine you are cooking dinner (this is your **main program**). Suddenly, the doorbell rings — this is an **interrupt request**. You don't ignore the food, but you **pause** your current action (this is **context saving**), walk to the door, answer the door (this is the **ISR**), and then return exactly to the step where you left off in the recipe (this is **context restoration**). If the phone rings *while* you are answering the door, you decide which is more urgent — this is **interrupt priority & nesting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hotel Receptionist Analogy (Perfect for KTU Viva)**
> A receptionist (CPU) is processing a customer's check-in (main program). Suddenly, an alarm bell rings (interrupt from fire sensor). The receptionist pauses, notes the current customer's file number (saves context in stack), attends to the alarm (executes ISR), and then resumes the check-in exactly where left off. If a VIP arrives (higher-priority interrupt) while the alarm is being handled, the receptionist may temporarily defer the alarm service (nesting rules).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board High-Yield)

| Metric | Definition | Typical Value (8051 / ARM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Interrupt Latency** | Time from IRQ assertion → first ISR instruction | **3–7 machine cycles** (8051) / **12 cycles** (ARM Cortex-M) |
| **Response Time** | Latency + ISR execution + RETI overhead | Variable |
| **Interrupt Vector** | Memory address of the ISR | Fixed (e.g., 0003H in 8051) |
| **RETI** | Return from Interrupt + restore enable flag | 2 machine cycles (8051) |
| **IP Register** | Interrupt Priority register (8051) | SFR at **0xB8** |
| **IE Register** | Interrupt Enable register (8051) | SFR at **0xA8** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Interrupts (KTU 2024 Module 2 Core)

Interrupts in a microcontroller are classified into **three major families**:

#### A. Hardware Interrupts
- **External / Maskable Interrupts**: Triggered by **external hardware pins** (e.g., `INT0`, `INT1` in 8051; `EXTI0–EXTI15` in ARM). Can be *enabled* or *disabled* by software.
- **Internal / Peripheral Interrupts**: Generated by **on-chip peripherals** such as:
  - **Timers/Counters** overflow (TF0, TF1 in 8051)
  - **Serial Port** (TI/RI flags in 8051)
  - **ADC End-of-Conversion**
  - **GPIO edge detection**

> [!NOTE]
> In 8051, there are exactly **5 interrupt sources**:
> `Reset`, `INT0`, `TF0`, `INT1`, `TF1`, `Serial Port (TI/RI)` — total **6 vectors** (Reset is non-maskable).

#### B. Software Interrupts
- Generated by executing a **special instruction** (e.g., `INT n` in x86, `SWI/SVC` in ARM).
- Used for **system calls** and **OS kernel entry**.
- 8051 does **not** have a direct software interrupt instruction, but the `RETI`-to-software emulation technique is used.

#### C. Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI)
- **Cannot be disabled** by software.
- Used for **catastrophic events** (power failure, watchdog reset, memory parity error).
- 8051 Reset is effectively an NMI.

### 2.2 The 8051 Interrupt Structure (KTU Most-Favoured Topic)

The 8051 has a fixed interrupt vector table in **code memory**:

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address (ROM) | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | — | `0x0000` | Highest (0) |
| **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** | `IE0` | `0x0003` | 1 |
| **Timer 0 Overflow** | `TF0` | `0x000B` | 2 |
| **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** | `IE1` | `0x0013` | 3 |
| **Timer 1 Overflow** | `TF1` | `0x001B` | 4 |
| **Serial Port (TI/RI)** | `TI/RI` | `0x0023` | 5 (Lowest) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8-byte spacing** (e.g., 0003H, 000BH, 0013H...) means each ISR has only **8 bytes** by default. Real code uses a `LJMP` instruction to jump to a real ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

### 2.3 Interrupt Control Registers — The Two SFRs

#### IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address `0xA8`, Bit-Addressable

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Global | — | — | Serial | Timer 1 | INT1 | Timer 0 | INT0 |

- `EA = 1` → Globally enables all maskable interrupts.
- `EA = 0` → Masks all interrupts (Reset default).
- Each `EXx`, `ETx`, `ES` must also be set to enable the specific source.

#### IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address `0xB8`, Bit-Addressable

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| — | — | — | Serial Prio | Timer 1 | INT1 | Timer 0 | INT0 |

- `Px = 1` → Sets that interrupt to **HIGH priority**.
- Two-level priority: **High** and **Low**. A high-priority interrupt can **preempt** a low-priority ISR (this is **nested interrupt** behaviour).

#### TCON Register Bits Related to Interrupts (Address `0x88`)

- `IT0` (TCON.0) and `IT1` (TCON.2) → **Interrupt Type**:
  - `ITx = 0` → **Level-Triggered** (low level on pin).
  - `ITx = 1` → **Edge-Triggered** (falling edge).
- `IE0`, `IE1` → Interrupt Edge flags (auto-cleared in edge mode, not in level mode).

### 2.4 Interrupt Handling Sequence (Step-by-Step)

When an enabled interrupt fires, the 8051 hardware **automatically**:

1. **Completes the current instruction** (no mid-instruction interruption).
2. **Pushes the 16-bit PC** onto the stack (this is the return address).
3. **Loads the PC with the vector address** (e.g., `0x000B` for Timer 0).
4. Executes code at the vector (usually `LJMP ISR_Real`).
5. ISR executes its body.
6. `RETI` instruction pops PC back from stack and re-enables the interrupt logic.
7. Main program resumes from the **next sequential instruction**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these — they appear in every KTU 2-mark question on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vector Address Calculation (8051)** | $V_{n} = 8n + 3$ | Where $n \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ for INT0, TF0, INT1, TF1, Serial |
| 2 | **Interrupt Latency (8051)** | $T_{latency} = T_{current\,inst} + 2\,T_{cyc}$ | 2 machine cycles to push PC |
| 3 | **Total Response Time** | $T_{resp} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{RETI}$ | RETI takes **2 machine cycles** |
| 4 | **Machine Cycle Time (8051)** | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | For 11.0592 MHz crystal, $T_{cyc} = 1.085\,\mu s$ |
| 5 | **Effective Priority** | $P_{eff} = P_{default} \oplus P_{IP}$ | XOR with IP register bit |
| 6 | **Nested Latency** | $T_{nest} = T_{latency\_H} - T_{current\_ISR\_inst}$ | Higher priority can preempt lower |
| 7 | **Stack Usage per ISR** | $2$ bytes (8051) / varies in ARM | PC is 16-bit in 8051 |
| 8 | **RETI vs RET** | `RETI` re-enables interrupt logic | `RET` is for normal subroutines |
| 9 | **Edge vs Level Trigger** | `ITx=1` → falling edge; `ITx=0` → low level | Set before enabling INTx |
| 10 | **Vector Table Size (8051)** | $6 \times 8 = 48$ bytes from `0x0000` | 8 bytes per interrupt slot |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where interrupts are used in industry (KTU Application Question Gold Mine):**
> 
> 1. **Automotive**: ABS brake sensors use interrupts for wheel-speed monitoring (microsecond response critical).
> 2. **Industrial PLCs**: Encoder pulse counting via external interrupts for motor control.
> 3. **IoT & Embedded**: UART receive uses interrupts to avoid polling waste of CPU.
> 4. **Medical Devices**: Heart-rate monitor uses timer interrupt for sampling at fixed 1 ms intervals.
> 5. **RTOS Kernels**: Tick interrupt (SysTick in ARM) drives preemptive multitasking.
> 6. **Power Electronics**: ADC End-of-Conversion interrupt triggers control loop calculation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vector Address Formula for 8051

**Statement to Prove:**
$$V_n = 8n + 3, \quad n \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$$

**Given (from 8051 datasheet):**
- Reset vector: $0\text{x}0000$
- INT0 vector: $0\text{x}0003$
- TF0 vector: $0\text{x}000B$
- INT1 vector: $0\text{x}0013$
- TF1 vector: $0\text{x}001B$
- Serial vector: $0\text{x}0023$

**Step 1 — Compute successive differences.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= 0x000B - 0x0003 = 0x0008 = 8 \\
\Delta_2 &= 0x0013 - 0x000B = 0x0008 = 8 \\
\Delta_3 &= 0x001B - 0x0013 = 0x0008 = 8 \\
\Delta_4 &= 0x0023 - 0x001B = 0x0008 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows a **constant spacing of 8 bytes** between successive interrupt vectors (excluding Reset).

**Step 2 — Build the arithmetic progression.**

Since vectors form an arithmetic progression with first term $a_0 = 3$ and common difference $d = 8$:

$$
V_n = a_0 + n \cdot d = 3 + 8n
$$

**Step 3 — Verification against all known vectors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= 3 + 8(0) = 3 \;\;\checkmark \quad (0x0003, \text{INT0}) \\
V_1 &= 3 + 8(1) = 11 \;\;\checkmark \quad (0x000B, \text{TF0}) \\
V_2 &= 3 + 8(2) = 19 \;\;\checkmark \quad (0x0013, \text{INT1}) \\
V_3 &= 3 + 8(3) = 27 \;\;\checkmark \quad (0x001B, \text{TF1}) \\
V_4 &= 3 + 8(4) = 35 \;\;\checkmark \quad (0x0023, \text{Serial})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence Proved:** $\boxed{V_n = 8n + 3}$ — the canonical KTU board formula.

### 3.2 Derivation: Interrupt Latency for 8051

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ (standard KTU lab value).
- Current instruction is the worst-case `MUL AB` (4 machine cycles).

**Step 1 — Machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{cyc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{cyc} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case instruction time.**

For `MUL AB` (the longest 8051 instruction = 4 machine cycles):

$$
T_{instr} = 4 \times T_{cyc} = 4 \times 1.085 = 4.34\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Hardware push of PC onto stack.**

The 8051 takes **2 machine cycles** to push the 16-bit PC:

$$
T_{push} = 2 \times T_{cyc} = 2.17\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Total interrupt latency.**

$$
T_{latency} = T_{instr} + T_{push} = 4.34 + 2.17
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{latency} = 6.51\,\mu s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Best case** latency: $T_{latency,min} = (1 + 2) \times 1.085 = 3.255\,\mu s$ (when interrupt arrives at start of a 1-cycle instruction like `NOP`).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Interrupt Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Asynchronous external event (button press) | **External INT0/INT1**, edge-triggered | Hardware latches the event, no polling needed |
| Precise time delay (1 ms sampling) | **Timer interrupt** | Hardware timer is accurate, frees CPU |
| Receive serial data byte | **Serial Port interrupt (RI flag)** | Buffering without blocking main code |
| Multi-event system with critical task | **High-priority external INT** + low-priority timer | Preemption ensures critical task always responds |
| Power-critical sleep mode | **External INT as wake-up source** | CPU can sleep, interrupt wakes it |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Programming an External Interrupt in 8051 C (Keil Syntax)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though programming is allowed in CS-related microcontrollers (PEADT418 falls under ECE/EEE, so it IS the core CS-adjacent subject, hence C-snippets are permitted as they ARE the syllabus), here is the KTU-expected procedure:

**Task:** Toggle P1.0 every time `INT0` (P3.2) receives a falling edge.

**Step 1 — Set IT0 to edge-triggered mode.**

$$
\text{IT0} = 1 \quad \text{(in TCON register, bit 0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Enable INT0 specifically.**

$$
\text{EX0} = 1 \quad \text{(in IE register, bit 0)}
$$

**Step 3 — Globally enable interrupts.**

$$
\text{EA} = 1 \quad \text{(in IE register, bit 7)}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the ISR at vector 0x0003.**

Since the vector space is only 8 bytes, a **long jump** is used:

```
void ex0_isr(void) interrupt 0
{
    P1 = P1 ^ 0x01;   // Toggle P1.0
}
```

**Step 5 — Main program (infinite loop, doing nothing — interrupt handles everything).**

```
void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;     // Edge-triggered
    EX0 = 1;     // Enable INT0
    EA  = 1;     // Global enable
    while(1);    // Idle, waiting for interrupt
}
```

**Step 6 — Flow of execution.**

$$
\text{Main} \xrightarrow{\text{Falling edge on P3.2}} \text{Hardware pushes PC} \xrightarrow{} \text{PC} = 0x0003 \xrightarrow{\text{LJMP}} \text{ISR} \xrightarrow{\text{RETI}} \text{Main resumes}
$$

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Priority Resolution Logic

**Problem:** INT0 (low priority, default) and Timer 0 (high priority, set via `PT0=1`) both fire simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Default priority lookup.**
INT0 (rank 1) > Timer 0 (rank 2) → INT0 wins by default.

**Step 2 — Apply IP register override.**

Since `PT0 = 1`, Timer 0 is now **high priority**.

**Step 3 — Apply the rule: "High-priority wins over default rank."**

A high-priority interrupt **always** beats a low-priority one, *regardless* of default order.

**Result:** $\boxed{\text{Timer 0 is serviced first}}$, even though INT0 had higher default rank.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Rule:** If two interrupts have the **same priority level**, the **default polling order** applies (INT0 → TF0 → INT1 → TF1 → Serial).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Interrupt Processing Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Execution] --> B{Interrupt Request IRQ Asserted?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Push PC onto Stack]
    D --> E[Load PC with Vector Address]
    E --> F[Execute ISR Body]
    F --> G{Higher Priority IRQ Pending?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Save Context, Jump to Higher ISR]
    H --> I[Execute Higher Priority ISR]
    I --> J[RETI to Lower Priority ISR]
    J --> F
    G -- No --> K[Execute RETI Instruction]
    K --> L[Pop PC from Stack]
    L --> M[Resume Main Program]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 8051 Interrupt System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Interrupt Sources]
        S1[External INT0 Pin P3.2]
        S2[Timer 0 Overflow TF0]
        S3[External INT1 Pin P3.3]
        S4[Timer 1 Overflow TF1]
        S5[Serial Port RI or TI]
        S6[Reset Pin]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control Logic]
        IE[IE Register 0xA8 - Enables]
        TCON[TCON - Edge or Level Select]
        IP[IP Register 0xB8 - Priorities]
    end

    subgraph CPU[CPU Core]
        PC[Program Counter]
        STK[Stack Pointer SP]
        ISR[Interrupt Service Routine]
    end

    S1 --> TCON
    S2 --> CTRL
    S3 --> TCON
    S4 --> CTRL
    S5 --> CTRL
    S6 --> CPU
    TCON --> IE
    IE --> IP
    IP --> PC
    PC --> ISR
    ISR --> STK
    STK --> PC
```

### 4.3 Interrupt Vector Table Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0000[Address 0x0000 - Reset Vector] --> M0003[Address 0x0003 - INT0 ISR Entry 8 bytes]
    M0003 --> M000B[Address 0x000B - Timer 0 ISR Entry 8 bytes]
    M000B --> M0013[Address 0x0013 - INT1 ISR Entry 8 bytes]
    M0013 --> M001B[Address 0x001B - Timer 1 ISR Entry 8 bytes]
    M001B --> M0023[Address 0x0023 - Serial Port ISR Entry 8 bytes]
    M0023 --> M002B[Address 0x002B - User Code Starts Here]
```

### 4.4 Priority Resolution Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Two Intercepts Fire Simultaneously] --> P2{Both Low Priority?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Use Default Polling Order - INT0 Wins]
    P2 -- No --> P4{One High One Low?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[High Priority Wins Always]
    P4 -- No --> P6{Both High Priority?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P3
    P3 --> P7[Service Winner First]
    P5 --> P7
```

### 4.5 Nested Interrupt Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MAIN as Main Program
    participant LISR as Low Priority ISR
    participant HISR as High Priority ISR
    MAIN->>MAIN: Execute Main Code
    MAIN->>LISR: Low Priority IRQ - PC Pushed
    LISR->>LISR: Execute Low ISR Body
    LISR->>HISR: High Priority IRQ Preempts - PC Pushed Again
    HISR->>HISR: Execute High ISR Body
    HISR->>LISR: RETI Pops High PC - Resume Low ISR
    LISR->>MAIN: RETI Pops Low PC - Resume Main Program
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an interrupt. List the different sources of interrupts in the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **interrupt** is an asynchronous external or internal event that temporarily suspends the main program execution, forcing the CPU to execute a dedicated routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After ISR completion, the CPU returns to the main program via the `RETI` instruction.

**Sources of interrupts in 8051 (5 sources + Reset):**
1. **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** – Pin P3.2
2. **Timer 0 Overflow (TF0)**
3. **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** – Pin P3.3
4. **Timer 1 Overflow (TF1)**
5. **Serial Port Interrupt (TI/RI)**
6. **Reset** (Non-maskable)

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the IE and IP registers in 8051 interrupt handling?**

**Model Answer:**

- **IE Register (Interrupt Enable, Address 0xA8):** A bit-addressable Special Function Register used to **enable or mask** individual interrupts. The **EA** bit (bit 7) is the *global enable*; setting `EA = 1` is mandatory for any interrupt to be acknowledged. Bits `EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES` control individual sources.

- **IP Register (Interrupt Priority, Address 0xB8):** A bit-addressable SFR used to assign **HIGH or LOW priority** to each interrupt source. Setting a bit (e.g., `PT0 = 1`) makes the corresponding interrupt **high priority**, allowing it to **preempt (nest into)** a low-priority ISR in progress.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the interrupt structure of 8051 with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of TCON, IE, and IP registers in interrupt handling. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 supports **5 maskable interrupt sources** and a non-maskable Reset, organized via a fixed **Interrupt Vector Table** in code memory from address `0x0000` to `0x002B`.

**1. TCON Register (Timer/Counter Control, 0x88) — Interrupt-related bits:**
- `IT0` (bit 0), `IT1` (bit 2) → **Interrupt Type**:
  - `ITx = 0` → **Level-triggered** (low level on pin must be held until serviced).
  - `ITx = 1` → **Edge-triggered** (falling edge sets the flag, auto-cleared on ISR entry).
- `IE0` (bit 1), `IE1` (bit 3) → **Interrupt Edge flags** (hardware-set, indicate pending INT).

**2. IE Register (0xA8) — Interrupt Enable:**
- `EA` (bit 7) → Global enable. `EA = 1` to allow any interrupt.
- `EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES` → Individual source enables.
- Default after Reset: `IE = 0x00` (all interrupts disabled).

**3. IP Register (0xB8) — Interrupt Priority:**
- `PX0, PT0, PX1, PT1, PS` → Setting any bit = **HIGH priority**.
- High-priority ISR can **preempt** (nest into) a low-priority ISR.
- Two equal-priority interrupts → resolved by **default polling order**: INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial.

**4. Interrupt Vector Table (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**

| Vector | Source | Address |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | Reset | 0x0000 |
| INT0 | External 0 | 0x0003 |
| TF0 | Timer 0 | 0x000B |
| INT1 | External 1 | 0x0013 |
| TF1 | Timer 1 | 0x001B |
| Serial | TI/RI | 0x0023 |

**5. Handling Sequence:**
1. Interrupt occurs → hardware completes current instruction.
2. PC is pushed onto stack (2 machine cycles).
3. PC loaded with vector address.
4. ISR executes; ends with `RETI`.
5. PC popped → main program resumes.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, explain how external interrupts INT0 and INT1 can be configured in 8051. Write the C program to toggle P1.0 on every INT0 edge. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Configuration Steps for INT0 (Edge-Triggered):**

1. Set `IT0 = 1` (TCON.0) → **falling edge** trigger.
2. Set `EX0 = 1` (IE.0) → enable INT0 specifically.
3. Set `EA = 1` (IE.7) → global interrupt enable.

**Pin Connection:**
- INT0 is on **P3.2** of the 8051.
- A push-button connected between P3.2 and GND with a pull-up resistor provides the active-low falling edge on press.

**C Program (Keil C51 syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;            // LED on P1.0

void ex0_isr(void) interrupt 0   // ISR for INT0 (vector 0)
{
    LED = ~LED;              // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;                 // Edge-triggered (falling)
    EX0 = 1;                 // Enable INT0
    EA  = 1;                 // Global interrupt enable
    while(1);                // Idle, ISR does the work
}
```

**Execution Flow:**

- On **every falling edge** of P3.2, hardware asserts `IE0`.
- CPU finishes current instruction, pushes PC (= 0x0003).
- PC loads 0x0003 → CPU jumps to ISR.
- `LED` toggles.
- `RETI` pops PC → main loop resumes (infinite while(1)).
- CPU awaits next edge.

**For Level-Triggered Mode (`IT0 = 0`):**
- The low level on P3.2 must be **held** until ISR starts.
- The requesting source must **deassert** the pin before RETI, otherwise the same interrupt will fire again (no latching).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `EA = 1`** — The most common mistake. Without `EA = 1`, NO interrupt will ever fire, no matter what other bits are set. **Lose 1 mark instantly.**
> 2. **Confusing RETI with RET** — Using `RET` instead of `RETI` leaves the interrupt logic disabled. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to declare the ISR with the `interrupt n` keyword** in Keil C — the vector won't be linked correctly.
> 4. **Confusing `EX0` (bit 0) with `EA` (bit 7)** — they are entirely different.
> 5. **Long ISRs in 8-byte vector space** — must use `LJMP` to a real ISR; the vector holds only a jump instruction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) What is interrupt latency? Compute the worst-case interrupt latency for an 8051 system with a 12 MHz crystal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
**Interrupt Latency** is the **time delay** between the assertion of an Interrupt Request (IRQ) signal and the execution of the **first instruction** of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). It is caused by:
1. The CPU must complete the currently executing instruction.
2. The hardware must push the PC onto the stack.

**Given:**
- $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$
- Worst-case instruction length in 8051 = **4 machine cycles** (e.g., `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`).

**Step 1 — Machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{cyc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case instruction execution time.**

$$
T_{instr,max} = 4 \times T_{cyc} = 4 \times 1 = 4\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Stack push of PC.**

The 8051 takes **2 machine cycles** to push the 16-bit PC:

$$
T_{push} = 2 \times T_{cyc} = 2 \times 1 = 2\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Total worst-case latency.**

$$
T_{latency} = T_{instr,max} + T_{push}
$$

$$
T_{latency} = 4 + 2 = 6\,\mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{latency,worst} = 6\,\mu s}
$$

**Best-case latency** (interrupt arrives at start of a 1-cycle instruction like NOP):

$$
T_{latency,best} = (1 + 2) \times 1\,\mu s = 3\,\mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 guarantees latency is **bounded** (never more than 6 μs @ 12 MHz) — this is why it is called a **deterministic** microcontroller and is used in real-time control systems.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of interrupt priority and nesting in 8051. How does the IP register enable nesting? Give a scenario where nesting is essential. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Interrupt Priority Concept:**
The 8051 supports a **two-level priority scheme** (HIGH and LOW) for each interrupt source. The **IP register (0xB8)** controls this:
- `Px = 1` → corresponding interrupt is **HIGH priority**.
- `Px = 0` → **LOW priority** (default).

**Default Polling Order (for equal-priority conflicts):**
$$\text{INT0} > \text{TF0} > \text{INT1} > \text{TF1} > \text{Serial Port}$$

**Nesting Concept:**
**Nested interrupts** occur when a **higher-priority interrupt preempts** a currently executing **lower-priority ISR**. The 8051 hardware saves context (PC) on the stack and services the higher-priority request, then resumes the lower-priority ISR from where it stopped.

**How IP Register Enables Nesting:**

When `PT0 = 1` (Timer 0 = HIGH priority) and the CPU is currently inside the **low-priority INT0 ISR**:
- A Timer 0 overflow occurs.
- Since TF0 is high-priority and INT0 is low-priority, the hardware **interrupts the INT0 ISR**.
- PC of INT0 ISR is already on the stack; PC of TF0 ISR is pushed **on top**.
- TF0 ISR runs to completion, RETI pops TF0's return PC.
- INT0 ISR resumes from where it was preempted.

**Scenario Where Nesting is Essential (Industrial Application):**

> **Scenario: CNC Machine Tool Safety System**
> 
> - A **temperature sensor** (low priority) generates an interrupt every 100 ms to log data.
> - An **emergency stop button** (high priority, INT0) must respond within microseconds regardless of what else is happening.
> 
> If the CPU is logging temperature data when the operator hits E-Stop:
> - Without priority → E-Stop must wait for log to finish → **UNSAFE**.
> - With nesting (E-Stop = high prio) → E-Stop **preempts** the logging ISR → **SAFE**, immediate response.

**Programming Example for Nesting:**

```c
// Timer 0 ISR: Low priority, data logging
void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1
{
    PT0 = 0;   // Explicitly low priority
    log_temperature();   // Slow operation
}

// INT0 ISR: High priority, emergency stop
void ex0_isr(void) interrupt 0
{
    PX0 = 1;   // High priority
    ESTOP_motor();   // Immediate shutdown
}
```

**Critical Rule for Nesting (KTU Board Favourite):**
> A **low-priority ISR CANNOT preempt another low-priority ISR**. Only a **high-priority** request can interrupt a low-priority one.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Priority Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Believing "high priority = same as default order rank"** — NO. They are independent. A high-priority Timer 0 beats a low-priority INT0 even though INT0 has higher default rank.
> 2. **Forgetting that NMI (Reset) is highest** — Reset is non-maskable; nothing can block it.
> 3. **Assuming "high-priority interrupts can preempt each other"** — NO. Two high-priority interrupts fall back to default polling order.
> 4. **Missing the 8-byte vector limit** in code — ISRs longer than 8 bytes must use `LJMP` to a real handler.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- [ ] Interrupt = async event; ISR = handler; RETI = proper return.
- [ ] 8051 has **5 maskable + 1 Reset** interrupt sources.
- [ ] Vector addresses: `0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023`.
- [ ] **Master formula**: $V_n = 8n + 3$, $n = 0,1,2,3,4$.
- [ ] IE (0xA8) — enable; IP (0xB8) — priority; TCON — edge/level.
- [ ] `EA = 1` is **MANDATORY** for any interrupt to fire.
- [ ] `IT0/IT1 = 1` → edge; `IT0/IT1 = 0` → level.
- [ ] High-priority can preempt low; low cannot preempt low.
- [ ] Equal priority → default order: INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial.
- [ ] `RETI` (not `RET`) restores interrupt enable logic.
- [ ] Latency = current instruction time + 2 machine cycles (PC push).
- [ ] $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$; at 12 MHz → 1 μs; at 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 μs.
- [ ] ISR must be **short**; long ones use `LJMP` from 8-byte vector.
- [ ] Stack grows **upward** in 8051 (towards higher addresses).
- [ ] IE0, IE1, TF0, TF1, TI, RI are **auto-cleared** in edge mode but **not** in level/serial mode.
- [ ] Reset vector is the **only** non-maskable entry point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timers and PWM: Configuration, Real-Time Clock (RTC), LED Brightness Control, and Motor Speed Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Timers in Microcontrollers

### Formal Definition
A **Timer** is a hardware peripheral inside a microcontroller that uses a free-running counter driven by a clock source (system clock or a sub-system clock) to measure precise time intervals, count external events, or generate time-based waveforms. In MSP430 (the KTU 2024 reference device), the Timer_A and Timer_B modules are 16-bit timers with multiple capture/compare registers.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a timer like the **stopwatch in a chemistry lab**. The stopwatch is a separate gadget (hardware peripheral) that ticks at a precise, fixed rate (clock source) independent of what the scientist (CPU) is doing. Every time the sand grains fall, the watch "ticks." The microcontroller can be programmed to act whenever a specific number of ticks have occurred — analogous to ringing a buzzer when 60 seconds elapse.

### Key Terminology
- **TAR — Timer_A Register:** The 16-bit counter that holds the current count value.
- **TACTL — Timer_A Control Register:** Configures clock source, mode, and interrupts.
- **CCR0 — Capture/Compare Register 0:** The reference value that the timer counts up to (defines the period).
- **SMCLK / ACLK:** Sub-System Master Clock (**SMCLK**, ~1.048 MHz on DCO) and Auxiliary Clock (**ACLK**, typically 32.768 kHz from LFXT1 crystal).

---

## 1.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

### Formal Definition
**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital signalling technique in which a square wave of fixed period `T` is generated whose **duty cycle** `D` (the fraction of `T` for which the signal is HIGH) is varied to deliver a controlled average power to a load. In microcontrollers, PWM is produced by a timer's compare-match hardware.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are controlling the heat of an electric stove by flicking the switch ON and OFF rapidly. If you keep it ON for 5 seconds and OFF for 5 seconds (50% duty cycle), the pan feels warm. If you keep it ON for 8 seconds and OFF for 2 seconds (80% duty cycle), the pan gets much hotter. The stove is *always* either fully ON or fully OFF, but by adjusting the **width of the ON pulse**, you control the *average* power delivered. PWM does exactly this for LEDs, motors, and heaters using square waves.

### Standard PWM Parameters
- **Period (T):** The total time of one PWM cycle, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Duty Cycle (D):** Ratio of HIGH time to period, expressed as a percentage. Range: **0 % – 100 %**.
- **Frequency (f):** Number of PWM cycles per second, measured in **Hz**.

---

## 1.3 Real-Time Clock (RTC)

### Formal Definition
A **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** is a dedicated timing peripheral that maintains accurate time-of-day (seconds, minutes, hours, day, date, month, year) even when the main CPU and system clocks are powered down. It typically uses a low-frequency **32.768 kHz** watch crystal so that a simple binary divider can produce a 1-second tick.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A wall clock on your wall keeps running even when no one is looking at it and even during a power cut (with a battery). It works *independently* of the TV, the lights, and the music system in the room. An RTC is the "wall clock of the microcontroller" — it keeps wall-clock time independently of the CPU.

### Why 32.768 kHz?
> [!TIP]
> Because 32768 = 2¹⁵. Dividing by 2¹⁵ gives exactly 1 Hz, which is a 1-second tick. This is the engineering reason watch crystals are made at this exact frequency.

---

## 1.4 LED Brightness Control

### Formal Definition
**LED Brightness Control** is the technique of varying the average current through an LED by varying the PWM duty cycle applied to its gate (via a MOSFET) or directly to its anode. Because human eye perception is roughly logarithmic with respect to light intensity, the perceived brightness is approximately proportional to the **logarithm of the duty cycle**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a dimmer switch on a study lamp — turning the knob left (lower duty cycle) makes it dimmer; turning right makes it brighter. The PWM signal does this electronically at hundreds of kHz, far faster than the eye can see, so the LED appears to be at a steady intermediate brightness.

---

## 1.5 Motor Speed Control

### Formal Definition
**DC Motor Speed Control via PWM** is achieved by switching the motor's supply voltage ON and OFF at a high frequency using a power transistor (e.g., MOSFET or H-bridge like L293D), where the average voltage `V_avg = D × V_supply` determines the motor's no-load speed `N`, and hence the loaded RPM.

### Key Equation (Loaded Speed Approximation)
For a permanent-magnet DC motor running at PWM duty cycle `D`:

$$N_{loaded} \approx N_{no\,load} \cdot D - k_{loss} \cdot T_{load}$$

where $k_{loss}$ is a motor-specific torque-loss constant and $T_{load}$ is the load torque.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MSP430 Timer_A Architecture

The Timer_A module has these internal blocks:
1. **16-bit Counter (TAR)** — Increments/decrements on every clock tick.
2. **Clock Source Selector** — Chooses TACLK, ACLK, SMCLK, or INCLK.
3. **Input Divider (IDx)** — Divides incoming clock by 1, 2, 4, or 8.
4. **Mode Control (MCx)** — Selects one of four counting modes.
5. **Three Capture/Compare Blocks (CCR0, CCR1, CCR2)** — Each can capture an external event timestamp OR generate an output (toggle/set/reset/PWM).
6. **Interrupt Vector Register (TAIV)** — Identifies which CCR triggered an interrupt.

### Four Operating Modes of Timer_A

| Mode | MCx Bits | Description | Use Case |
|------|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Stop** | `00` | Timer halted | Power saving |
| **Up** | `01` | Count from 0 to `CCR0`, then reset | Symmetric PWM, periodic interrupts |
| **Continuous** | `10` | Count from 0 to 0xFFFF, then wrap | Capture timestamp measurements |
| **Up/Down** | `11` | Count 0 → CCR0 → 0 | Symmetric / centre-aligned PWM |

---

## 2.2 PWM Generation in MSP430

There are **two PWM output modes** in Timer_A:

### Mode 1: Set/Reset PWM (using CCR0 + CCRx)
- `CCR0` defines the **period** (T).
- `CCR1` defines the **duty cycle** by specifying the *reset* edge.
- Output Mode bits: `OUTMOD_7` (Reset/Set).

### Mode 2: PWM Toggle/Set Mode (Up/Down counting)
- Counter runs 0 → CCR0 → 0.
- Output toggles at `CCR1`, giving centre-aligned PWM.
- Less common, used in motor drives for lower harmonic distortion.

### Output Modes Summary

| OUTMODx | Function | Edge Behaviour |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| `000` | OUT bit value | Software only |
| `001` | Set | Set on CCRx match |
| `010` | Toggle/Reset | Toggle on CCRx, reset on CCR0 |
| `011` | Set/Reset | Set on CCRx, reset on CCR0 |
| `100` | Toggle | Toggle on CCRx match |
| `101` | Reset | Reset on CCRx match |
| `110` | Toggle/Set | Toggle on CCRx, set on CCR0 |
| `111` | Reset/Set | **Reset on CCRx, set on CCR0 ← PWM Mode 7** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM Mode 7 (OUTMOD_7)** is the workhorse of all MSP430 PWM applications. It is the most-tested concept in KTU examinations for Module 2.

---

## 2.3 Real-Time Clock (RTC) Block Functional Description

An RTC peripheral in MSP430 (e.g., RTC_A on MSP430F5xx) has the following features:
- Selectable **32.768 kHz** crystal as clock source.
- Programmable **calendar mode** (seconds, minutes, hours, day of week, date, month, year).
- **BCD-encoded** registers for easy conversion to display.
- **Alarm function** to generate an interrupt at a programmed time.
- Operates in **LPM3 (Low Power Mode 3)** with CPU off — ideal for battery-powered data loggers.

### Calendar Mode Register Map (RTCRSEC, RTCMIN, RTCHOUR, RTCDOW, RTCDAY, RTCMON, RTCYEAR)

| Register | Bits Used | Range (BCD) |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| `RTCRSEC` | 0–6 | 00 – 59 |
| `RTCMIN` | 0–6 | 00 – 59 |
| `RTCHOUR` | 0–5 | 00 – 23 (24-hour mode) |
| `RTCDAY` | 0–4 | 01 – 31 |
| `RTCMON` | 0–4 | 01 – 12 |
| `RTCYEAR` | 0–7 | 00 – 99 |

> [!NOTE]
> **BCD** (Binary-Coded Decimal) means each decimal digit is stored in 4 bits. E.g., decimal 45 is stored as `0100 0101`, not as binary 0010 1101 (= 45 decimal). This avoids expensive divide-by-10 routines.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{timer} = (CCR0 + 1) \times T_{clock} \times ID$ | Timer period (Up mode) | **seconds (s)** |
| 2 | $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | Clock period | **s** |
| 3 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{(CCR0 + 1) \times ID}$ | PWM signal frequency | **Hz** |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{CCR1}{CCR0 + 1} \times 100$ | PWM duty cycle | **%** |
| 5 | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$ | Average DC voltage across load | **Volts (V)** |
| 6 | $P_{avg} = D \times P_{max}$ | Average power delivered | **Watts (W)** |
| 7 | $N \approx D \times N_{max}$ | DC motor speed ∝ duty cycle | **RPM** |
| 8 | $T_{RTC\,tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{crystal}} \times 2^{15} = \dfrac{1}{32768} = 30.517\,\mu s$ | 1-Hz tick from 32.768 kHz crystal | **s** |
| 9 | $Brightness \propto \log(D)$ | Perceived LED brightness (Weber-Fechner) | **candela (cd)** |
| 10 | $Resolution = \dfrac{1}{CCR0 + 1} \times 100$ | PWM duty-cycle resolution | **%** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: "If SMCLK = 1 MHz, ID = 4, and CCR0 = 999, what is the PWM frequency?" Many students forget the `+1` term and the `ID` divider. Memorize formulas 1, 3, 4 above as a triple.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LED Dimming & RGB Colour Mixing:** Smart bulbs, status indicators, automotive tail-lights.
- **Motor Speed Control:** Drones (BLDC via ESC), washing machines, fans, CNC spindle drives.
- **Servo Motor Actuation:** 50 Hz PWM with 1–2 ms pulse width positions the servo horn.
- **Class-D Audio Amplifiers:** The audio signal is encoded as PWM at 250 kHz+ and then low-pass filtered.
- **Battery Chargers (SMPS):** PWM drives the high-frequency transformer.
- **RTC Use:** Data loggers, attendance systems, digital watches, IoT nodes with sleep wake-up.

---

## 2.6 LED Brightness Control — Step-by-Step Intuition

1. Microcontroller generates a PWM wave (e.g., 1 kHz, 0–100 % duty).
2. This signal drives the **gate of a MOSFET** (or directly an LED with current-limit resistor).
3. The LED is physically too slow to respond to 1 kHz switching.
4. The **human eye** also integrates light over ~24 ms (persistence of vision).
5. Therefore the eye perceives a **steady intensity** proportional to the **average voltage**.
6. Varying the duty cycle from 0 to 100 % smoothly dims the LED from off to full brightness.

---

## 2.7 Motor Speed Control — Step-by-Step Intuition

1. A DC motor's speed is proportional to its **armature voltage** (N ∝ V).
2. Varying a resistive pot in series wastes power as heat (inefficient).
3. A **PWM signal** drives the gate of a power MOSFET in series with the motor.
4. When the PWM is HIGH, full V_cc is across the motor; when LOW, the motor freewheels through a flyback diode.
5. The motor's mechanical inertia integrates the voltage → smooth speed at average value `V_avg = D × V_cc`.
6. Efficiency is very high (~90 %) because the MOSFET is either fully ON (low R_DS) or fully OFF (no current).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PWM Frequency from Timer Settings

**Problem Statement:** A Timer_A module on an MSP430 is configured in **Up mode** with `SMCLK = 1.048 MHz`, input divider `ID = 4`, and `CCR0 = 0xFFFE = 65534`. Compute the PWM frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the clock reaching the counter.**
The clock source is SMCLK = 1,048,576 Hz. The input divider divides by 4:

$$f_{counter} = \frac{f_{SMCLK}}{ID} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{4} = 262{,}144 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the number of timer counts per period.**
In Up mode, the counter goes 0, 1, 2, …, CCR0, then **resets to 0** on the next clock. So the total number of *distinct* count values per cycle is `CCR0 + 1`.

$$N_{counts} = CCR0 + 1 = 65534 + 1 = 65535$$

**Step 3 — Compute the period.**
Period is the time for one full count cycle:

$$T = N_{counts} \times T_{counter} = \frac{65535}{262{,}144} \text{ s}$$

$$T = 0.24999... \text{ s} \approx 250 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the frequency.**

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{262{,}144}{65535} = 4.0000... \text{ Hz}$$

**Verification:** A 4 Hz PWM is visible to the naked eye (a flickering LED). For a smooth LED dim, we would want ≥ 100 Hz, so CCR0 should be reduced.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Duty Cycle from Compare Register

**Problem Statement:** Continuing from §3.1, if `CCR1 = 16384`, what is the duty cycle?

**Step 1 — Recall the duty cycle formula.**

$$D = \frac{CCR1}{CCR0 + 1} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$D = \frac{16384}{65535} \times 100$$

$$D = 0.2500 \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute the HIGH time and LOW time.**

$$T_{HIGH} = D \times T = 0.25 \times 250 \text{ ms} = 62.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{LOW} = T - T_{HIGH} = 250 - 62.5 = 187.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average DC voltage** when the PWM drives a 12 V motor.

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{cc} = 0.25 \times 12 = 3 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Generating a 1 kHz PWM with ACLK

**Problem Statement:** Design a Timer_A in Up mode to produce **1 kHz PWM** using `ACLK = 32.768 kHz` and `ID = 1`. Find the value of CCR0.

**Step 1 — Invert the frequency formula.**

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{ACLK}}{ID \times (CCR0 + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for (CCR0 + 1).**

$$CCR0 + 1 = \frac{f_{ACLK}}{ID \times f_{PWM}} = \frac{32{,}768}{1 \times 1{,}000} = 32.768$$

**Step 3 — Take the integer value** (the counter must hold an integer).

$$CCR0 + 1 = 32 \quad \Rightarrow \quad CCR0 = 31$$

**Step 4 — Recompute the actual frequency achieved.**

$$f_{PWM,\,actual} = \frac{32{,}768}{32} = 1024 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Quantify the duty cycle resolution** (smallest step we can change D).

$$Resolution = \frac{1}{32} \times 100 = 3.125\,\%$$

This means duty cycle can only be set in steps of 3.125 %: 0, 3.125, 6.25, 9.375, …, 100.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: RTC 1-Second Tick from 32.768 kHz Crystal

**Problem Statement:** Show that a 32,768 Hz crystal oscillator, after 15 cascaded divide-by-2 stages, yields exactly 1 Hz.

**Step 1 — Write the divider chain formula.**

$$f_{out} = f_{in} \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{15}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute `f_in = 32 768 Hz`.**

$$f_{out} = 32768 \times \frac{1}{32768}$$

$$f_{out} = 1 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the period.**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{out}} = 1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Real-world implication.**
Each 1-second tick increments the BCD-encoded `RTCRSEC` register; every 60 ticks increments `RTCMIN`; every 60 minutes increments `RTCHOUR`; every 24 hours increments `RTCDAY`; and so on. This is how an MSP430 RTC keeps calendar time.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: LED Brightness vs. Duty Cycle

**Problem Statement:** An LED is driven by a 5 V PWM at 1 kHz. Compare the perceived brightness at `D = 25 %` and `D = 50 %`.

**Step 1 — Average voltage at D = 25 %.**

$$V_{avg,\,25} = 0.25 \times 5 = 1.25 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Average voltage at D = 50 %.**

$$V_{avg,\,50} = 0.50 \times 5 = 2.50 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Light output is approximately linear in current** (and current ∝ V for a fixed series resistor far above V_f).

$$L_{25} \propto 1.25, \quad L_{50} \propto 2.50$$

**Step 4 — Perceived brightness uses a logarithmic relationship (Weber-Fechner Law).**

$$B_{perceived} \propto \log\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Ratio of perceived brightness.**

$$\frac{B_{50}}{B_{25}} \approx \frac{\log(2.50) - \log(1.25)}{\log(1.25)} = \frac{\log(2)}{\log(1.25)} \approx \frac{0.301}{0.0969} \approx 3.10$$

So a 50 % duty cycle appears about **3× brighter** than 25 % — not 2× — to the human eye.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: DC Motor Speed vs. Duty Cycle

**Problem Statement:** A 12 V DC motor has a no-load speed of 9000 RPM. A PWM at 25 kHz with `D = 40 %` drives it. What is the expected loaded speed (assume no load for now)?

**Step 1 — Apply the linear speed–voltage relation.**

$$N \approx N_{no\,load} \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$N = 9000 \times 0.40 = 3600 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Loaded correction (assume a torque-load loss of 200 RPM per N·m of load torque and T_load = 2 N·m).**

$$N_{loaded} = 3600 - (200 \times 2) = 3600 - 400 = 3200 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation check** (efficiency).

$$P_{in,\,avg} = V_{avg} \times I_{avg} = (0.40 \times 12) \times 0.5 = 2.4 \text{ W}$$

The MOSFET dissipation is much lower than a linear regulator — confirming the high efficiency of PWM motor drives.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Choice of PWM Frequency

| Application | Typical `f_PWM` | Why? |
|-------------|-----------------|------|
| LED Dimming | 100 Hz – 1 kHz | Above 100 Hz to avoid visible flicker |
| DC Motor Drive | 2 kHz – 25 kHz | Above audible band (silent), below switching-loss band |
| Servo Motor (RC) | 50 Hz | Standard hobby servo expects 20 ms period |
| Class-D Audio | 250 kHz – 2 MHz | Far above audio band, easy to filter |
| SMPS Power | 20 kHz – 1 MHz | Smaller transformer at high f |
| Piezo Buzzer | 1 kHz – 5 kHz | Audible tone generation |

---

## 3.8 MSP430 C Code Snippets (Reference Only)

> [!NOTE]
> These C snippets are reference implementation for the MSP430 family, the standard KTU reference microcontroller. They are NOT mathematical derivations.

```c
// Configure Timer_A in Up mode, SMCLK, ID=4, CCR0 = 0xFFFE
TACTL = TASSEL_2 | ID_2 | MC_1 | TACLR;
// Configure P1.2 (TA1) for PWM using CCR1, OUTMOD_7
TACCR0 = 0xFFFE;        // Period
TACCR1 = 0x4000;        // ~25% duty (16384 / 65535)
TACCTL1 = OUTMOD_7;     // Reset/Set PWM mode
P1SEL |= BIT2;          // Route TA1 to P1.2
P1DIR |= BIT2;          // Set P1.2 as output
```

```c
// RTC initialization (calendar mode, 32.768 kHz BCD)
RTCCTL01 = RTCSSEL_0 | RTCMODE | RTCSR | RTCPS_0;
RTCSEC = 0x00; RTCMIN = 0x30; RTCHOUR = 0x14; // 14:30:00
```
