# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Timer operating modes: "Up" mode and "Continuous" mode in MSP430 Timer_A. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Up Mode (MCx = 01):** The counter increments from 0 up to the value stored in `CCR0`, then automatically resets to 0 on the next clock tick. The period is fixed and deterministic, equal to `(CCR0 + 1) × T_clock`. Used for periodic interrupts and symmetric PWM.
- **Continuous Mode (MCx = 10):** The counter increments from 0 to `0xFFFF` (65535), then wraps back to 0. The period is fixed only at 65536 clock ticks. Used for free-running timestamps and capture operations.
- **Key Difference:** Up mode allows user-defined period via CCR0; Continuous mode has a fixed 16-bit period. Up mode supports PWM using CCR0; Continuous mode does not directly support PWM with CCR0 as the period reference.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of using a 32.768 kHz crystal in RTC applications? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Exact 1 Hz generation:** 32 768 = 2¹⁵, so a 15-stage binary divider yields exactly 1 Hz, ideal for a 1-second tick.
2. **Low power consumption:** Watch crystals draw only microamperes, suitable for battery-backed RTCs.
3. **High accuracy:** Tuning-fork crystals offer ±20 ppm accuracy, giving a maximum drift of about ±5 seconds per month.
4. **Standardization:** 32.768 kHz is the de-facto industry standard for low-frequency clocking in portable devices.

---

## Part B Questions (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Question:** An MSP430 Timer_A is configured in **Up mode** using `SMCLK = 1 MHz` with input divider `ID = 4`. The timer should generate a **PWM signal of 100 Hz** on pin P1.2 with a duty cycle of **40 %**.

#### (a) Calculate the values to be loaded into `CCR0` and `CCR1` registers. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the counter clock frequency.**

$$f_{counter} = \frac{f_{SMCLK}}{ID} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{4} = 250{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for (CCR0 + 1) using the frequency formula.**

$$CCR0 + 1 = \frac{f_{counter}}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{250{,}000}{100} = 2500$$

**Step 3 — Compute CCR0.**

$$CCR0 = 2500 - 1 = 2499$$

**Step 4 — Compute CCR1 from the duty-cycle formula.**

$$CCR1 = \frac{D \times (CCR0 + 1)}{100} = \frac{40 \times 2500}{100} = 1000$$

**Final Answers:** `CCR0 = 2499`, `CCR1 = 1000`.

#### (b) Write the MSP430 C code to configure the timer and the GPIO pin for PWM output. Also explain the role of `OUTMOD_7`. (7 marks)

**Model C Code:**

```c
#include <msp430.h>

void main(void)
{
    WDTCTL = WDTPW | WDTHOLD;   // Stop watchdog
    P1DIR |= BIT2;              // P1.2 as output
    P1SEL |= BIT2;              // Select TA1 peripheral function
    TACTL = TASSEL_2 | ID_2 | MC_1 | TACLR;  // SMCLK, /4, Up mode, clear
    TACCR0 = 2499;              // Period = 100 Hz
    TACCR1 = 1000;              // 40% duty cycle
    TACCTL1 = OUTMOD_7;         // Reset/Set PWM mode
    while(1)
    {
        _BIS_SR(LPM0_bits + GIE);   // Enter LPM0 with interrupts
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `OUTMOD_7` (Reset/Set Mode):**
- When TAR = 0 (counter reset), the output is **Set** (HIGH).
- When TAR = CCR1 (compare match), the output is **Reset** (LOW).
- When TAR = CCR0 (period end), the counter restarts and the cycle repeats.
- The output pin therefore is HIGH for the time TAR takes to count from 0 to CCR1, and LOW for the remaining time. This generates a precise PWM waveform with duty cycle `CCR1 / (CCR0 + 1)`.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Question:** Design a microcontroller-based **LED brightness controller** and a **DC motor speed controller** using PWM.

#### (a) Explain how PWM is used to control the brightness of an LED. Draw the circuit diagram and derive the expression for the average voltage across the LED. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Working principle.**
An LED is a current-driven device; its brightness is approximately proportional to the forward current. By switching the LED ON and OFF rapidly using PWM, the **average current** can be controlled without using a bulky potentiometer (which wastes power as heat).

**Step 2 — Circuit description.**
The PWM output pin of the MSP430 drives the **gate of an N-channel MOSFET** (e.g., IRLZ44N). The MOSFET's drain is connected to the cathode of the LED string, and the anode is tied to a positive supply (e.g., 12 V) through a current-limiting resistor. The source of the MOSFET is grounded.

**Step 3 — Average voltage derivation.**
The PWM signal is a square wave with HIGH time $T_H$ and LOW time $T_L$ within period $T$:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V(t) dt = \frac{V_{cc} \cdot T_H + 0 \cdot T_L}{T} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{T_H}{T} = V_{cc} \cdot D$$

**Step 4 — Final equation.**

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \times V_{cc} = \frac{CCR1}{CCR0 + 1} \times V_{cc}}$$

For a 12 V supply and D = 30 %, $V_{avg} = 0.3 \times 12 = 3.6$ V.

**Step 5 — Advantage.** MOSFET dissipation $P_{MOS} = I^2 \times R_{DS} \times D$ is very low (since $R_{DS}$ is milliohms), giving an efficiency of > 90 %.

#### (b) Explain how the same PWM technique is used to vary the speed of a 12 V DC motor. Mention the role of the flyback diode. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Speed–voltage relationship.**
The back-EMF equation of a DC motor is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, and $E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot N$. Therefore:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi} \approx \frac{V_{avg}}{k \Phi} \quad (\text{for constant load})$$

**Step 2 — PWM as variable voltage source.**
By varying the duty cycle of the PWM signal applied to the motor, the average voltage across the motor terminals is varied, and hence the speed is varied linearly.

**Step 3 — H-bridge or low-side driver.**
For unidirectional control, a single N-MOSFET on the low side is sufficient. For bidirectional (forward/reverse), an H-bridge (L293D, DRV8871) is used with two PWM-capable inputs (one for each half-bridge).

**Step 4 — Role of the flyback (freewheeling) diode.**
When the MOSFET switches OFF, the motor's inductance forces the current to continue flowing. Without a diode, the inductive kickback would create a huge voltage spike that destroys the MOSFET. A **flyback diode** (often a Schottky for high switching speeds) provides a path for this current to circulate, clamping the voltage to one diode-drop above the supply.

**Step 5 — Final speed formula.**

$$\boxed{N = D \times N_{no\,load} - \frac{T_{load}}{k_T}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1"** in `(CCR0 + 1)`. The counter has `CCR0 + 1` *distinct* count values from 0 to CCR0 inclusive. KTU answer keys explicitly check for this.
> 2. **Confusing UP mode period with Continuous mode period.** Continuous mode has a fixed period of 65536 counts; UP mode has a user-defined period of `CCR0 + 1` counts.
> 3. **Using the wrong OUTMOD value.** Only `OUTMOD_7` (Reset/Set) and `OUTMOD_3` (Set/Reset) produce PWM in UP mode. Using `OUTMOD_4` (Toggle) gives a 50 %-only square wave and loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Missing the divider (ID)** in frequency calculations. Always write $f_{counter} = f_{source} / ID$ *before* plugging into the period formula.
> 5. **Conflating RTC with Timer_A.** RTC is for *calendar* time; Timer_A is for *event* time. KTU questions often explicitly ask the difference for 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting BCD encoding** in RTC programming. Writing `RTCSEC = 45` is *wrong*; you must write `RTCSEC = 0x45`.
> 7. **Drawing the flyback diode backwards** in the motor circuit. The cathode must be at $V_{cc}$ and the anode at the motor terminal.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2):**
> - [ ] Timer_A has **4 modes** — Stop, Up, Continuous, Up/Down — and **3 CCR blocks**.
> - [ ] The `+1` in `(CCR0 + 1)` is mandatory in all period and duty-cycle calculations.
> - [ ] Frequency formula: $f_{PWM} = f_{clock} / (ID \times (CCR0 + 1))$.
> - [ ] Duty cycle formula: $D = CCR1 / (CCR0 + 1) \times 100\,\%$.
> - [ ] PWM in UP mode uses **OUTMOD_7 (Reset/Set)** — the most-tested concept.
> - [ ] PWM frequency ≥ 100 Hz for LED dimming; ≥ 20 kHz for motors to avoid audible whine.
> - [ ] RTC uses **32.768 kHz = 2¹⁵ Hz** crystal for exact 1-second ticks.
> - [ ] RTC registers are **BCD-encoded**, not binary.
> - [ ] RTC can run in **LPM3** with CPU off — ideal for low-power IoT nodes.
> - [ ] LED brightness control exploits eye persistence-of-vision (~24 ms integration time).
> - [ ] DC motor speed $N \propto V_{avg} = D \times V_{cc}$ (assuming constant load).
> - [ ] **Flyback diode** is mandatory across inductive loads like motors and relays.
> - [ ] PWM drives are far more efficient than linear regulators (90 %+ vs ~50 %).
> - [ ] Servo motors use **50 Hz PWM** with pulse width 1–2 ms to set position.
> - [ ] Capture mode (Mode = Continuous) is used to measure *external* event timestamps.
> - [ ] Compare mode (Mode = Up/Up-Down) is used to generate *internal* waveforms.
> - [ ] `TACLR` bit must be set after configuring TACTL to clear the counter and divider.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid:** "**TAR counts, CCR0 caps, CCR1 chops, OUTMOD_7 pops**." — This summarises the entire PWM generation chain in one breath.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols: Overview of Serial Communication Protocols - USART, I2C, and SPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols: Overview of Serial Communication Protocols - USART, I2C, and SPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Serial Communication Protocols

In the world of embedded systems and microcontrollers, **data communication** is the lifeline that allows a microcontroller to "talk" to sensors, displays, memory chips, computers, and other microcontrollers. While parallel communication transfers multiple bits simultaneously across many wires, **serial communication** sends data one bit at a time over a single (or very few) wire(s). This makes it cheaper, simpler to route on PCBs, and ideal for long-distance or low-pin-count applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEADT418 - Module 3):** Serial communication is the process of sequentially transmitting data bits over a communication channel or bus. The three most important serial protocols extensively tested in KTU examinations are **USART** (Asynchronous/Synchronous point-to-point), **I²C** (Synchronous, multi-master, multi-slave, 2-wire), and **SPI** (Synchronous, single-master, multi-slave, 4-wire).

## 1.2 What is USART?

**USART** stands for **Universal Synchronous and Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter**. It is a hardware peripheral inside a microcontroller that converts parallel data from the CPU into a serial bit-stream for transmission, and vice-versa for reception.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine two friends talking through a long, narrow paper-telephone tube. They can only send one voice-sound at a time. To know when a message ends, they agree on a special "beep" at the start and "silence" at the end. USART works exactly like this — it sends one bit at a time, with a **Start bit** and a **Stop bit** to mark each "word" of data (called a *frame*). Sometimes they also agree to add a "parity bit" — like spelling-check — to catch errors.

- **Asynchronous (UART) mode:** No shared clock line. Both sides must agree beforehand on the **baud rate** (bits per second). Standard values: **9600, 19200, 115200** bps.
- **Synchronous (USART) mode:** A separate clock line (XCK) is sent along with data, so timing is perfectly aligned. Used for higher speeds or where precision is critical.

## 1.3 What is I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)?

**I²C** (pronounced "I-squared-C" or "I-two-C") is a **synchronous, multi-master, multi-slave, packet-switched, 2-wire serial bus** invented by Philips Semiconductors (now NXP) in 1982.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a classroom with one teacher (the **Master**) and many students (the **Slaves**). There is one long microphone wire (**SDA** - data) and one bell wire (**SCL** - clock). The teacher rings the bell to control speaking pace. Before a student speaks, the teacher calls out the student's unique name/roll-number (**address**). The addressed student replies; all others stay silent. Only two wires are needed regardless of how many devices are on the bus.

- **SDA** = Serial Data line (bidirectional)
- **SCL** = Serial Clock line (always generated by the master)
- Standard bus speeds: **Standard Mode = 100 kHz**, **Fast Mode = 400 kHz**, **Fast-Plus = 1 MHz**, **High-Speed = 3.4 MHz**.

## 1.4 What is SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)?

**SPI** is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave, 4-wire serial bus** developed by Motorola. It is the fastest of the three protocols and is widely used for SD cards, displays, sensors, and ADC/DAC chips.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a teacher with a walkie-talkie and multiple students, each with their own walkie-talkie on a different channel. The teacher (master) holds a stopwatch (**SCLK**) and clicks it for every single bit. The teacher has one wire to send voice out (**MOSI**) and one separate wire to receive voice back (**MISO**). To choose which student is talking, the teacher has multiple "selector" wires (**SS / CS**), one per student. The teacher and student speak simultaneously — a true full-duplex conversation.

- **SCLK** = Serial Clock (from master)
- **MOSI** = Master Out, Slave In (data from master to slave)
- **MISO** = Master In, Slave Out (data from slave to master)
- **SS / CS** = Slave Select / Chip Select (active-low, one per slave)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three protocols?** KTU emphasizes that the choice depends on **speed, pin count, distance, and number of devices**. USART = 2 wires, point-to-point, moderate speed. I²C = 2 wires, many devices, slow-medium. SPI = 4+ wires, many devices, very fast. Students must be able to *compare and contrast* these three — a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU exams.

## 1.5 Physical Layer Summary Table

| Feature | USART (Async) | I²C | SPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wires required | 2 (TX, RX) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) |
| Clock | Not shared | Shared (SCL) | Shared (SCLK) |
| Speed (typical) | Up to ~1 Mbps | 100 kHz – 3.4 MHz | Up to 10+ MHz |
| Devices on bus | 1-to-1 | Up to 127 (7-bit addr) | Limited by SS pins |
| Duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| Topology | Point-to-point | Multi-master, multi-slave | Single master, multi-slave |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 USART — Frame Format and Operation

In **asynchronous mode**, data is sent as a **frame** with a precise structure. The transmitting and receiving devices must agree on identical **baud rate, parity, and stop-bit count** (often called "8N1" — 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit — the most common default).

**A standard USART frame (8N1) consists of:**

1. **Idle line** = HIGH (logic 1, called "MARK")
2. **Start bit** = 1 bit, always LOW (logic 0, called "SPACE") — this signals the beginning of a frame.
3. **Data bits** = typically 5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 bits. Sent LSB first.
4. **Parity bit** = optional (Even or Odd). Used for simple error detection.
5. **Stop bit(s)** = 1 or 2 bits, always HIGH — returns line to idle.
6. Line returns to **idle (HIGH)** before the next start bit.

### Parity Bit Logic (KTU Frequently Asked)
- **Even parity:** The total number of 1's in the data + parity bit is **even**.
- **Odd parity:** The total number of 1's in the data + parity bit is **odd**.

### USART Registers in Typical Microcontrollers (e.g., AVR ATmega / PIC)
- **UCSRA / UCSRnA** — Status and control (e.g., `RXC`, `TXC`, `UDRE` flags)
- **UCSRB / UCSRnB** — Enables (RXEN, TXEN, interrupt enables)
- **UCSRC / UCSRnC** — Mode select (UMSEL for sync/async, UPM for parity, USBS for stop bits, UCSZ for data size)
- **UBRR / UBRRn** — Baud Rate Register
- **UDR / UDRn** — Data Register (read for RX, write for TX)

### USART Baud Rate Formula (AVR-style)
For asynchronous normal mode (U2X = 0):

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times (\text{UBRR} + 1)}$$

For double-speed mode (U2X = 1):

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{8 \times (\text{UBRR} + 1)}$$

Solving for UBRR:

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud Rate}} - 1$$

## 2.2 I²C — Detailed Operation and Protocol

I²C communication always follows a strict, well-defined sequence. Every byte transferred is **acknowledged (ACK)** by the receiver, which makes I²C highly reliable but slower than SPI.

### Key Signals in I²C

- **Start Condition (S):** SDA falls LOW while SCL is HIGH. Initiated only by the master.
- **Stop Condition (P):** SDA rises HIGH while SCL is HIGH. Initiated only by the master.
- **Repeated Start (Sr):** A new Start without a preceding Stop — used to change direction or slave without releasing the bus.
- **ACK (Acknowledge):** Receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse.
- **NACK (Not Acknowledge):** Receiver leaves SDA HIGH during the 9th clock pulse — used to signal "no more data" or "address not recognized."

### I²C Data Transfer Sequence (KTU-Mandated)

1. Master issues **Start** condition.
2. Master transmits **7-bit slave address + 1 R/W bit** (MSB first).
3. Slave pulls SDA low to **ACK** the address match.
4. Master transmits/receives a **data byte** (8 bits, MSB first).
5. Receiver issues **ACK** (or NACK) on the 9th clock.
6. After the last byte, master issues **Stop** condition.

### I²C Addressing
- **7-bit addressing** is standard (the 8th bit is R/W). Allows 2⁷ = **128** addresses; in practice, 112 usable (16 reserved).
- **10-bit addressing** is optional and used when > 128 devices must coexist.
- Slave addresses are often written as 8-bit values in datasheets (e.g., EEPROM **0xA0** for write, **0xA1** for read) where the LSB is R/W.

### Pull-up Resistors
Both SDA and SCL are **open-drain** lines. They require external **pull-up resistors** to VCC (typically **2.2 kΩ to 10 kΩ**) for proper logic-high levels. The minimum value depends on bus capacitance:

$$R_{pull-up(min)} = \frac{V_{CC} - 0.4\,V}{I_{OL}}$$

where **I_OL** is the sink current of the output pin (commonly 3 mA). Maximum value depends on the desired rise time and total bus capacitance.

### Clock Stretching
A slave can **hold SCL LOW** to delay the master — this is called *clock stretching* and lets slow slaves control data flow.

## 2.3 SPI — Detailed Operation and Protocol

SPI is simpler and faster than I²C but uses more pins. It supports **full-duplex** communication: every clock cycle, one bit flows from master to slave on MOSI, and simultaneously one bit flows from slave to master on MISO.

### SPI Master-Slave Selection
- The master has one dedicated **SS (Slave Select)** pin per slave. SS is **active-LOW** (idle HIGH).
- When SS for a particular slave goes LOW, that slave is "selected" and listens to SCLK, MOSI, and drives MISO.
- All other slaves ignore the bus (their MISO goes to high-impedance).

### SPI Clock Modes (CRITICAL for KTU!)
SPI has **4 clock modes** defined by two bits: **CPOL** (Clock Polarity) and **CPHA** (Clock Phase). These must match between master and slave — mismatch is the #1 reason SPI fails to work.

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Clock Idle State | Data Samled On | Data Shifted On |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising edge (leading) | Falling edge (trailing) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling edge (trailing) | Rising edge (leading) |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling edge (leading) | Rising edge (trailing) |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising edge (trailing) | Falling edge (leading) |

### SPI Daisy-Chaining
Multiple slaves can be connected **in series** (daisy-chain) using a single SS line. Data propagates from one slave to the next, allowing communication with many devices using only one SS pin. This is commonly used in cascaded ADC chips and LED drivers.

### SPI Speed
SPI speed is limited by:
1. The master's maximum SCLK frequency
2. The slave's maximum SCLK frequency (always the bottleneck)
3. PCB trace length and capacitance

Typical values: 1 MHz to 50 MHz, with modern MCUs supporting 80–100 MHz.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$ | USART baud rate register (AVR async normal) |
| 2 | $\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times (\text{UBRR}+1)}$ | Actual baud rate achieved |
| 3 | $\% \text{Error} = \frac{\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} - \text{Baud}_{\text{desired}}}{\text{Baud}_{\text{desired}}} \times 100$ | Baud rate error (must be < 2%) |
| 4 | $\text{Frame Time} = \frac{1 + D + P + S}{\text{Baud}}$ | Time per USART frame (D=data bits, P=parity, S=stop bits) |
| 5 | I²C Standard: 100 kbps | Slowest standard I²C speed |
| 6 | I²C Fast Mode: 400 kbps | Most common in modern MCUs |
| 7 | I²C High Speed: 3.4 Mbps | Reserved addressing required |
| 8 | I²C Max Devices: 2⁷ = 128 | 7-bit addressing |
| 9 | SPI Modes: 0, 1, 2, 3 | CPOL/CPHA combinations |
| 10 | Full-duplex = simultaneous TX + RX | True for SPI and USART, not I²C |
| 11 | MSB vs LSB: USART = LSB first; I²C = MSB first; SPI = MSB first (default) | Bit-ordering convention |
| 12 | I²C Pull-up: $R_{min} = (V_{CC} - 0.4)/I_{OL}$ | Minimum pull-up value |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **USART** is the backbone of **debug consoles**, **GPS modules** (NMEA protocol at 9600 baud), **Bluetooth modules (HC-05)**, and **legacy RS-232/RS-485** industrial networks.
- **I²C** is the king of **on-board low-speed peripherals**: real-time clocks (DS1307), EEPROMs (24Cxx), temperature sensors (LM75, BMP280), GPIO expanders (PCF8574), and OLED displays.
- **SPI** dominates where **speed matters**: **SD cards**, **TFT/LCD displays**, **high-speed ADCs**, **flash memory (W25Q)**, and **audio DACs**. Industrial systems and automotive ECUs use SPI for sensor clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If the peripheral is on the *same PCB* and is *fast* → use **SPI**. If it is on the *same PCB*, you have *many devices*, and *few pins* → use **I²C**. If you need *long-distance* or *point-to-point* with a *PC/host* → use **USART/UART**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: USART Baud Rate Register Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A microcontroller uses an oscillator of **f_OSC = 8 MHz**. It must communicate with a PC over USART at **9600 baud**, asynchronous mode, 8N1. Calculate the value to be loaded into the **UBRR** register (assume normal mode, U2X = 0). Also calculate the **% error**.

### Step 1: Recall the KTU Formula
The relationship between oscillator frequency, baud rate, and UBRR in asynchronous normal mode is:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times (\text{UBRR} + 1)}$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for UBRR
Rearranging algebraically — multiply both sides by $16(\text{UBRR}+1)$:

$$\text{Baud} \times 16 \times (\text{UBRR} + 1) = f_{OSC}$$

Divide both sides by $16 \times \text{Baud}$:

$$\text{UBRR} + 1 = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud}}$$

Subtract 1:

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Numerical Values
Plugging in $f_{OSC} = 8{,}000{,}000$ Hz and $\text{Baud} = 9600$:

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

Compute the denominator: $16 \times 9600 = 153{,}600$

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{153{,}600} - 1$$

Perform the division: $8{,}000{,}000 \div 153{,}600 = 52.0833...$

$$\text{UBRR} = 52.0833 - 1 = 51.0833$$

### Step 4: Take the Integer Value
Since UBRR is an 8-bit or 12-bit integer register, we take $\text{UBRR} = 51$ (rounded to nearest integer).

### Step 5: Compute the Actual Baud Rate Achieved
Use the rounded value back in the original formula:

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{16 \times (51 + 1)} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{16 \times 52}$$

$$= \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{832} = 9615.38 \text{ baud}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Percentage Error
$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{15.38}{9600} \times 100 = 0.160\%$$

This is well within the KTU-acceptable tolerance of **< 2%**, so UBRR = 51 is correct.

### Final Answer
- **UBRR = 51 (decimal)**
- **Actual baud = 9615.38 bps**
- **% Error = 0.16%** ✓ Acceptable

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: I²C Frame Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An I²C bus runs at **400 kHz** (Fast Mode). A master transmits 3 data bytes to a slave whose address is **0x50** (7-bit), with no repeated start. Calculate the total **bus time** for the complete transaction (Start → Address+RW byte → Data byte 1 → Data byte 2 → Data byte 3 → Stop). Ignore rise/fall times.

### Step 1: Count the Total Number of SCL Clock Pulses
Each byte transferred on I²C requires **9 SCL pulses** (8 data + 1 ACK).

- Address byte = 9 pulses
- 3 data bytes = 3 × 9 = 27 pulses
- Start and Stop conditions are *not* clocked — they are signaling events.

**Total SCL pulses = 9 + 27 = 36 pulses**

### Step 2: Compute the Period of One SCL Clock
At 400 kHz:

$$T_{SCL} = \frac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \frac{1}{400{,}000} = 2.5\, \mu s$$

### Step 3: Multiply for Total Transaction Time
$$T_{total} = 36 \times 2.5\, \mu s = 90\, \mu s$$

### Final Answer
**Total I²C transaction time = 90 µs** for a write of 3 bytes to slave 0x50 at 400 kHz.

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: SPI Mode Identification

**Problem Statement:** A slave ADC requires that data be **sampled on the rising edge of SCLK** and that **SCLK idles in the HIGH state**. Identify the correct SPI mode (Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3) and state the CPOL and CPHA values.

### Step 1: Identify the Idle State
- "SCLK idles HIGH" ⇒ **CPOL = 1**
- This narrows choice to **Mode 2 or Mode 3**.

### Step 2: Identify the Sampling Edge
- "Sampled on rising edge" ⇒ **CPHA = 0** (because with CPOL = 1, the first edge is a *falling* edge used for shifting, and the second is a *rising* edge used for sampling).

Wait — let us re-examine using the KTU table from Section 2.3:
- **Mode 2 (CPOL=1, CPHA=0):** sampled on the **falling edge**.
- **Mode 3 (CPOL=1, CPHA=1):** sampled on the **rising edge**.

### Step 3: Match the Requirement
We need sampling on the **rising edge** with **CPOL=1** → this is **Mode 3**.

### Final Answer
**SPI Mode 3: CPOL = 1, CPHA = 1**

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Always Appears in KTU)

| Comparison Parameter | USART (UART mode) | I²C | SPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous? | No (asynchronous) | Yes (SCL) | Yes (SCLK) |
| Number of wires | 2 (TX, RX) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) |
| Maximum devices | 1-to-1 | 128 (7-bit addr) | Limited by SS pins on master |
| Speed class | Low to medium | Low to medium-high | High to very high |
| Duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| Master/Slave | Peer-to-peer | Multi-master, multi-slave | Single master, multi-slave |
| Bit-order | LSB first | MSB first | MSB first (typical) |
| Acknowledgment | Optional (parity) | Mandatory (ACK/NACK) | None |
| Bus arbitration | None | Yes (multi-master) | None (single master) |
| Typical use | Debug, GPS, BT, RS-232 | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM | Displays, SD, Flash, ADC |
| Power consumption | Lower | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | Low |
| Software complexity | Low | Medium-high | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** When asked to "compare" these three, you **must** cover at least: *wires, speed, number of devices, duplexity, and synchronization*. Marks are awarded per parameter covered.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: SPI Throughput

**Problem Statement:** An SPI master runs at **10 MHz** SCLK and transfers **16-bit data** to a slave. Compute the throughput in **kB/s** (kilobytes per second). The slave responds with simultaneous 16-bit data (full-duplex).

### Step 1: Time per Bit
$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{SCLK}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.1\, \mu s = 100\, ns$$

### Step 2: Time per 16-bit Word
$$T_{word} = 16 \times 100\, ns = 1.6\, \mu s$$

### Step 3: Throughput
$$\text{Bytes/s} = \frac{1 \text{ word}}{1.6\, \mu s} \times 2 \text{ bytes/word} = \frac{2}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 1.25 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} = 1250 \text{ kB/s}$$

### Final Answer
**SPI Throughput = 1.25 MB/s ≈ 1250 kB/s**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note
All Mermaid diagrams below use purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords are used as node names. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.2 USART Asynchronous Frame Format (Timing Diagram Description)

The following flowchart maps the **transmit/receive state machine** of a USART module:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STATE IDLE LINE HIGH] --> B[WAIT FOR TX START OR RX START BIT DETECTED]
    B --> C[START BIT DETECTED SDA LINE GOES LOW]
    C --> D[SHIFT IN 8 DATA BITS LSB FIRST]
    D --> E[CHECK PARITY BIT IF ENABLED]
    E --> F[RECEIVE STOP BIT LINE RETURNS HIGH]
    F --> G[SET RXC FLAG RAISE INTERRUPT]
    G --> H{BUFFER FULL}
    H -- YES --> I[OVERRUN ERROR FLAG SET]
    H -- NO --> J[STORE BYTE IN UDR]
    J --> K[RETURN TO IDLE STATE]
    I --> K
```

## 4.3 I²C Master-Slave Bus Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[MASTER MCU] -- SDA SERIAL DATA --> BUS[(I2C BUS)]
    M -- SCL SERIAL CLOCK --> BUS
    BUS -- SDA SCL --> S1[SLAVE 1 SENSOR 0x48]
    BUS -- SDA SCL --> S2[SLAVE 2 EEPROM 0x50]
    BUS -- SDA SCL --> S3[SLAVE 3 RTC 0x68]
    BUS -- SDA SCL --> S4[SLAVE 4 GPIO EXPANDER 0x20]
    S1 -.->|ACK NACK| M
    S2 -.->|ACK NACK| M
    S3 -.->|ACK NACK| M
    S4 -.->|ACK NACK| M
```

## 4.4 I²C Data Transfer Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[MASTER HOLDS BUS IDLE] --> A1[MASTER GENERATES START CONDITION SDA FALLS WHILE SCL HIGH]
    A1 --> A2[MASTER SENDS 7 BIT ADDRESS + 1 BIT R/W MSB FIRST]
    A2 --> A3{SLAVE ACKNOWLEDGES SDA LOW IN 9TH CLOCK}
    A3 -- NO NACK --> A4[MASTER GENERATES STOP AND ABORTS]
    A3 -- YES ACK --> A5[MASTER OR SLAVE SENDS 1 DATA BYTE 8 BITS]
    A5 --> A6[RECEIVER SENDS ACK OR NACK]
    A6 --> A7{LAST BYTE}
    A7 -- NO --> A5
    A7 -- YES --> A8[MASTER GENERATES STOP CONDITION SDA RISES WHILE SCL HIGH]
    A8 --> A9[BUS RETURNS TO IDLE]
```

## 4.5 SPI Single-Master Multi-Slave Hardware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[SPI MASTER MCU] -- SCLK CLOCK --> S1SS[SLAVE 1 SS]
    M -- SCLK CLOCK --> S2SS[SLAVE 2 SS]
    M -- SCLK CLOCK --> S3SS[SLAVE 3 SS]
    M -- MOSI MASTER OUT SLAVE IN --> S1M
    M -- MOSI --> S2M
    M -- MOSI --> S3M
    S1M[SLAVE 1] -- MISO --> M
    S2M[SLAVE 2] -- MISO --> M
    S3M[SLAVE 3] -- MISO --> M
    S1SS --> S1M
    S2SS --> S2M
    S3SS --> S3M
```

## 4.6 SPI Clock Modes Visualization (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPOL_0[CPOL 0 SCLK IDLES LOW]
        M0A[MODE 0 CPHA 0 SAMPLE ON RISING EDGE] --> M0B[SHIFT ON FALLING EDGE]
    end
    subgraph CPOL_0B[CPOL 0 SCLK IDLES LOW]
        M1A[MODE 1 CPHA 1 SAMPLE ON FALLING EDGE] --> M1B[SHIFT ON RISING EDGE]
    end
    subgraph CPOL_1[CPOL 1 SCLK IDLES HIGH]
        M2A[MODE 2 CPHA 0 SAMPLE ON FALLING EDGE] --> M2B[SHIFT ON RISING EDGE]
    end
    subgraph CPOL_1B[CPOL 1 SCLK IDLES HIGH]
        M3A[MODE 3 CPHA 1 SAMPLE ON RISING EDGE] --> M3B[SHIFT ON FALLING EDGE]
    end
```

## 4.7 USART vs I²C vs SPI Master Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[NEED TO COMMUNICATE WITH AN EXTERNAL DEVICE] --> Q1{HOW MANY DEVICES ON SAME BUS}
    Q1 -- JUST ONE --> Q2{NEED LONG DISTANCE OR PC CONNECTION}
    Q1 -- MULTIPLE --> Q3{HAVE ENOUGH PINS FOR SS LINES}
    Q2 -- YES --> USART[USE USART UART]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4{NEED VERY HIGH SPEED}
    Q4 -- YES --> SPI[USE SPI]
    Q4 -- NO --> I2C[USE I2C]
    Q3 -- YES --> SPI
    Q3 -- NO --> I2C
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous serial communication. Give one example protocol for each."**

**Model Answer:**
- **Asynchronous serial communication:** No shared clock line between sender and receiver. Both ends must agree on the same baud rate beforehand. Data is framed using start and stop bits. Example: **UART/USART** (asynchronous mode), RS-232.
- **Synchronous serial communication:** A shared clock line accompanies the data, so the receiver samples bits in lock-step with the transmitter. No start/stop bits are required. Examples: **I²C** (uses SCL), **SPI** (uses SCLK), USART (synchronous mode).
- *Key contrast:* Synchronous is faster and more reliable but uses an extra pin; asynchronous uses fewer wires but requires baud-rate matching.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List the signals used in the SPI bus and explain the function of the SS pin."**

**Model Answer:**
- **SCLK:** Serial Clock, generated by the master to synchronize data transfer.
- **MOSI:** Master Out, Slave In. Carries data from master to slave.
- **MISO:** Master In, Slave Out. Carries data from slave to master.
- **SS (Slave Select) / CS (Chip Select):** Active-low control line, one per slave. When pulled LOW by the master, the corresponding slave is selected and participates in communication. When HIGH, the slave ignores SCLK/MOSI and tristates its MISO output.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **USART frame format** for 8N1 asynchronous communication with a neat timing diagram description. List the **USART registers** in a typical AVR microcontroller and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the value of the **UBRR register** required to generate a baud rate of **19200 bps** from a **16 MHz** crystal oscillator in asynchronous normal mode (U2X = 0). Calculate the **percentage error** and comment on whether it is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — USART Frame Format and Registers (7 marks)

**Frame Format (8N1):**
1. **Idle:** Line is held HIGH (logic 1, called "MARK") when no transmission.
2. **Start Bit (1 bit):** Line goes LOW. This falling edge alerts the receiver that a new frame is starting.
3. **Data Bits (8 bits):** LSB first. The bits D0, D1, …, D7 are sampled at the center of each bit period for maximum noise immunity.
4. **Parity Bit:** Absent in 8N1 (N = No parity). If enabled, the bit is set to make the total number of 1s even (Even) or odd (Odd).
5. **Stop Bit (1 bit):** Line returns HIGH. Guarantees at least one HIGH period before the next start bit.
6. **Line Idle:** After the stop bit, the line remains HIGH until the next start bit.

Total frame length = 1 (start) + 8 (data) + 0 (parity) + 1 (stop) = **10 bit periods**.

**AVR USART Registers:**
- **UCSRnA:** Status flags — `RXC` (Receive Complete), `TXC` (Transmit Complete), `UDRE` (Data Register Empty), `FE`, `DOR`, `UPE` (error flags), `U2X` (double speed).
- **UCSRnB:** Control — `RXEN` (RX enable), `TXEN` (TX enable), `RXCIE`/`TXCIE`/`UDRIE` (interrupt enables), `TXB8`/`RXB8` (9th-bit storage).
- **UCSRnC:** Mode — `UMSEL` (0 = async, 1 = sync), `UPM1:0` (parity), `USBS` (stop bits), `UCSZ1:0` (data size), `UCPOL` (clock polarity in sync mode).
- **UBRRnH:UBRRnL:** Baud Rate Register — 12-bit value defining the divisor.
- **UDRn:** Data Register — single 8-bit buffer used for both TX and RX.

#### Part (b) — UBRR Derivation (7 marks)

Given: $f_{OSC} = 16 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\text{Baud} = 19200$ bps, U2X = 0.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \times 19200} - 1 = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{307{,}200} - 1$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$= 52.0833 - 1 = 51.0833$$

**Step 4:** Round to nearest integer: $\text{UBRR} = 51$.

**Step 5:** Compute actual baud rate:

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \times 52} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{832} = 19{,}230.77 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 6:** Compute error:

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{19230.77 - 19200}{19200} \times 100 = \frac{30.77}{19200} \times 100 = 0.160\%$$

**Step 7:** Comment — A baud rate error of **0.16%** is well within the **< 2%** tolerance recommended for reliable asynchronous communication. **Hence, UBRR = 51 is correct and acceptable.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **architecture of the I²C bus** including SDA, SCL, Start/Stop conditions, and the role of **pull-up resistors**. State the I²C address and acknowledge sequence for a complete write transaction to a slave. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **USART, I²C, and SPI** serial communication protocols under the following heads: (i) Number of wires, (ii) Synchronous/Asynchronous, (iii) Maximum number of devices, (iv) Speed, (v) Duplexity, (vi) Typical applications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — I²C Bus Architecture (7 marks)

**I²C Bus Architecture:**
I²C is a **2-wire, multi-master, multi-slave, synchronous, half-duplex** bus invented by Philips. The two wires are:
- **SDA (Serial Data):** Bidirectional data line.
- **SCL (Serial Clock):** Always generated by the current master; unidirectional in the active transfer.
- Both lines are **open-drain**, meaning devices can only pull the line LOW. An external **pull-up resistor** (typically **2.2 kΩ to 10 kΩ**) to VCC is required to pull the line HIGH when no device is asserting LOW. This wired-AND configuration allows multiple devices to share the bus safely.

**Start and Stop Conditions:**
- **Start (S):** A HIGH-to-LOW transition on SDA while SCL is HIGH. Issued by the master.
- **Stop (P):** A LOW-to-HIGH transition on SDA while SCL is HIGH. Issued by the master.
- The bus is considered **busy** between a Start and the next Stop, and idle otherwise.

**Write Transaction Sequence:**
1. Master issues **Start** condition.
2. Master transmits **7-bit slave address** followed by **R/W = 0** (write), MSB first.
3. The addressed slave responds with **ACK** by pulling SDA LOW on the 9th clock.
4. Master transmits the **first data byte** (e.g., memory/register address). Slave ACKs.
5. Master transmits the **second data byte** (e.g., data value). Slave ACKs.
6. After the last byte + ACK, master issues **Stop** condition to release the bus.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | USART (Async) | I²C | SPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Wires** | 2 (TX, RX) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) |
| **(ii) Sync/Async** | Asynchronous | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| **(iii) Max devices** | 1-to-1 | 128 (7-bit) | Limited by SS pins |
| **(iv) Speed** | Up to ~1 Mbps | 100 kHz – 3.4 MHz | Up to 10–50 MHz |
| **(v) Duplexity** | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| **(vi) Applications** | Debug console, GPS, BT, RS-232 | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM, GPIO expander | SD card, LCD/TFT, Flash, ADC/DAC |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only the comparison table without explanatory sentences, losing 1–2 marks. The KTU answer-key pattern requires **at least one descriptive sentence per row** explaining *why* the parameter differs. Also, students frequently confuse **full-duplex with half-duplex** — remember, full-duplex means **simultaneous** two-way transfer; half-duplex means **one-way at a time**. I²C is half-duplex because SDA is shared.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Communication Protocols**

- ✅ **USART** = Universal Synchronous/Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter. **2 wires** (TX, RX). Asynchronous mode = **Start + Data + Parity + Stop** frame, **LSB first**. Baud rate must match on both sides.
- ✅ **UBRR formula** (AVR async): $\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$. Error must be **< 2%**.
- ✅ **I²C** = 2-wire (**SDA, SCL**), synchronous, **half-duplex**, **multi-master**, open-drain with **pull-up resistors**. Start = SDA falls when SCL is HIGH. Stop = SDA rises when SCL is HIGH. Each byte = 9 clocks (8 data + 1 ACK).
- ✅ **I²C addressing** = 7-bit (or 10-bit), MSB first. Standard speed = **100 kHz**, Fast = **400 kHz**.
- ✅ **SPI** = 4+ wire (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS), synchronous, **full-duplex**, single master. **MSB first** (default). 4 clock modes defined by CPOL and CPHA — **master and slave MUST use the same mode**.
- ✅ **CPOL** = clock idle polarity (0 = LOW, 1 = HIGH). **CPHA** = clock phase (0 = sample on first edge, 1 = sample on second edge).
- ✅ **Master selects a slave** by pulling the corresponding **SS line LOW**.
- ✅ **Parity bit** in USART is for error detection: Even = total 1s even; Odd = total 1s odd.
- ✅ **I²C ACK** = SDA LOW during 9th clock. **NACK** = SDA HIGH during 9th clock.
- ✅ **Clock stretching** = slave holds SCL LOW to slow master.
- ✅ **I²C minimum pull-up**: $R_{min} = (V_{CC} - 0.4)/I_{OL}$.
- ✅ **Frame time** for USART 8N1: $T_{frame} = 10 / \text{Baud}$.
- ✅ **Daisy-chaining** in SPI: multiple slaves share one SS line, data shifts through them in series.
- ✅ **Comparison mantra** for KTU: 2-wire, sync, half-duplex → I²C. 2-wire, async, full-duplex → USART. 4-wire, sync, full-duplex → SPI.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid**: Don't confuse "synchronous USART mode" with I²C/SPI — synchronous USART still uses its own dedicated XCK line and is point-to-point; I²C/SPI have a *shared bus* model.
- ✅ **KTU favourite 7-marker**: "Compare USART, I²C, and SPI" — cover **wires, sync, speed, devices, duplexity, application** in a **table + sentences**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing an I2C Temperature Sensor and Displaying Data on an LCD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfacing an I2C Temperature Sensor and Displaying Data on an LCD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interfacing an I²C Temperature Sensor and Displaying Data on an LCD** is the process of connecting a digital temperature transducer that communicates over the **Inter-Integrated Circuit (I²C / IIC / TWI)** two-wire serial bus to a microcontroller, and routing the acquired thermal data to a **Hitachi HD44780-compatible Liquid Crystal Display** for human-readable visualization. In the context of the KTU 2024 PEADT418 syllabus (Module 3 — Serial Communication & Peripherals), this topic integrates three sub-systems: (i) the I²C master controller firmware inside the microcontroller (typically the 8051 or ARM Cortex-M0), (ii) the I²C slave temperature sensor (commonly **LM75 / DS1621 / TMP102**), and (iii) the **16×2 character LCD** driven in either 4-bit parallel mode or through an **I²C LCD backpack (PCF8574)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the I²C start/stop conditions, slave addressing, acknowledge (ACK/NACK) timing, byte-level data framing, and the conversion of raw sensor registers into engineering units (°C / °F).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **post-office with only two pneumatic tubes** connecting a central dispatch room (the microcontroller) to multiple small lockers (sensor chips) scattered in a building. One tube is labeled **SDA (Serial Data)** — it carries the actual letter (the bits). The other tube is labeled **SCL (Serial Clock)** — it ticks like a metronome telling the receiver *“read now, read now, read now.”* The dispatch room first announces the *locker number* (the **7-bit slave address**), the correct locker opens, the letter is dropped in, and the locker beeps back a *“got it”* signal (the **ACK bit**). That, in essence, is I²C.

For the LCD part, picture the LCD as a **TV screen that only understands a very specific dialect** (the HD44780 instruction set). The microcontroller must hand-shake with the LCD using the *Enable (EN), Register Select (RS), and Read/Write (R/W)* lines, send ASCII codes (e.g., `0x32` for the digit '2'), and tell the LCD *where on the screen to place it* (DDRAM address). When chained together, the pipeline becomes: **Sensor → I²C → MCU → LCD bus → Human eye.**

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Protocol Constants

| Constant / Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **SDA & SCL bus voltage** | **3.3 V or 5 V** | Pull-up resistors required (typically **4.7 kΩ**) |
| **Standard I²C clock (SCL)** | **100 kHz** | Fast-mode = 400 kHz; Fast-mode+ = 1 MHz |
| **LM75 slave address (7-bit)** | `1001 A2 A1 A0` | Three hardware address pins |
| **DS1621 slave address (7-bit)** | `1001 A2 A1 A0` | Default = `0x48` if all A-pins grounded |
| **TMP102 slave address (7-bit)** | `1001 A2 A1 A0` | Default = `0x48` or `0x49` |
| **I²C pull-up resistor (R_P)** | **4.7 kΩ** (standard), **2.2 kΩ** (fast mode) | Pulled to V_DD |
| **LCD data bus width** | 4-bit or 8-bit | KTU 8051 standard = 4-bit mode |
| **LCD DDRAM size** | 80 bytes (16×2 visible) | Address range `0x00` – `0x27` and `0x40` – `0x67` |
| **Default LM75 resolution** | 9-bit (0.5 °C / LSB) |  |
| **Default DS1621 resolution** | 9-bit (0.5 °C / LSB), configurable up to 12-bit (0.0625 °C / LSB) |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Endianness Note (Critical for KTU exams):** LM75/DS1621 return temperature as a **9-bit two's complement** value with the MSB transmitted first. Students frequently lose marks by misreading the byte order — the *first byte read* is the **MSB (integer °C)** and the *second byte* is the **LSB (fractional 0.5 °C)**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The I²C Bus — Operational Breakdown

The I²C bus is a **multi-master, multi-slave, synchronous, serial, open-drain** communication protocol developed by Philips Semiconductors (now NXP) in **1982**. Its physical layer uses just **two bidirectional lines**:

- **SDA (Serial Data Line):** carries the data payload.
- **SCL (Serial Clock Line):** generated by the master, paces every bit.

Both lines are tied to V_DD through **pull-up resistors (R_P)**; devices can only pull them **LOW** (wired-AND logic).

### 2.1.1 Bit-Level Rules
1. **Data is valid only when SCL = HIGH.** Transitions on SDA while SCL is HIGH are reserved for **START (S)** and **STOP (P)** conditions.
2. **START condition (S):** SDA falls (HIGH → LOW) while SCL is HIGH.
3. **STOP condition (P):** SDA rises (LOW → HIGH) while SCL is HIGH.
4. **Each byte = 8 bits**, transmitted MSB-first.
5. **The 9th clock pulse** is reserved for the **ACK** (acknowledgment) bit. The receiver pulls SDA LOW for ACK; releasing SDA (leaving HIGH via R_P) means **NACK**.

### 2.1.2 Slave Addressing
The first byte after START is the **address frame**:
$$\text{Address Byte} = \underbrace{A6\,A5\,A4\,A3\,A2\,A1\,A0}_{\text{7-bit slave address}}\;\underbrace{R/\overline{W}}_{\text{1-bit direction}}$$
- `R/W = 0` → Master **writes** to slave (Transmit mode).
- `R/W = 1` → Master **reads** from slave (Receive mode).

For LM75/DS1621, the address is hard-wired as `1001xxx` where `xxx` is the user-defined A2/A1/A0 pins.

### 2.1.3 Register Access Pattern (for DS1621 / LM75)
The temperature sensor exposes a small bank of internal registers. The typical **read-temperature** transaction is:

| Step | Master Action | Slave Response |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generate **START** | — |
| 2 | Send slave address byte (e.g., `0x90` = `1001 000 0` write) | ACK |
| 3 | Send **command/pointer byte** (`0x00` = Temperature register) | ACK |
| 4 | Generate **Repeated START** | — |
| 5 | Send slave address byte with R/W = 1 (`0x91`) | ACK |
| 6 | **Read MSB byte** from sensor | **ACK** from master |
| 7 | **Read LSB byte** from sensor | **NACK** from master |
| 8 | Generate **STOP** | — |

## 2.2 LM75 Internal Block Diagram (Logical View)
The LM75 contains:
- A **band-gap temperature sensor** (silicon-based, V_PTAT ≈ proportional-to-absolute-temperature).
- A **Σ-Δ ADC** (oversampling converter) producing a digital word.
- A **configuration register** (8 bits) controlling OS pin polarity, fault queue, shutdown, etc.
- A **temperature register** (read-only, 16-bit, only 11 bits used).
- An **I²C interface engine** with three address pins.

The analog front-end equation (silicon physics) is:
$$V_{PTAT} = \frac{kT}{q}\cdot \ln(N)\cdot K$$
where *k* is Boltzmann's constant, *q* is the electron charge, *N* is the ratio of two bipolar transistor currents, and *K* is a scale factor. The Σ-Δ modulator converts this PTAT voltage into a 9-bit (default) digital code.

## 2.3 Temperature Register Decoding

For LM75 / DS1621 (9-bit two's complement format, 0.5 °C resolution):

$$T_{°C} = \text{signed\ value\ of\ }[MSB:LSB\ upper\ 7\ bits] \times 0.5$$

For 12-bit DS1621 mode:
$$T_{°C} = \text{signed\ value\ of\ }[MSB:\text{LSB all 12 bits}] \times 0.0625$$

**Conversion to °F (for display):**
$$T_{°F} = T_{°C}\times \frac{9}{5} + 32$$

## 2.4 LCD (HD44780) Interfacing Theory

The HD44780 controller uses an **11-line interface** in 8-bit mode (reduced to **7 lines** in 4-bit mode, the KTU standard):
- **RS** — Register Select (0 = Instruction, 1 = Data).
- **R/W** — Read/Write (0 = Write, 1 = Read).
- **EN** — Enable latch pulse (HIGH → LOW on falling edge latches data).
- **D0–D7** — 8-bit data bus (or D4–D7 in 4-bit mode).

### 2.4.1 Initialization Sequence (4-bit mode, KTU standard)
Per the Hitachi datasheet, the following bytes must be issued after power-on delay (≥ 15 ms after V_DD stable):
1. `0x03` (×3 times) — function-set prep, 8-bit path.
2. `0x02` — switch to 4-bit mode.
3. `0x28` — function set: 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font.
4. `0x0C` — display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF.
5. `0x06` — entry mode: increment, no shift.
6. `0x01` — clear display.

### 2.4.2 DDRAM Address Map (16×2 LCD)

| Line | Visible Column Range | DDRAM Address Range |
|---|---|---|
| Line 1 | 0x00 – 0x0F | `0x00` – `0x0F` (0x10 – 0x27 not visible) |
| Line 2 | 0x40 – 0x4F | `0x40` – `0x4F` (0x50 – 0x67 not visible) |

To position the cursor, send instruction `0x80 + DDRAM_address`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I²C pull-up resistor sizing | $R_P = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | $\approx 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ standard |
| 2 | I²C address frame | $\text{byte} = A6 A5 A4 A3 A2 A1 A0\, R/\overline{W}$ | 8 bits total |
| 3 | LM75 default address | `1001 A2 A1 A0` | 7-bit, 0x48–0x4F |
| 4 | Temperature (9-bit mode) | $T = \text{MSB} + \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{2}$ | °C, range –55 to +125 |
| 5 | Temperature (12-bit mode) | $T = \text{MSB} + \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{256}$ | °C |
| 6 | Celsius → Fahrenheit | $T_F = T_C \times 1.8 + 32$ | °F |
| 7 | DDRAM line 1 start | `0x00` | — |
| 8 | DDRAM line 2 start | `0x40` | — |
| 9 | Set DDRAM address | `0x80` + addr | Send via EN pulse |
| 10 | Clear display | `0x01` | ≈ 1.53 ms execution |
| 11 | Function set (4-bit, 2-line) | `0x28` | — |
| 12 | Display ON, cursor OFF | `0x0C` | — |
| 13 | Entry mode set | `0x06` | Increment, no shift |
| 14 | Bit-rate latency per bit | $t_{bit} = 1/f_{SCL}$ | e.g., 10 µs at 100 kHz |
| 15 | Bit-baud period (SCL) | $T_{SCL} = 10\ \mu s$ (Standard) / $2.5\ \mu s$ (Fast) | per I²C spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** I²C temperature sensing with LCD readout is the cornerstone of **HVAC controllers, server-rack thermal monitors, IoT edge nodes, biomedical incubators, and industrial process chillers**. Mastering this stack gives a KTU graduate the prototype-building block for any embedded sensor-to-display pipeline they will encounter in industry.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Temperature from LM75 Raw Registers

Suppose the master reads the following two bytes from the LM75 temperature register:
- **MSB byte (first read) = `0x19`** (binary `0001 1001`)
- **LSB byte (second read) = `0x00`** (binary `0000 0000`)

The 9-bit signed value is formed by concatenating: *all 8 bits of MSB* + *the MSB (D7) of the LSB byte*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Combined 9-bit word} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,\,0 \\
&= \text{binary } 0\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,0 \\
&= 0\text{x}0\,32 = 50_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the MSB (bit 8) = 0 → positive temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\,°C} &= \text{signed integer} \times 0.5 \\
T_{\,°C} &= 50 \times 0.5 \\
T_{\,°C} &= 25.0\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check in °F:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\,°F} &= 25.0 \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 \\
&= 25.0 \times 1.8 + 32 \\
&= 45.0 + 32 \\
&= 77.0\ \text{°F}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Two's Complement for Negative Temperatures

If the LM75 returns MSB = `0xEC` and LSB = `0x80`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSB binary} &= 1\,1\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0 \\
\text{LSB MSB} &= 1 \\
\text{Combined 9-bit} &= 1\,1\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to negative via two's complement (invert + add 1, on 9 bits):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inverted} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0 \\
\text{Add 1} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1\,1 = 39_{10} \\
T_{\,°C} &= -39 \times 0.5 = -19.5\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign check:** The MSB (bit 8) of the 9-bit word is `1` → negative number.

> [!NOTE]
> In C/embedded C, simply type-casting a `signed short int` does the two's complement conversion automatically. In assembly, you must explicitly check bit 8 (bit 7 of the MSB byte) and then complement + increment.

## 3.3 Derivation: LCD Custom Character Generation (5×8 dot matrix)

To display the **degree symbol (°)** on the LCD, we must program CGRAM (Character Generator RAM) address `0x00` to `0x06` (8 bytes = one custom glyph). The encoding uses the lower 5 bits of each byte for the 5 columns of the dot matrix:

| CGRAM Byte | Hex Value | Visualized Row (5 columns) |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | `0x06` | `00110` |
| 0x01 | `0x09` | `01001` |
| 0x02 | `0x09` | `01001` |
| 0x03 | `0x06` | `00110` |
| 0x04 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x05 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x06 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x07 | `0x00` | `00000` |

The command to set CGRAM address is:
$$\text{instruction} = 0\text{x}40 + \text{CGRAM\_addr}$$

After 8 bytes are written, switch to DDRAM with `0x80 + DDRAM_addr` and then output the custom character code `0x00` to display the ° symbol.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Master Transmit vs Master Receive

| Condition | Master → Slave | Slave → Master | SDA direction during ACK pulse |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master Transmitter (Write) | Data byte | — | Slave drives SDA LOW for ACK |
| Master Transmitter (Last byte) | Data byte | — | Master leaves SDA HIGH (NACK) |
| Master Receiver (Read) | — | Data byte | Master drives SDA LOW for ACK |
| Master Receiver (Last byte) | — | Data byte | Master leaves SDA HIGH (NACK) → STOP |

## 3.5 Timing Derivation for 100 kHz Bit-Baud

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SCL} &= \frac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000}\ \text{s} = 10\ \mu\text{s} \\
T_{\text{byte}} &= 9 \times T_{SCL} = 9 \times 10\ \mu\text{s} = 90\ \mu\text{s} \\
T_{\text{address+data+stop}} &\approx 3\ \text{bytes} = 270\ \mu\text{s} \text{ per transaction}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **sensor can be polled roughly 3,700 times per second** over standard-mode I²C, which is overkill for thermal monitoring (thermal mass of any object makes >1 Hz sampling wasteful).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — System-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["Microcontroller 8051 ARM"] -->|SDA line| SDA_BUS["I2C Bus SDA with 4.7k pull up"]
    MCU -->|SCL line| SCL_BUS["I2C Bus SCL with 4.7k pull up"]
    SDA_BUS --> TEMP_SENSOR["LM75 or DS1621 Temperature Sensor slave 0x48"]
    SCL_BUS --> TEMP_SENSOR
    SDA_BUS --> LCD_BACKPACK["PCF8574 I2C LCD Backpack slave 0x27"]
    SCL_BUS --> LCD_BACKPACK
    LCD_BACKPACK -->|8 bit parallel| LCD["HD44780 16x2 LCD Module"]
    TEMP_SENSOR -->|Analog thermal| DIE["Silicon die band gap sensor"]
    MCU -->|4 bit DB4 to DB7| LCD_DIRECT["Optional direct 4 bit LCD bus"]
    MCU -->|RS EN R/W| LCD_CTRL["LCD control lines RS EN RW"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Read Temperature Transaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MASTER as Microcontroller Master
    participant BUS as I2C Bus SDA and SCL
    participant LM75 as LM75 Slave 0x48
    MASTER->>BUS: START condition SDA falls while SCL HIGH
    MASTER->>LM75: Send address byte 0x90 = 10010000 R/W=0 write
    LM75-->>MASTER: ACK pulse SDA LOW on 9th SCL
    MASTER->>LM75: Send pointer byte 0x00 = Temperature register
    LM75-->>MASTER: ACK
    MASTER->>BUS: Repeated START
    MASTER->>LM75: Send address byte 0x91 = 10010001 R/W=1 read
    LM75-->>MASTER: ACK
    LM75-->>MASTER: MSB byte = 0x19 = 25 deg C integer part
    MASTER-->>LM75: Master ACK
    LM75-->>MASTER: LSB byte = 0x00 = 0.0 deg C fractional
    MASTER-->>LM75: Master NACK to signal end
    MASTER->>BUS: STOP condition SDA rises while SCL HIGH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Firmware Read-Display Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Power on Reset"] --> B["Initialize MCU GPIO and I2C pins as open drain"]
    B --> C["Initialize LCD 4 bit mode sequence 0x03 0x03 0x03 0x02"]
    C --> D["LCD function set 0x28 Display ON 0x0C Clear 0x01"]
    D --> E["Print static label Temp C on LCD line 1"]
    E --> F["Loop start"]
    F --> G["I2C START"]
    G --> H["Send slave address 0x90 with write"]
    H --> I["Send pointer 0x00 temperature register"]
    I --> J["Repeated START"]
    J --> K["Send slave address 0x91 with read"]
    K --> L["Read MSB byte and send ACK"]
    L --> M["Read LSB byte and send NACK"]
    M --> N["I2C STOP"]
    N --> O["Combine MSB and LSB into 9 bit signed value"]
    O --> P["Convert to ASCII decimal string with 1 decimal place"]
    P --> Q["Set DDRAM cursor to line 2 column 0 using 0x80 plus 0x40"]
    Q --> R["LCD print converted string and degree custom char and C"]
    R --> F
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> POWERON
    POWERON --> WAIT15MS: Wait 15 ms after Vdd stable
    WAIT15MS --> INIT8BIT1: Send 0x03
    INIT8BIT1 --> WAIT4MS1: Wait 4.1 ms
    WAIT4MS1 --> INIT8BIT2: Send 0x03
    INIT8BIT2 --> WAIT100US: Wait 100 us
    WAIT100US --> INIT8BIT3: Send 0x03
    INIT8BIT3 --> INIT4BIT: Send 0x02 switch to 4 bit
    INIT4BIT --> FUNCSET: Send 0x28 4 bit 2 line 5x8
    FUNCSET --> DISPOFF: Send 0x08 display off
    DISPOFF --> CLEAR: Send 0x01 clear display
    CLEAR --> ENTRYMODE: Send 0x06 entry mode increment
    ENTRYMODE --> DISPON: Send 0x0C display on cursor off
    DISPON --> READY: LCD ready for user data
    READY --> READY: Process instruction or data
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Explain the I²C START and STOP conditions with a timing diagram description. Why are pull-up resistors required on the SDA and SCL lines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **START (S):** A HIGH-to-LOW transition on the **SDA** line while **SCL** is held HIGH. (1 mark)
- **STOP (P):** A LOW-to-HIGH transition on the **SDA** line while **SCL** is held HIGH. (1 mark)
- **Pull-up resistors** are required because I²C devices use **open-drain (or open-collector) outputs** — they can only pull the line LOW, never actively drive it HIGH. The pull-up to V_DD restores the HIGH level when no device is pulling LOW, implementing the **wired-AND bus** logic. (1 mark)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the ACK and NACK pulses on the I²C bus. When is each generated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ACK (Acknowledgment):** The **receiver** (slave for write, master for read) pulls the **SDA line LOW** during the **9th clock pulse** of a byte transfer, signaling that the byte was successfully received. (1.5 marks)
- **NACK (Not-Acknowledged):** The receiver leaves SDA HIGH (via the pull-up) during the 9th clock pulse. (1 mark)
- **When used:** NACK is sent by the master after the **last byte it wants to read** to terminate the read sequence, after which a STOP condition is generated. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for Module 3, the question paper offers **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. We present both alternatives below.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design of Full I²C Sensor + LCD System**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, S8 B.Tech ECE]**
**With a neat block diagram, describe the interfacing of an LM75 digital temperature sensor with an 8051 microcontroller using the I²C bus. Explain the slave address format and the steps to read the temperature register.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The 8051's general-purpose I/O pins P1.0 and P1.1 are configured as bidirectional SDA and SCL, each pulled HIGH through a **4.7 kΩ** resistor to V_DD (5 V). The LM75's SDA (pin 1) and SCL (pin 2) connect to the same bus. The LM75's A0/A1/A2 pins are tied to GND, fixing the address as `1001 000` = **0x48** (write = `0x90`, read = `0x91`). V_CC is decoupled with a 0.1 µF capacitor to GND, and the OS (overtemperature shutdown) pin can be left open or connected to an interrupt pin.

2. **Slave Address (2 marks):**
The 7-bit slave address is `1001 A2 A1 A0`. With A2 = A1 = A0 = 0, the address is `0x48`. The full transmitted byte becomes `0x90` (write) or `0x91` (read). The MSB-first transmission order is `1 0 0 1 0 0 0 R/W`.

3. **Steps to Read Temperature (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Master generates START.
   - **Step 2:** Master sends `0x90` (address + write). Slave ACKs.
   - **Step 3:** Master sends `0x00` (pointer to temperature register). Slave ACKs.
   - **Step 4:** Master generates **repeated START**.
   - **Step 5:** Master sends `0x91` (address + read). Slave ACKs.
   - **Step 6:** Master reads MSB byte → sends ACK.
   - **Step 7:** Master reads LSB byte → sends NACK.
   - **Step 8:** Master generates STOP.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the procedure to display the read temperature value (e.g., `25.5 °C`) on a 16×2 LCD interfaced in 4-bit mode. Show the DDRAM addressing and necessary LCD commands.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **LCD Initialization (2 marks):** After 15 ms power-on delay, send `0x33` (= two 0x03 nibbles in 4-bit prep), then `0x32` (= 0x03 followed by 0x02 to enter 4-bit mode), then `0x28` (function set: 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font), `0x0C` (display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF), `0x06` (entry mode: increment), and finally `0x01` (clear display).

2. **Positioning the Cursor (2 marks):**
   - To write on **Line 1**, Column 0: send `0x80 + 0x00 = 0x80`.
   - To write on **Line 2**, Column 0: send `0x80 + 0x40 = 0xC0`.
   For example, to place the temperature value at Line 2, Column 0, we issue the command `0xC0`.

3. **Sending Data (2 marks):** The ASCII value of each character is sent as two 4-bit nibbles with EN pulse between them. For "25.5 °C":
   - `'2'` = 0x32, `'5'` = 0x35, `'.'` = 0x2E, `'5'` = 0x35, `' '` = 0x20, `°` (custom char code 0x00) = 0x00, `'C'` = 0x43.

4. **Static label (1 mark):** Before the loop, print "Temp:" at Line 1, Column 0 with `0x80`. In the loop, refresh Line 2 with the latest reading.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that the HD44780 requires the **EN line to be pulsed HIGH→LOW** to latch every nibble. Failing to toggle EN will result in the LCD silently ignoring all data. Additionally, after every command (0x01, 0x02, 0x80) you **must insert a 1.5 ms delay**, not the usual 40 µs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Protocol & Register Analysis

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, S8 B.Tech EEE]**
**Describe the I²C bus specification in detail. List the electrical characteristics, addressing scheme, and the standard data transfer format. Why is I²C preferred over SPI for sensor networks?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bus Specification (2 marks):**
The I²C bus uses **two bidirectional open-drain lines, SDA and SCL**, with pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ). It supports multiple masters and multiple slaves (up to 127 devices per bus, limited by 7-bit addressing). Standard mode is 100 kbps, fast mode is 400 kbps, fast-mode+ is 1 Mbps, and high-speed mode is 3.4 Mbps.

2. **Addressing Scheme (2 marks):**
The first byte after START is the **address byte** consisting of a fixed upper 4-bit class code (e.g., `1010` for EEPROM, `1001` for LM75) and a lower 3-bit hardware-defined suffix, followed by the R/W bit. Some modern devices support 10-bit addressing to extend capacity.

3. **Data Transfer Format (2 marks):** Each byte is **clocked MSB-first** on SDA, with the receiver acknowledging on the 9th SCL cycle. A complete transaction consists of:
   - START
   - Address byte (with R/W)
   - Data byte(s), each with ACK/NACK
   - STOP

4. **Why I²C over SPI for sensor networks (1 mark):**
I²C needs only **2 wires** for any number of devices (vs. SPI's 3 + N chip-selects), supports **multi-master arbitration**, and is officially standardized across vendors. This makes it ideal for low-pin-count MCUs reading multiple sensors (e.g., temp + humidity + accelerometer).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**The DS1621 temperature sensor is read by an ARM Cortex-M0 via I²C. The master reads MSB = 0x14 and LSB = 0x80. Calculate the temperature in °C and °F. Show all bit-level conversion steps.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Combine to 12-bit two's complement value (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSB} &= 0\text{x}14 = 0001\,0100 \\
\text{LSB} &= 0\text{x}80 = 1000\,0000 \\
\text{12-bit word} &= 0001\,0100\,1000\,0000 = 0\text{x}1480
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **Interpret as signed integer (2 marks):**
MSB of 12-bit = bit 11 = 0 → positive number.

$$
\text{Integer} = 0\text{x}1480 = 1\times 16^3 + 4\times 16^2 + 8\times 16 = 4096 + 1024 + 128 = 5248
$$

3. **Apply resolution (1 mark):**
DS1621 in 12-bit mode has 0.0625 °C per LSB.

$$
T_{\,°C} = 5248 \times 0.0625 = 328.0\ \text{°C}
$$

**Wait — this is impossible** (DS1621 max = +125 °C). So actually the correct interpretation is the 9-bit upper portion:
$$
T = 0\text{x}14 = 20 \rightarrow 20 \times 0.5 = 10.0\ \text{°C}
$$

(2 marks) **Re-derivation in correct 9-bit mode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{9-bit word} &= 0001\,0100\,1 = 0\text{x}0\,0\,29 = 41 \\
T_{\,°C} &= 41 \times 0.5 = 20.5\ \text{°C} \\
T_{\,°F} &= 20.5 \times 1.8 + 32 = 36.9 + 32 = 68.9\ \text{°F}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Examiners love this!):** A common student error is to ignore the **two's complement sign bit** (bit 11 for 12-bit, bit 8 for 9-bit). If the MSB of the combined word were `1` (e.g., 0xEC 0x00), the student must perform **two's complement negation** before multiplying by resolution. Students often return a *positive* number for a *negative* temperature, costing 2–3 marks. Always: check sign bit first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **I²C uses only 2 wires (SDA + SCL)** and **open-drain drivers with pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ standard).**
- ✅ **START = SDA falls while SCL HIGH; STOP = SDA rises while SCL HIGH.** These are the only legal SDA transitions while SCL is HIGH.
- ✅ **Address byte = 7-bit slave address + 1-bit R/W.** LM75 / DS1621 default 7-bit address = `0x48`.
- ✅ **Every byte is acknowledged on the 9th SCL cycle** (ACK = SDA LOW, NACK = SDA HIGH).
- ✅ **LM75 / DS1621 return temperature as a 9-bit two's complement value**, MSB first, 0.5 °C per LSB.
- ✅ **Read sequence = START → addr(W) → pointer byte → Repeated START → addr(R) → MSB → ACK → LSB → NACK → STOP.**
- ✅ **LCD 4-bit initialization sequence** = `0x03 0x03 0x03 0x02 0x28 0x0C 0x06 0x01`.
- ✅ **DDRAM address for Line 1 = `0x00`; Line 2 = `0x40`.** Use command `0x80 + addr` to set cursor.
- ✅ **Custom degree symbol (°)** is created in CGRAM address `0x00` with 8 bytes, then displayed by sending data `0x00`.
- ✅ **Every LCD command/data write requires an EN HIGH→LOW pulse** to latch the nibble(s).
- ✅ **Always wait ≥ 1.5 ms after the Clear Display command (`0x01`)** — this is the longest HD44780 instruction.
- ✅ **Check the sign bit (bit 8 for 9-bit, bit 11 for 12-bit)** before converting raw registers to °C; otherwise negative temperatures display as huge positives.
- ✅ **SCL clock periods:** Standard = 10 µs, Fast = 2.5 µs, Fast+ = 1 µs — make sure the MCU's bit-bang delays match.
- ✅ **Repeated START (Sr) ≠ STOP+START** — Sr avoids releasing the bus, preventing another master from stealing it between transactions.
- ✅ **Multi-slave bus arbitration** is automatic in I²C; lower address bit wins contention (1 dominates 0 in wired-AND). This is why unique addresses matter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this topic:** *Draw the SDA/SCL timing diagram, label START/STOP, label ACK/NACK on the 9th pulse, show the address byte with R/W, then write the read sequence and convert the result with proper sign handling. A complete 14-mark answer will include all five.*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The I²C Bus — Operational Breakdown

The I²C bus is a **multi-master, multi-slave, synchronous, serial, open-drain** communication protocol developed by Philips Semiconductors (now NXP) in **1982**. Its physical layer uses just **two bidirectional lines**:

- **SDA (Serial Data Line):** carries the data payload.
- **SCL (Serial Clock Line):** generated by the master, paces every bit.

Both lines are tied to V_DD through **pull-up resistors (R_P)**; devices can only pull them **LOW** (wired-AND logic).

### 2.1.1 Bit-Level Rules
1. **Data is valid only when SCL = HIGH.** Transitions on SDA while SCL is HIGH are reserved for **START (S)** and **STOP (P)** conditions.
2. **START condition (S):** SDA falls (HIGH → LOW) while SCL is HIGH.
3. **STOP condition (P):** SDA rises (LOW → HIGH) while SCL is HIGH.
4. **Each byte = 8 bits**, transmitted MSB-first.
5. **The 9th clock pulse** is reserved for the **ACK** (acknowledgment) bit. The receiver pulls SDA LOW for ACK; releasing SDA (leaving HIGH via R_P) means **NACK**.

### 2.1.2 Slave Addressing
The first byte after START is the **address frame**:
$$\text{Address Byte} = \underbrace{A6\,A5\,A4\,A3\,A2\,A1\,A0}_{\text{7-bit slave address}}\;\underbrace{R/\overline{W}}_{\text{1-bit direction}}$$
- `R/W = 0` → Master **writes** to slave (Transmit mode).
- `R/W = 1` → Master **reads** from slave (Receive mode).

For LM75/DS1621, the address is hard-wired as `1001xxx` where `xxx` is the user-defined A2/A1/A0 pins.

### 2.1.3 Register Access Pattern (for DS1621 / LM75)
The temperature sensor exposes a small bank of internal registers. The typical **read-temperature** transaction is:

| Step | Master Action | Slave Response |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generate **START** | — |
| 2 | Send slave address byte (e.g., `0x90` = `1001 000 0` write) | ACK |
| 3 | Send **command/pointer byte** (`0x00` = Temperature register) | ACK |
| 4 | Generate **Repeated START** | — |
| 5 | Send slave address byte with R/W = 1 (`0x91`) | ACK |
| 6 | **Read MSB byte** from sensor | **ACK** from master |
| 7 | **Read LSB byte** from sensor | **NACK** from master |
| 8 | Generate **STOP** | — |

## 2.2 LM75 Internal Block Diagram (Logical View)
The LM75 contains:
- A **band-gap temperature sensor** (silicon-based, V_PTAT ≈ proportional-to-absolute-temperature).
- A **Σ-Δ ADC** (oversampling converter) producing a digital word.
- A **configuration register** (8 bits) controlling OS pin polarity, fault queue, shutdown, etc.
- A **temperature register** (read-only, 16-bit, only 11 bits used).
- An **I²C interface engine** with three address pins.

The analog front-end equation (silicon physics) is:
$$V_{PTAT} = \frac{kT}{q}\cdot \ln(N)\cdot K$$
where *k* is Boltzmann's constant, *q* is the electron charge, *N* is the ratio of two bipolar transistor currents, and *K* is a scale factor. The Σ-Δ modulator converts this PTAT voltage into a 9-bit (default) digital code.

## 2.3 Temperature Register Decoding

For LM75 / DS1621 (9-bit two's complement format, 0.5 °C resolution):

$$T_{°C} = \text{signed\ value\ of\ }[MSB:LSB\ upper\ 7\ bits] \times 0.5$$

For 12-bit DS1621 mode:
$$T_{°C} = \text{signed\ value\ of\ }[MSB:\text{LSB all 12 bits}] \times 0.0625$$

**Conversion to °F (for display):**
$$T_{°F} = T_{°C}\times \frac{9}{5} + 32$$

## 2.4 LCD (HD44780) Interfacing Theory

The HD44780 controller uses an **11-line interface** in 8-bit mode (reduced to **7 lines** in 4-bit mode, the KTU standard):
- **RS** — Register Select (0 = Instruction, 1 = Data).
- **R/W** — Read/Write (0 = Write, 1 = Read).
- **EN** — Enable latch pulse (HIGH → LOW on falling edge latches data).
- **D0–D7** — 8-bit data bus (or D4–D7 in 4-bit mode).

### 2.4.1 Initialization Sequence (4-bit mode, KTU standard)
Per the Hitachi datasheet, the following bytes must be issued after power-on delay (≥ 15 ms after V_DD stable):
1. `0x03` (×3 times) — function-set prep, 8-bit path.
2. `0x02` — switch to 4-bit mode.
3. `0x28` — function set: 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font.
4. `0x0C` — display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF.
5. `0x06` — entry mode: increment, no shift.
6. `0x01` — clear display.

### 2.4.2 DDRAM Address Map (16×2 LCD)

| Line | Visible Column Range | DDRAM Address Range |
|---|---|---|
| Line 1 | 0x00 – 0x0F | `0x00` – `0x0F` (0x10 – 0x27 not visible) |
| Line 2 | 0x40 – 0x4F | `0x40` – `0x4F` (0x50 – 0x67 not visible) |

To position the cursor, send instruction `0x80 + DDRAM_address`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I²C pull-up resistor sizing | $R_P = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | $\approx 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ standard |
| 2 | I²C address frame | $\text{byte} = A6 A5 A4 A3 A2 A1 A0\, R/\overline{W}$ | 8 bits total |
| 3 | LM75 default address | `1001 A2 A1 A0` | 7-bit, 0x48–0x4F |
| 4 | Temperature (9-bit mode) | $T = \text{MSB} + \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{2}$ | °C, range –55 to +125 |
| 5 | Temperature (12-bit mode) | $T = \text{MSB} + \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{256}$ | °C |
| 6 | Celsius → Fahrenheit | $T_F = T_C \times 1.8 + 32$ | °F |
| 7 | DDRAM line 1 start | `0x00` | — |
| 8 | DDRAM line 2 start | `0x40` | — |
| 9 | Set DDRAM address | `0x80` + addr | Send via EN pulse |
| 10 | Clear display | `0x01` | ≈ 1.53 ms execution |
| 11 | Function set (4-bit, 2-line) | `0x28` | — |
| 12 | Display ON, cursor OFF | `0x0C` | — |
| 13 | Entry mode set | `0x06` | Increment, no shift |
| 14 | Bit-rate latency per bit | $t_{bit} = 1/f_{SCL}$ | e.g., 10 µs at 100 kHz |
| 15 | Bit-baud period (SCL) | $T_{SCL} = 10\ \mu s$ (Standard) / $2.5\ \mu s$ (Fast) | per I²C spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** I²C temperature sensing with LCD readout is the cornerstone of **HVAC controllers, server-rack thermal monitors, IoT edge nodes, biomedical incubators, and industrial process chillers**. Mastering this stack gives a KTU graduate the prototype-building block for any embedded sensor-to-display pipeline they will encounter in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Temperature from LM75 Raw Registers

Suppose the master reads the following two bytes from the LM75 temperature register:
- **MSB byte (first read) = `0x19`** (binary `0001 1001`)
- **LSB byte (second read) = `0x00`** (binary `0000 0000`)

The 9-bit signed value is formed by concatenating: *all 8 bits of MSB* + *the MSB (D7) of the LSB byte*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Combined 9-bit word} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,\,0 \\
&= \text{binary } 0\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,0 \\
&= 0\text{x}0\,32 = 50_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the MSB (bit 8) = 0 → positive temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\,°C} &= \text{signed integer} \times 0.5 \\
T_{\,°C} &= 50 \times 0.5 \\
T_{\,°C} &= 25.0\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check in °F:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\,°F} &= 25.0 \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 \\
&= 25.0 \times 1.8 + 32 \\
&= 45.0 + 32 \\
&= 77.0\ \text{°F}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Two's Complement for Negative Temperatures

If the LM75 returns MSB = `0xEC` and LSB = `0x80`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSB binary} &= 1\,1\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0 \\
\text{LSB MSB} &= 1 \\
\text{Combined 9-bit} &= 1\,1\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to negative via two's complement (invert + add 1, on 9 bits):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inverted} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0 \\
\text{Add 1} &= 0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1\,1 = 39_{10} \\
T_{\,°C} &= -39 \times 0.5 = -19.5\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign check:** The MSB (bit 8) of the 9-bit word is `1` → negative number.

> [!NOTE]
> In C/embedded C, simply type-casting a `signed short int` does the two's complement conversion automatically. In assembly, you must explicitly check bit 8 (bit 7 of the MSB byte) and then complement + increment.

## 3.3 Derivation: LCD Custom Character Generation (5×8 dot matrix)

To display the **degree symbol (°)** on the LCD, we must program CGRAM (Character Generator RAM) address `0x00` to `0x06` (8 bytes = one custom glyph). The encoding uses the lower 5 bits of each byte for the 5 columns of the dot matrix:

| CGRAM Byte | Hex Value | Visualized Row (5 columns) |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | `0x06` | `00110` |
| 0x01 | `0x09` | `01001` |
| 0x02 | `0x09` | `01001` |
| 0x03 | `0x06` | `00110` |
| 0x04 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x05 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x06 | `0x00` | `00000` |
| 0x07 | `0x00` | `00000` |

The command to set CGRAM address is:
$$\text{instruction} = 0\text{x}40 + \text{CGRAM\_addr}$$

After 8 bytes are written, switch to DDRAM with `0x80 + DDRAM_addr` and then output the custom character code `0x00` to display the ° symbol.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Master Transmit vs Master Receive

| Condition | Master → Slave | Slave → Master | SDA direction during ACK pulse |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master Transmitter (Write) | Data byte | — | Slave drives SDA LOW for ACK |
| Master Transmitter (Last byte) | Data byte | — | Master leaves SDA HIGH (NACK) |
| Master Receiver (Read) | — | Data byte | Master drives SDA LOW for ACK |
| Master Receiver (Last byte) | — | Data byte | Master leaves SDA HIGH (NACK) → STOP |

## 3.5 Timing Derivation for 100 kHz Bit-Baud

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{SCL} &= \frac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000}\ \text{s} = 10\ \mu\text{s} \\
T_{\text{byte}} &= 9 \times T_{SCL} = 9 \times 10\ \mu\text{s} = 90\ \mu\text{s} \\
T_{\text{address+data+stop}} &\approx 3\ \text{bytes} = 270\ \mu\text{s} \text{ per transaction}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **sensor can be polled roughly 3,700 times per second** over standard-mode I²C, which is overkill for thermal monitoring (thermal mass of any object makes >1 Hz sampling wasteful).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — System-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
