For the HC-05 ↔ STM32U575 link, the standard configuration is **9600-8-N-1** (9600 bps, 8 data bits, **No** parity, **1** stop bit).

## 2.3 Baud Rate Generation Formula (KTU High-Yield)

The STM32U575's USART baud rate is derived from the peripheral clock $f_{\text{CLK}}$ via a programmable baud rate generator:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{\text{USARTDIV}}
$$

Where **USARTDIV** is stored in the **BRR (Baud Rate Register)** as a 16-bit unsigned fixed-point value:

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \text{DIV\_Mantissa} + \frac{\text{DIV\_Fraction}}{16}
$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| UART Bit Period | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| USARTDIV (oversampling ×16) | $\text{USARTDIV} = \dfrac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{16 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ | Dimensionless |
| USARTDIV (oversampling ×8) | $\text{USARTDIV} = \dfrac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{8 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ | Dimensionless |
| BRR Register Value | $\text{BRR} = 16 \times \text{DIV\_Mantissa} + \text{DIV\_Fraction}$ | Integer |
| Frame Duration (8N1) | $T_{\text{frame}} = 10 \times T_b$ | Seconds (s) |
| Throughput | $\text{Bytes/s} = \dfrac{\text{Baud Rate}}{10}$ | B/s |
| Effective Data Rate | $\eta = \dfrac{8}{10} = 80\%$ | 80% efficiency for 8N1 |
| Air Time per Byte | $T_{\text{byte}} = \dfrac{8}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| RF Carrier Frequency | $f_c \in [2402, 2480]$ MHz | 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing |
| Hop Rate | **1600 hops/sec** | FHSS |
| Class 2 TX Power | **+4 dBm** (typ.) | ~2.5 mW radiated |
| Receiver Sensitivity | **−80 dBm** (typ.) | BER = 0.1% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Warning (KTU Examiner Pitfall):** Always carry units in derivations. Mixing $f_{\text{CLK}}$ in MHz with baud rate in bps will collapse the answer. Convert $f_{\text{CLK}}$ to Hz before substitution.

## 2.4 HC-05 Operating Modes

The HC-05 operates in two mutually exclusive modes, selected by the **STATE / EN pin** logic level at power-up:

| Mode | STATE Pin | Default Baud (Data) | Default Baud (AT Cmds) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Mode** | LOW (or floating) | 9600 bps | — | Transparent UART-to-Bluetooth bridge |
| **AT Command Mode** | HIGH (3.3V at boot) | 38400 bps | 38400 bps | Configuration of name, PIN, role, etc. |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This architecture underpins **smart home controllers**, **wearable health monitors**, **industrial telemetry panels**, and **educational robotics** — anywhere a sensor-equipped MCU must report to a user's handheld device without the bulk of a Wi-Fi stack or the cost of an LCD.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating BRR for 9600 bps

> **Problem:** An STM32U575 is clocked from a **16 MHz HSI** (High-Speed Internal) source feeding the USART3 peripheral. Compute the BRR register value to achieve **9600 bps** in **oversampling-by-16** mode.

### Step 1: Identify Known Quantities

$$
f_{\text{CLK}} = 16 \text{ MHz} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad \text{Baud} = 9600 \text{ bps}, \quad \text{Oversampling} = 16
$$

### Step 2: Apply the USARTDIV Formula

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{16 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Denominator

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{153{,}600}
$$

### Step 4: Perform the Division

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = 104.1666\ldots
$$

### Step 5: Decompose into Mantissa and Fraction

$$
\text{Mantissa} = \lfloor 104.1666 \rfloor = 104, \quad \text{Fractional Part} = 0.1666\ldots
$$

The fractional component stored in BRR must be multiplied by 16 and rounded:

$$
\text{DIV\_Fraction} = 0.1666 \times 16 = 2.666 \approx 3
$$

### Step 6: Compose the BRR Register Value

$$
\text{BRR} = 16 \times \text{Mantissa} + \text{DIV\_Fraction}
$$

$$
\text{BRR} = 16 \times 104 + 3 = 1664 + 3 = 1667
$$

### Step 7: Verify the Achieved Baud Rate

$$
\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{1667} = 9598.08 \text{ bps}
$$

### Step 8: Compute the Percentage Error

$$
\text{Error} = \left \vert \frac{9598.08 - 9600}{9600} \right \vert \times 100\% = 0.02\%
$$

This is well within the **±2% tolerance** mandated by the USART peripheral specification. ✔

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the error is non-zero:** USARTDIV is stored as a *fixed-point* value. The truncation of the fractional part to the nearest 1/16 introduces a quantization error of at most one LSB, which is acceptable for moderate baud rates but can be significant at high speeds (e.g., 921600 bps with a low-frequency clock).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Time to Transmit a Sensor Packet

> **Problem:** An STM32U575 transmits a **16-byte** packet (e.g., `"T:25.50,H:60.10\r\n"`) over USART to an HC-05 at **9600 bps, 8N1**. Calculate the **total air time** consumed by the transmission.

### Step 1: Compute the Bit Period

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.1667 \text{ μs}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Bits per Frame

For **8N1** configuration (8 data, 1 start, 1 stop, no parity):

$$
N_{\text{bits/frame}} = 1 + 8 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Bits Transmitted

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 16 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte} = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Total Air Time

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{total}} \times T_b = 160 \times 104.1667 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 16{,}666.67 \text{ μs} = 16.667 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 5: Cross-Check Using Throughput

$$
\text{Bytes/s} = \frac{9600}{10} = 960 \text{ B/s}
$$

$$
T = \frac{16 \text{ B}}{960 \text{ B/s}} = 0.01667 \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common STM32U575 USART Pins for Bluetooth

| USART Instance | TX Pin (AF7) | RX Pin (AF7) | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USART1** | **PA9** | **PA10** | Debug / high-speed |
| **USART2** | **PA2** (also PD5) | **PA3** (also PD6) | HC-05 (typical) |
| **USART3** | **PD8** (also PB10, PC4) | **PD9** (also PB11, PC5) | HC-05 / sensor hubs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Compatibility Note:** The HC-05 operates at **3.3V TTL**. The STM32U575's I/O pins are also **3.3V tolerant**. **No level shifter is required** — a direct wire connection is safe. *Do not* connect 5V Arduino signals to the STM32U575 RX pin without a level divider.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Network\nTemperature, Humidity,\nAccelerometer] --> B[STM32U575 MCU\nCortex-M33 Core]
    B -- USART3 TX\n9600 bps, 8N1\n3.3V TTL --> C[HC-05 Module\nBluetooth Class 2]
    C -- 2.4 GHz RF\nSPP / RFCOMM --> D[Mobile Device\nAndroid / iOS]
    D -- Bluetooth Serial\nTerminal App --> E[User Dashboard\nTelemetry Display]
    B -- GPIO PA0 --> F[Status LED]
    B -- GPIO PB0 --> G[User Push Button]
    H[3.3V LDO Regulator] --> B
    H --> C
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style H fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.2 Detailed Hardware Wiring Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STM32U575_QFP48
        MCU_USART3_TX[PD8 - USART3_TX]
        MCU_USART3_RX[PD9 - USART3_RX]
        MCU_GPIO_STATE[PB0 - GPIO Output]
        MCU_VDD[VDD - 3.3V]
        MCU_GND[VSS - GND]
    end
    subgraph HC05_Module
        HC05_VCC[VCC - 3.6V max]
        HC05_GND[GND]
        HC05_TX[TXD - 3.3V]
        HC05_RX[RXD - 3.3V]
        HC05_STATE[STATE - Mode Select]
        HC05_EN[EN - Enable Pin]
    end
    MCU_USART3_TX -- Direct wire\nCross-coupled --> HC05_RX
    MCU_USART3_RX -- Direct wire\nCross-coupled --> HC05_TX
    MCU_VDD --> HC05_VCC
    MCU_GND --> HC05_GND
    MCU_GPIO_STATE -- 10kΩ pull-down\nor HIGH at boot --> HC05_STATE
    style STM32U575_QFP48 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style HC05_Module fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **TX ↔ RX Crossover Rule (KTU Examiner Favorite):** The MCU's **TX pin connects to the module's RX pin**, and vice versa. Failing to cross the lines is the #1 wiring mistake in viva voce examinations.

## 4.3 Software Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power-On Reset\nSTM32U575 Boot] --> B[Configure GPIO Clocks\nfor USART3 AF]
    B --> C[Set PD8 as AF7\nSet PD9 as AF7]
    C --> D[Enable USART3\nPeripheral Clock]
    D --> E[Set BRR = 1667\nfor 9600 bps at 16 MHz]
    E --> F[Enable UE, TE, RE\nin USART3 CR1]
    F --> G[Enable NVIC IRQ\nfor RXNE Interrupt]
    G --> H[HC-05 Powers Up\nLED blinks fast]
    H --> I[Mobile App Scans\nand Pairs with HC-05]
    I --> J{PIN accepted?\n1234 / 0000}
    J -- No --> K[Pairing Fails\nCheck PIN via AT mode]
    J -- Yes --> L[SPP Socket Open\nHC-05 LED steady]
    L --> M[Data Transfer\nTransparent UART]
    M --> N[STM32U575 TX buffer\nwrites to TDR]
    M --> O[USART3 ISR reads RDR\non RXNE flag]
    style A fill:#e1f5fe
    style E fill:#fff9c4
    style L fill:#c8e6c9
    style N fill:#ffccbc
    style O fill:#ffccbc
```

## 4.4 Data Packet State Machine (Custom Protocol Layer)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> HeaderReceived : Start byte 'S' detected
    HeaderReceived --> PayloadReceiving : Length byte L parsed
    PayloadReceiving --> ChecksumVerifying : L bytes received
    ChecksumVerifying --> PacketComplete : XOR checksum matches
    ChecksumVerifying --> ErrorFlag : Checksum mismatch
    PacketComplete --> Idle : Process command, ACK sent
    ErrorFlag --> Idle : Discard, NAK sent
    note right of PayloadReceiving : Add timeout\n(STM32 timer) to avoid\nstuck state on RF loss
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packet Protocol Recommendation (KTU Best Practice):** Implement a simple framed protocol for robustness — `[STX][LEN][PAYLOAD][CHK][ETX]`. Without framing, the mobile app cannot distinguish packet boundaries at higher baud rates, leading to corruption.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the SPP (Serial Port Profile) in Bluetooth communication between an STM32U575 and a mobile device. List any two AT commands used to configure the HC-05 module.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SPP Role (2 marks):** The **Serial Port Profile (SPP)** defines how to set up virtual serial ports over Bluetooth's RFCOMM protocol. It enables the HC-05 module to expose its UART as a wireless COM port, allowing the STM32U575's USART data to be transmitted transparently to a mobile application as if connected by a physical cable. SPP handles connection establishment, authentication (PIN pairing), and data flow control, abstracting the radio layer from the application.

**AT Commands (1 mark — any two):**
- `AT+NAME<name>` — sets the Bluetooth device's broadcast name.
- `AT+PSWD<password>` — changes the pairing PIN.
- `AT+UART=<baud>,<stop>,<parity>` — configures the UART parameters.
- `AT+ROLE=0` / `AT+ROLE=1` — sets the device as slave or master.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the asynchronous serial data frame format used for UART communication between STM32U575 and HC-05 at 9600-8N1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8N1 frame (one configuration of UART) consists of the following bits in sequence:
1. **Start bit** (1 bit, always LOW) — signals the receiver that a new byte is incoming.
2. **Data bits** (8 bits, LSB first) — carries the actual byte.
3. **No parity bit** — error checking is disabled.
4. **Stop bit** (1 bit, always HIGH) — marks the end of the frame and returns the line to idle.

Frame structure: `[START][D0][D1]...[D7][STOP]`, total 10 bits per byte. The idle line is held HIGH. At 9600 bps, each bit lasts $T_b = 1/9600 \approx 104.17$ μs, so a full frame occupies **1.042 ms**. The HC-05 accepts these TTL-level bits and re-encodes them into Bluetooth packets for RF transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a block diagram, describe the architecture for **bidirectional data transfer** between an STM32U575 and a mobile phone using the HC-05 Bluetooth module. Mention the role of USART, GPIO, and the mobile application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The USART3 peripheral of an STM32U575 is fed by a **16 MHz HSI clock**. Compute the value to be loaded into the BRR register to achieve a baud rate of **115200 bps** in **oversampling-by-16** mode. Also calculate the **% error** with respect to the desired baud rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture Description (7 marks):**

The bidirectional wireless data transfer system has the following blocks:

1. **STM32U575 Microcontroller (2 marks):** The Cortex-M33 based MCU hosts the application logic. It configures USART3 in **asynchronous mode, 115200 bps, 8N1**, with transmit (TE) and receive (RE) enabled. GPIO pins PD8 and PD9 are configured as **Alternate Function 7 (AF7)** to route USART3 TX and RX. A status LED on PA0 and a user button on PB0 provide local feedback.

2. **HC-05 Bluetooth Module (2 marks):** The HC-05 acts as a **wireless UART bridge**. It accepts 3.3V TTL serial data on its RXD pin, packetizes it using the Bluetooth 2.0 + EDR stack, and broadcasts over the 2.4 GHz band. On reception, it decodes incoming RF packets and re-emits them as serial bits on its TXD pin. Default slave role allows mobile-initiated pairing.

3. **Mobile Application (2 marks):** An Android app (e.g., *Serial Bluetooth Terminal*) uses the platform's Bluetooth API to scan, pair (PIN: 1234), and open an **SPP RFCOMM socket**. Data typed or received is displayed in a text console and sent verbatim to the HC-05. The application may optionally parse framed packets from the MCU for sensor visualization.

4. **Wiring and Flow (1 mark):** MCU PD8 (TX) → HC-05 RXD; MCU PD9 (RX) → HC-05 TXD; shared GND; 3.3V to HC-05 VCC. A voltage divider or logic level translator is unnecessary since both operate at 3.3V.

**(b) BRR Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the USARTDIV formula:**

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{16 \times \text{Baud Rate}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 115200}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{1{,}843{,}200} = 8.6805\ldots
$$

**Step 3 — Extract Mantissa and Fraction:**

$$
\text{Mantissa} = 8, \quad \text{Fraction} = 0.6805
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Fraction to 1/16 units:**

$$
\text{DIV\_Fraction} = 0.6805 \times 16 = 10.888 \approx 11
$$

**Step 5 — Compose the BRR Register:**

$$
\text{BRR} = 16 \times 8 + 11 = 128 + 11 = \mathbf{139}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Achieved Baud Rate:**

$$
\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{139} = 115{,}107.91 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the % Error:**

$$
\text{Error}\% = \left \vert \frac{115{,}107.91 - 115{,}200}{115{,}200} \right \vert \times 100 = 0.08\%
$$

This is well within the **±2% tolerance** of the USART specification. ✔

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **two operating modes of the HC-05** (Data Mode and AT Command Mode). Describe a procedure to change the device name to *KTU_BT* and the PIN to *5678*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of using Bluetooth (HC-05) for IoT applications compared to **Wi-Fi (ESP8266)**. Mention two real-world use cases where Bluetooth is the preferred choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) HC-05 Modes and Configuration (7 marks):**

**Two Operating Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Data Mode** | Default mode after power-up. HC-05 acts as a transparent wireless UART bridge. The STATE pin is LOW. Default baud is 9600 bps. |
| **AT Command Mode** | Configuration mode for modifying module parameters. STATE pin must be **HIGH (3.3V) at power-up**. Default baud is 38400 bps. Module responds with `OK` to valid commands. |

**Procedure to Rename to *KTU_BT* and Set PIN to *5678* (4 marks):**

1. **Power off** the HC-05. Press and hold the small push-button on the module (or pull STATE HIGH externally).
2. **Power on** while holding the button. The LED will blink **slowly** (approximately every 2 seconds) — this confirms **AT mode** entry.
3. Open a serial terminal (e.g., Tera Term on PC, or STM32 USART) at **38400 bps, 8N1**.
4. Send the following AT commands (each followed by `\r\n`):
   - `AT` → response `OK` (sanity check)
   - `AT+NAME=KTU_BT` → response `OK`
   - `AT+PSWD=5678` → response `OK`
5. Power cycle the module. On next boot, the device will broadcast as `KTU_BT` and require PIN `5678` for pairing.

**(b) Bluetooth vs Wi-Fi for IoT (7 marks):**

**Advantages of Bluetooth (HC-05) (2 marks):**
- **Low power consumption** (~30 mA active, <1 mA in sniff mode) — ideal for battery-powered sensors.
- **Simpler integration** — no TCP/IP stack or router configuration; direct point-to-point link.
- **Lower cost** — HC-05 modules cost ~$3–5 vs ESP8266 ~$2–4 with extra complexity.
- **Built-in security** — frequency hopping and PIN-based pairing.

**Limitations of Bluetooth (HC-05) (2 marks):**
- **Short range** — Class 2 limited to ~10 m; insufficient for whole-home or outdoor coverage.
- **Lower bandwidth** — up to 3 Mbps (BT 2.0) vs 54 Mbps (Wi-Fi g) and beyond.
- **Point-to-point topology** — only one master can connect at a time (no easy mesh).
- **No native Internet** — cannot directly reach cloud services without a gateway.

**Preferred Use Cases (3 marks):**
1. **Wearable Health Monitors** — A heart-rate sensor on a wristband reports data to a smartphone app via Bluetooth. Low power is critical since the device runs on a coin cell for weeks.
2. **Industrial Remote Control Panels** — A handheld maintenance tablet pairs with a PLC over Bluetooth to upload firmware or read diagnostics in environments where Wi-Fi congestion is high.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Wiring Reversal:** Forgetting to cross TX and RX between MCU and HC-05 — silent failure, no data on terminal.
> - **Baud Rate Mismatch:** Using 38400 in data mode and 9600 in AT mode; students often swap these.
> - **STATE Pin Logic:** Failing to hold STATE HIGH at power-up to enter AT mode — module boots into data mode regardless of button press timing.
> - **Voltage Levels:** Connecting 5V Arduino RX to STM32U575 input — destroys the I/O pin. Always verify 3.3V compatibility.
> - **Missing Termination Resistors:** Long jumper wires (>20 cm) can cause reflections at 115200 bps; keep wires short or use shielded cables.
> - **Forgetting the BRR Formula in derivations:** Examiners award zero if you do not show $f_{\text{CLK}}$ and oversampling factor explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Bluetooth ↔ STM32U575**

- ✅ **Bluetooth** operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** (2.400 – 2.4835 GHz), with **79 channels** spaced **1 MHz** apart and **1600 hops/sec**.
- ✅ The **STM32U575** does **not** contain an internal Bluetooth radio — it interfaces with an **external module (HC-05/HC-06)** via **UART/USART**.
- ✅ **HC-05 default settings:** Slave mode, name `HC-05`, PIN `1234`, data mode baud **9600 bps**, AT mode baud **38400 bps**.
- ✅ **Voltage compatibility:** STM32U575 I/O = **3.3V**, HC-05 logic = **3.3V** → **no level shifter needed**.
- ✅ **Wiring rule:** MCU **TX → Module RX**, MCU **RX → Module TX** (always crossed).
- ✅ **BRR Formula:** $\text{USARTDIV} = f_{\text{CLK}} / (16 \times \text{Baud})$. For **16 MHz @ 9600 bps**, BRR = **1667** (0.02% error).
- ✅ **8N1 frame = 10 bits per byte**, yielding **80% data efficiency** (8 payload bits / 10 total).
- ✅ **Mode selection on HC-05:** STATE pin **LOW** at boot = Data Mode; **HIGH** at boot = AT Command Mode.
- ✅ **Mobile app protocols:** Use **SPP / RFCOMM** socket; install apps like *Serial Bluetooth Terminal* (Android) or *LightBlue* (iOS).
- ✅ **Robust packet design:** Always use a framed protocol (`STX-LEN-PAYLOAD-CHK-ETX`) for error detection over RF.
- ✅ **Range:** Class 2 modules ≈ **10 m line-of-sight**; obstacles (walls, water) attenuate significantly.
- ✅ **Interrupt-driven RX** is preferred over polling — use the **RXNE flag** in the USART ISR to offload CPU.
- ✅ **KTU favorite questions:** BRR calculation (always expect a derivation), pin mapping (TX/RX crossover), AT command listing, and protocol layer description.
- ✅ **Common pitfall to avoid:** Never connect HC-05 VCC to **5V** — the absolute maximum is **3.6V**, and 5V will damage the on-board voltage regulator.
- ✅ **Future-proofing:** Consider upgrading to **HM-10** (Bluetooth 4.0 BLE) for ultra-low-power IoT applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS Concepts: FreeRTOS Overview, Task Creation, Scheduling, Timers, and Inter-task Communication (Queues and Semaphores)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RTOS Concepts: FreeRTOS Overview, Task Creation, Scheduling, Timers, and Inter-task Communication (Queues and Semaphores)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an RTOS? (Formal Definition)

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is a specialized operating system designed to serve real-time applications that process data as it comes in, typically without buffer delays. The correctness of a real-time system depends not only on the **logical result** of computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> An RTOS is an operating system intended to serve real-time process requests. It must guarantee response within strict timing constraints (called **deadlines**) — typically in the order of milliseconds or microseconds.

FreeRTOS specifically is a **class of RTOS** that is designed to be small enough to run on a microcontroller — though its use is not limited to microcontroller applications. FreeRTOS is **open source**, distributed under the MIT License, and is the de-facto standard RTOS for embedded microcontrollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Real-Time vs Soft Real-Time:**
> - **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline is a **system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> - **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline degrades performance but is **not catastrophic** (e.g., video streaming).
> - **FreeRTOS** is a *soft* real-time kernel that can be configured with deterministic scheduling to approach hard real-time behavior on resource-constrained MCUs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Master Chef in a Tiny Kitchen 🍳

Imagine a single chef (the **CPU**) working in a tiny kitchen (the **microcontroller**) with only one stove (the **execution core**). However, there are 10 different dishes on order (these are **tasks**). The chef cannot cook all of them simultaneously. So the chef:

1. **Plans** an order of cooking — this is **scheduling**.
2. **Switches** between dishes quickly — chopping onions for dish A, then stirring soup for dish B, then plating dish C.
3. **Remembers** that dish A's sauce is still simmering on the back burner — this is a **timer**.
4. **Writes** a note "Sauce ready, serve with pasta" and places it in a shared tray so the waiter (another task) can pick it up — this is a **queue**.
5. Ensures that only one chef uses the salt shaker at a time — this is a **semaphore (mutex)**.

FreeRTOS is precisely that **master chef's playbook** — a tiny, deterministic kernel that lets multiple "dishes" (tasks) share the single "stove" (CPU) in a fair, predictable, and time-bound manner.

## 1.3 Why FreeRTOS for KTU Microcontrollers?

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Footprint | As low as **4 KB** ROM, **1 KB** RAM |
| License | **MIT License** (free for commercial use) |
| Supported MCUs | ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, MSP430, RISC-V, x86 |
| Kernel Type | Preemptive priority-based, optionally cooperative |
| Minimum Tick Rate | Configurable, typically **1 ms (1000 Hz)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Engineering Metric:**
> The **tick rate (configTICK_RATE_HZ)** is the heartbeat of the OS. A 1 kHz tick rate means the scheduler is invoked every **1 ms** to decide which task runs next. Lower tick rates reduce overhead but increase maximum scheduling latency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FreeRTOS Kernel Architecture

FreeRTOS is a **microkernel**, meaning the kernel itself provides only essential services:
- **Task management** (creation, scheduling, deletion)
- **Time management** (timers, delays, tick hooks)
- **Inter-task communication** (queues, semaphores, mutexes, event groups)
- **Memory management** (heap_1 through heap_5 schemes)

Everything else (drivers, networking, filesystems) is left to user code or third-party libraries.

### The Three Core Building Blocks

1. **Tasks** — Independent threads of execution, each with its own stack.
2. **Scheduler** — Decides which task runs on the CPU at any given moment.
3. **IPC Primitives** — Queues, semaphores, mutexes for safe data exchange.

## 2.2 Task States — The Heart of RTOS

A FreeRTOS task can be in one of four states:

| State | Description | CPU Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Running** | Currently executing on the CPU | Yes (1 task only) |
| **Ready** | Eligible to run, waiting for CPU | No, but highest priority |
| **Blocked** | Waiting for an event (delay, queue, semaphore) | No |
| **Suspended** | Explicitly suspended via `vTaskSuspend()` | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Blocked" is different from "Suspended":**
> Blocked tasks have a **timeout** (e.g., "wait 100 ms for data"). Suspended tasks have **no timeout** — they remain suspended until explicitly resumed by another task. This distinction is a high-yield KTU concept.

## 2.3 Task Creation — The Anatomy of `xTaskCreate()`

```c
BaseType_t xTaskCreate(
    TaskFunction_t pvTaskCode,     // Function pointer to the task
    const char * const pcName,     // Human-readable name (debug only)
    configSTACK_DEPTH_TYPE usStackDepth, // Stack size in words
    void *pvParameters,            // Argument passed to task
    UBaseType_t uxPriority,        // 0 = lowest; higher = higher priority
    TaskHandle_t *pxCreatedTask    // Optional handle to control task
);
```

### Step-by-Step Task Lifecycle Logic

1. **Memory Allocation** — Allocates TCB (Task Control Block) and stack from the FreeRTOS heap.
2. **TCB Initialization** — Populates TCB with priority, stack pointer (PSP), state = Ready.
3. **Stack Initialization** — Pre-fills the stack with a known pattern for stack overflow detection.
4. **List Insertion** — Adds the task to the **Ready List** at the position corresponding to its priority.
5. **Scheduler Notification** — If `xTaskCreate()` was called before the scheduler started, the task simply waits. If called *after* the scheduler is running, a yield is forced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Overflow Detection in FreeRTOS:**
> FreeRTOS provides two methods:
> - **Method 1:** Check the last valid stack byte using a known pattern (faster, less reliable).
> - **Method 2:** Fill the entire stack with `0xa5` and check if the high-water mark exceeds the stack size (slower, more reliable).

## 2.4 Scheduling Algorithms in FreeRTOS

FreeRTOS supports three scheduling modes:

### Mode 1: Preemptive Priority-Based Scheduling (Default)
The highest-priority Ready task **always** runs. When a higher-priority task becomes Ready (e.g., unblocked by ISR), the current task is **immediately preempted**.

### Mode 2: Time-Slicing (Round-Robin Among Equal Priorities)
Tasks of **the same priority** share CPU time using time slices. The default time slice equals the tick period (`1 / configTICK_RATE_HZ`).

### Mode 3: Cooperative Scheduling
Tasks voluntarily yield via `taskYIELD()`. Preemption is disabled by setting `configUSE_PREEMPTION = 0`.

### Configuration Macros (High-Yield for KTU)

| Macro | Function | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `configUSE_PREEMPTION` | Enable preemptive scheduling | 1 |
| `configUSE_TIME_SLICING` | Enable round-robin among equal priorities | 1 |
| `configMAX_PRIORITIES` | Number of distinct priority levels | 5 |
| `configTICK_RATE_HZ` | Scheduler tick frequency | 1000 Hz |
| `configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE` | Minimum stack for idle task | 128 words |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following mathematical relationships govern RTOS behavior. These are **board-exam favorite** derivations.

$$
f_{\text{tick}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{tick}}}
$$

$$
\text{Max Scheduling Latency} = T_{\text{tick}} + \text{Context Switch Time}
$$

$$
\text{CPU Utilization} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}}{1}
$$

$$
\text{Schedulability Test (RMS):} \quad U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

$$
\text{Task Stack Size} \geq \text{Nested Call Depth} \times \text{Max Frame Size} + \text{Context Size}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tick Frequency | $f_{\text{tick}}$ | Hz (Hertz) | Scheduler interrupt rate |
| Tick Period | $T_{\text{tick}}$ | s (seconds) | Time between two scheduler ticks |
| Task Computation Time | $C_i$ | s (seconds) | Worst-case execution time of task *i* |
| Task Period | $T_i$ | s (seconds) | Period at which task *i* is released |
| Context Switch Time | $T_{cs}$ | s (seconds) | Time to save/restore registers |
| CPU Utilization | $U$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of CPU used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schedulability Bound (Liu & Layland):**
> For *n* periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), the system is **guaranteed** to be schedulable if:
> $$U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$
> For $n \to \infty$, this bound converges to $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ (**69.3 %**). This is a theorem KTU students must memorize.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive ECUs:** ABS, airbag, engine control — all use hard real-time kernels.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers, infusion pumps — FreeRTOS is FDA-cleared in many products.
- **IoT Edge Nodes:** Sensor fusion and wireless stacks on Cortex-M0+ devices.
- **Aerospace:** NASA's CFS (Core Flight System) is built on FreeRTOS.
- **Industrial Robotics:** PLCs, CNC machines, and motion controllers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Worst-Case Scheduling Latency for FreeRTOS

**Given:**
- Tick rate $f_{\text{tick}} = 1 \text{ kHz} \Rightarrow T_{\text{tick}} = 1 \text{ ms}$
- Context switch time $T_{cs} = 5 \mu\text{s}$ (typical for Cortex-M4 at 168 MHz)

**Step 1:** Convert tick period to seconds.

$$
T_{\text{tick}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2:** The worst-case latency occurs when a high-priority event arrives **just after** a tick has been processed. The system must wait for the next tick.

$$
T_{\text{worst-latency}} = T_{\text{tick}} + T_{cs}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
T_{\text{worst-latency}} = 1 \text{ ms} + 0.005 \text{ ms} = 1.005 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** With a 1 kHz tick and 5 µs context switch, the worst-case scheduling latency is approximately **1.005 ms**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Schedulability Test for 3 Periodic Tasks

**Task Set (Rate Monotonic Priority Assignment):**

| Task | $C_i$ (Computation) | $T_i$ (Period) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 ms | 4 ms |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 ms | 6 ms |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 ms | 12 ms |

**Step 1:** Compute the utilization of each task.

$$
U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333
$$

$$
U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2:** Sum to get total utilization.

$$
U_{\text{total}} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.25 = 0.833
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Liu & Layland's bound for $n = 3$.

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$
U_{\text{total}} = 0.833 \; > \; U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7797
$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

> [!WARNING]
> The utilization exceeds the bound, so **schedulability is NOT guaranteed** by the Liu & Layland sufficient condition. However, this is only a **sufficient** condition (not necessary). An exact response-time analysis must be performed to confirm schedulability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Response Time Analysis (RTA) for the Same Task Set

**Step 1:** Response time recurrence for $\tau_i$:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 2:** For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no interference):

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** For $\tau_2$:

$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(fixed point)}
$$

$$
R_2 = 3 \text{ ms} < T_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** For $\tau_3$:

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(fixed point)}
$$

$$
R_3 = 10 \text{ ms} < T_3 = 12 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set **is schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, even though the sufficient condition failed.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: FreeRTOS IPC Primitive Comparison

| Primitive | Purpose | Block on Empty/Full? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Queue** | Pass data between tasks | Yes (configurable timeout) | Producer-Consumer, command passing |
| **Binary Semaphore** | Signal an event | Yes (waits for "give") | ISR-to-task notification |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Count resources | Yes (waits for count > 0) | Resource pool management |
| **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion | Yes (with priority inheritance) | Protecting shared variables |
| **Event Group** | Multi-bit synchronization | Yes (any/all bits) | Waiting on multiple conditions |
| **Stream Buffer** | Byte-stream with triggering | Yes | UART pipelines, file IO |
| **Message Buffer** | Variable-size messages | Yes | Inter-task log messages |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Using a Queue to Pass Data Between Two Tasks

**Step 1:** Create the queue in main().

```c
QueueHandle_t xSensorQueue = xQueueCreate(10, sizeof(float));
```

**Step 2:** Producer task sends data.

```c
void vProducerTask(void *pvParam) {
    float reading = 0.0f;
    while (1) {
        reading = read_sensor();
        if (xQueueSend(xSensorQueue, &reading, pdMS_TO_TICKS(100)) != pdPASS) {
            // Queue was full — log error
        }
        vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(50));
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Consumer task receives data.

```c
void vConsumerTask(void *pvParam) {
    float value = 0.0f;
    while (1) {
        if (xQueueReceive(xSensorQueue, &value, portMAX_DELAY) == pdTRUE) {
            process(value);
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 4:** The semantics:
- `xQueueSend()` blocks (or times out) if the queue is full.
- `xQueueReceive()` blocks (or times out) if the queue is empty.
- `portMAX_DELAY` means "wait forever."
- `pdMS_TO_TICKS()` converts milliseconds to ticks (critical for portability across tick rates).

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Using a Counting Semaphore for ISR-Task Synchronization

**Step 1:** Create semaphore (initial count = 0, max = 10).

```c
SemaphoreHandle_t xButtonSem = xSemaphoreCreateCounting(10, 0);
```

**Step 2:** ISR "gives" the semaphore.

```c
void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void) {
    BaseType_t xHigherPriorityTaskWoken = pdFALSE;
    xSemaphoreGiveFromISR(xButtonSem, &xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);
    portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xHigherPriorityTaskWoken);
}
```

**Step 3:** Task "takes" the semaphore.

```c
void vButtonHandlerTask(void *pvParam) {
    while (1) {
        if (xSemaphoreTake(xButtonSem, portMAX_DELAY) == pdTRUE) {
            handle_button_press();
        }
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU-Favorite Question):**
> Use `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` inside an ISR — **not** the regular `xSemaphoreGive()`. The `*FromISR` variants are designed to be ISR-safe and include yield-on-exit logic.

## 3.7 Software Timers in FreeRTOS

FreeRTOS timers are **daemon-task based**, not hardware. A single timer service task (`Tmr Svc`) manages all software timers.

### Key API Functions

```c
TimerHandle_t xTimerCreate(
    const char *pcTimerName,
    TickType_t xTimerPeriodInTicks,
    UBaseType_t uxAutoReload,
    void *pvTimerID,
    TimerCallbackFunction_t pxCallbackFunction
);
```

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `xTimerStart()` | Start a timer |
| `xTimerStop()` | Stop a timer |
| `xTimerChangePeriod()` | Change timer's period |
| `xTimerReset()` | Reset the timer's count to zero |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Shot vs Auto-Reload:**
> - `pdFALSE` (one-shot): Timer fires **once** after the period and stops.
> - `pdTRUE` (auto-reload): Timer fires **periodically** until explicitly stopped.

**Real-World Example:** Reading a temperature sensor every 5 seconds. Create an auto-reload timer with a 5000-tick period (at 1 ms tick). The callback re-reads the sensor.

## 3.8 Priority Inversion Problem and Inheritance (Bonus Insight)

**Scenario:** Low-priority task L holds a mutex. High-priority task H tries to take the same mutex but blocks. Medium-priority task M preempts L, preventing L from releasing the mutex. H stays blocked indefinitely — this is **priority inversion**.

**Solution:** FreeRTOS mutexes implement **priority inheritance** — when H blocks on a mutex held by L, L is temporarily **promoted** to H's priority. L runs, releases the mutex, and reverts.

> [!WARNING]
> Use **Mutexes** (not binary semaphores) for mutual exclusion. Mutexes support priority inheritance; binary semaphores do not. This is a classic KTU exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FreeRTOS Kernel Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application Task 1] --> kernel[FreeRTOS Kernel Core]
    app2[Application Task 2] --> kernel
    app3[ISR Service Routines] --> kernel
    kernel --> sched[Scheduler]
    kernel --> timer[Timer Service Task]
    kernel --> queue[Queue Manager]
    kernel --> sema[Semaphore and Mutex Manager]
    kernel --> mem[Memory Manager Heap N]
    sched --> tick[SysTick Interrupt configTICK RATE HZ]
    timer --> prioQ[Priority Sorted Lists]
    queue --> prioQ
    sema --> prioQ
```

## 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Ready: xTaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempted or Time Slice Expired
    Running --> Blocked: Delay or Wait on Queue or Semaphore
    Blocked --> Ready: Timeout or Event Occurs
    Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend
    Ready --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume
    Running --> [*: vTaskDelete
    Blocked --> [*
    Ready --> [*
    Suspended --> [*
```

## 4.3 Preemptive Priority Scheduling Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[SysTick Interrupt Fires] --> save[Save Current Task Context to PSP]
    save --> scan[Scan Ready List for Highest Priority Task]
    scan --> check{Highest Priority\nDiffers from\nCurrent?}
    check --> yes[Load New Task Context]
    check --> no[Restore Current Task Context]
    yes --> run[Return to Thread Mode\nNew Task Resumes]
    no --> run2[Return to Thread Mode\nCurrent Task Continues]
    run --> end([End of ISR])
    run2 --> end
    save --> end
```

## 4.4 Queue Producer-Consumer Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Producer Task] -->|xQueueSend| qbox[FreeRTOS Queue\nCapacity 10 Items\nItem Size 4 bytes]
    qbox -->|xQueueReceive| c1[Consumer Task]
    qbox -->|Full blocks here| p1
    qbox -->|Empty blocks here| c1
    isr1[ISR] -.->|xQueueSendFromISR| qbox
    qbox -.->|xQueueReceiveFromISR| isr1
```

## 4.5 Semaphore Operations — Binary and Counting

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    task1[Task A Priority 3] -->|xSemaphoreTake| sem{Resource Available?}
    sem -->|Yes| use[Critical Section Use Resource]
    sem -->|No| block[Task Blocked in Semaphore Wait List]
    block -->|On Give| ready[Task Moves to Ready List]
    ready --> use
    use -->|xSemaphoreGive| sem
    isr[ISR] -.->|xSemaphoreGiveFromISR| sem
    sem -.->|Wake Higher Priority| yield[portYIELD FROM ISR]
```

## 4.6 Software Timer Lifecycle (Daemon Task)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t1[xTimerCreate] --> t2[Timer in Dormant State]
    t2 --> t3[xTimerStart]
    t3 --> t4[Active Counting Down]
    t4 --> t5{Period Elapsed?}
    t5 -->|Auto Reload pdTRUE| t6[Callback Executes in Tmr Svc]
    t6 --> t4
    t5 -->|One Shot pdFALSE| t7[Timer Goes Dormant]
    t6 --> t7
    t7 --> t2
    t4 --> t8[xTimerStop]
    t8 --> t2
```

## 4.7 Inter-Task Communication Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Application
        T1[Sensor Task P 2] 
        T2[Control Task P 3]
        T3[Display Task P 1]
        T4[Comm Task P 2]
    end
    subgraph IPC
        Q1[Sensor Data Queue]
        Q2[Command Queue]
        M1[UART Mutex]
        E1[System Event Group]
    end
    T1 -->|xQueueSend| Q1
    Q1 -->|xQueueReceive| T2
    T2 -->|xQueueSend| Q2
    Q2 -->|xQueueReceive| T3
    T4 <-->|xSemaphoreTake Give| M1
    T1 -->|xEventGroupSetBits| E1
    E1 -->|xEventGroupWaitBits| T2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a hard real-time system and a soft real-time system. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hard real-time system** must meet its deadlines under all conditions; failure to do so is considered a **system failure** with potentially catastrophic consequences. Example: **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** in an automobile — a missed deadline could result in loss of life.

A **soft real-time system** will attempt to meet deadlines, but the utility of the result degrades if the deadline is missed. Example: **video streaming on a smartphone** — frame drops cause stutter but the system continues to function.

| Attribute | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline | Must be met | Best-effort |
| Failure impact | Catastrophic | Degraded performance |
| Example | Airbag, ABS | Audio/video playback |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the role of the idle task in FreeRTOS? Can it be deleted?**

**Model Answer:**

The **idle task** is automatically created by the kernel when `vTaskStartScheduler()` is called. It runs at the **lowest priority (priority 0)** and executes whenever no other task is Ready. Its primary roles are:

1. **CPU housekeeping** — freeing memory of deleted tasks (via the optional idle hook).
2. **Power saving** — the idle hook can place the MCU into a low-power sleep mode (e.g., `__WFI()` on ARM).

The idle task **cannot be deleted** by the user. However, it can be customized via the **idle hook function** (`vApplicationIdleHook()`). The idle hook is invoked once per iteration of the idle task loop.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different states a FreeRTOS task can be in, with a neat state transition diagram. How does a task move from the Ready state to the Blocked state?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the FreeRTOS preemptive priority-based scheduler with a flowchart. What is the role of `configMAX_PRIORITIES` and `configTICK_RATE_HZ`?

#### Model Solution for (a):

A FreeRTOS task can exist in one of **four states**:

1. **Running** — Currently executing on the CPU. Exactly one task is in this state.
2. **Ready** — Eligible to run but not currently using the CPU. Waiting in the Ready List.
3. **Blocked** — Waiting for a temporal or external event (delay expiration, queue data, semaphore release).
4. **Suspended** — Inactive and excluded from scheduling until explicitly resumed.

**State Transition Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler picks
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked: vTaskDelay or xQueueReceive or xSemaphoreTake
    Blocked --> Ready: Delay expires or event occurs
    Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume
    Running --> [*: vTaskDelete
```

A task moves from **Ready to Blocked** in the following scenarios:
- Calling `vTaskDelay(ticks)` — blocks for *ticks* scheduler ticks.
- Calling `xQueueReceive()` on an empty queue.
- Calling `xSemaphoreTake()` on an unavailable semaphore.
- Calling `xTaskNotifyWait()` to wait for a notification.

When the blocking event occurs (timeout elapsed, data arrived, semaphore given), the kernel moves the task from the **Blocked List** back to the **Ready List** at the position corresponding to its priority.

#### Model Solution for (b):

FreeRTOS uses a **preemptive priority-based scheduler** with optional time-slicing. At every SysTick interrupt, the scheduler:

1. Saves the current task's context (registers, PSP) onto its stack.
2. Increments the system tick counter (`xTickCount`).
3. Checks if any Blocked tasks have unblocked due to the tick.
4. Selects the **highest-priority Ready task** to run next.
5. If the selected task differs from the current one, performs a **context switch**.

**Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SysTick Fires] --> B[Save Current Context]
    B --> C[Increment Tick Count]
    C --> D[Move Expired Delayed Tasks to Ready]
    D --> E{Highest Priority\nReady Task\nSame as Current?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Restore Current Context]
    E -->|No| G[Load New Task Context]
    F --> H[Return from ISR]
    G --> H
```

**Role of `configMAX_PRIORITIES`:**
- Defines the **maximum number of distinct priority levels** the application can use.
- Typical values: 5, 7, 32. Set in `FreeRTOSConfig.h`.
- Higher values allow finer priority granularity but increase memory usage (each priority has its own Ready List).

**Role of `configTICK_RATE_HZ`:**
- Defines the **frequency** of the SysTick interrupt (in Hz).
- Common value: `1000` (1 ms tick) for general use, `10000` (100 µs tick) for high-rate control loops.
- **Trade-off:** Higher tick rate → lower scheduling latency but more CPU overhead (typically 1–5 %).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of inter-task communication in FreeRTOS. Compare Queues, Semaphores, and Mutexes with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write short notes on FreeRTOS software timers. Explain the difference between one-shot and auto-reload timers with code examples.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** in FreeRTOS allows tasks and ISRs to share data and synchronize execution. The kernel provides several primitives:

**1. Queues**
- Used to **pass data** between tasks (e.g., sensor readings, commands).
- FIFO structure, each item is a copy of the data passed.
- Blocking behavior on send (if full) and receive (if empty).

**Example Use Case:** A temperature sensor task produces readings; an LCD task displays them. The queue ensures thread-safe handoff.

**2. Semaphores (Binary and Counting)**
- **Binary Semaphore:** Used for **signaling** (e.g., ISR-to-task notification). Has a count of 0 or 1.
- **Counting Semaphore:** Used to manage a **pool of identical resources** (e.g., 5 UART peripherals). Count can be 0 to *N*.

**Example Use Case:** A button press ISR gives a binary semaphore; a debounce task takes it.

**3. Mutexes (Mutual Exclusion Semaphores)**
- Used to **protect shared resources** (variables, peripherals) from concurrent access.
- Include **priority inheritance** to prevent priority inversion.

**Example Use Case:** Protecting a global buffer accessed by two tasks (producer and consumer).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Queue | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore | Mutex |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carries data? | Yes (copy by value) | No | No | No |
| Max count | Queue length | 1 | N | 1 |
| Priority inheritance | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Typical use | Data passing | Event signaling | Resource pool | Critical section |
| Take blocks? | Yes (empty) | Yes (count = 0) | Yes (count = 0) | Yes (locked) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake is using a **binary semaphore** where a **mutex** is required. The result is unbounded **priority inversion** in systems with three or more priority levels. Always choose **mutexes** for mutual exclusion and **binary semaphores** for one-way event signaling.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**FreeRTOS Software Timers** are timer objects managed by a dedicated **Timer Service (Daemon) Task** (`Tmr Svc`). They are not hardware timers; they are scheduled by the kernel using the tick interrupt.

**Key Characteristics:**
- All timer callbacks execute in the context of the **Tmr Svc task**, not the calling task.
- Timers are **lightweight** — many timers can be created with minimal RAM.
- Timers are **daemon-task based**, meaning the callback must not block (use `xQueueReceive()` with non-zero timeout, not `portMAX_DELAY`).

**One-Shot Timer:**

```c
TimerHandle_t xOneShot = xTimerCreate(
    "OneShot",                  // Name
    pdMS_TO_TICKS(2000),        // 2-second period
    pdFALSE,                    // One-shot (no auto-reload)
    0,                          // Timer ID
    vOneShotCallback            // Callback
);
xTimerStart(xOneShot, 0);
```

Behavior: After starting, the timer waits 2 seconds, calls `vOneShotCallback` **once**, and then becomes **dormant**. It will not fire again until restarted.

**Auto-Reload Timer:**

```c
TimerHandle_t xPeriodic = xTimerCreate(
    "Periodic",
    pdMS_TO_TICKS(5000),        // 5-second period
    pdTRUE,                     // Auto-reload enabled
    0,
    vPeriodicCallback
);
xTimerStart(xPeriodic, 0);
```

Behavior: After starting, the timer fires every 5 seconds, calling `vPeriodicCallback` periodically until explicitly stopped via `xTimerStop()`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Attribute | One-Shot (`pdFALSE`) | Auto-Reload (`pdTRUE`) |
|---|---|---|
| Fires once? | Yes | No — fires repeatedly |
| Returns to dormant? | Yes, after first fire | No, remains active |
| Typical use | Timeout, single delay | Periodic sampling, blink LED |
| Change period runtime? | Yes, via `xTimerChangePeriod()` | Yes, via `xTimerChangePeriod()` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Do not call blocking APIs like `vTaskDelay()` or `xQueueReceive(portMAX_DELAY)` inside a timer callback. The timer callback runs in the **Tmr Svc task** context. Blocking it will stall **all** software timers in the system.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: RTOS Concepts**

- [x] An **RTOS** must respond to events within strict timing constraints. FreeRTOS is a **soft real-time** kernel that is configurable, MIT-licensed, and uses minimal memory.
- [x] A FreeRTOS task has **four states**: Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended. The kernel transitions tasks using `xTaskCreate()`, `vTaskSuspend()`, `vTaskResume()`, and blocking APIs.
- [x] **Task creation** allocates a TCB and a stack; stack overflow is detected via pattern-checking (0xa5 fill or last-byte sentinel).
- [x] FreeRTOS uses **preemptive priority-based scheduling** with optional time-slicing for tasks of equal priority.
- [x] **Tick rate** is set by `configTICK_RATE_HZ` (default 1000 Hz). Higher tick rate reduces latency but increases CPU overhead.
- [x] **Queues** pass data (FIFO, copy by value). `xQueueSend()` blocks if full; `xQueueReceive()` blocks if empty.
- [x] **Binary semaphores** signal events. **Counting semaphores** manage resource pools. **Mutexes** protect shared resources and offer **priority inheritance**.
- [x] Always use `*FromISR` variants (`xQueueSendFromISR`, `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`) inside ISRs, and call `portYIELD_FROM_ISR()` if a higher-priority task was woken.
- [x] **Software timers** are managed by the **Tmr Svc daemon task**. Two types: **one-shot** and **auto-reload**.
- [x] Do not block inside a timer callback — it runs in the daemon task's context.
- [x] **Liu & Layland bound** for RMS schedulability: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Sufficient but not necessary; use Response Time Analysis for exact results.
- [x] **Priority inversion** is solved by **mutexes** (priority inheritance), not binary semaphores.
- [x] `configMAX_PRIORITIES` sets the number of priority levels; minimum 1, typical 5–32.
- [x] `portMAX_DELAY` means infinite block; `pdMS_TO_TICKS(ms)` converts ms to ticks for portability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid (Mnemonic): "Q-S-M-E"**
> **Q**ueues — carry data; **S**emaphores — signal events; **M**utexes — protect resources (with inheritance); **E**vent Groups — multi-bit sync. Use this order to remember which primitive to pick for each scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing an IoT-Based Home Automation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT418 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing an IoT-Based Home Automation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **IoT-Based Home Automation System** is an embedded cyber-physical system that integrates a **microcontroller unit (MCU)**, **sensor nodes**, **actuator subsystems**, and a **wireless communication stack** to enable remote monitoring, intelligent decision-making, and control of household appliances and environmental parameters over the **Internet Protocol (IP) network**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT418 – Microcontrollers, Module 4), such a system is engineered around a resource-constrained MCU that acts as the *edge node*, bridging the physical world (sensors/loads) with the digital cloud (servers/mobile clients) using lightweight IoT protocols.

The system is governed by three functional pillars:

1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors (temperature, LDR, PIR, gas) producing analog/digital signals.
2. **Network Layer** — Wireless transceivers (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa) for data transport.
3. **Application Layer** — Cloud platforms (ThingSpeak, Blynk, AWS IoT, Firebase) and mobile dashboards for HMI (Human-Machine Interface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The design must explicitly demonstrate microcontroller-centric concepts — GPIO control, ADC for sensor digitization, **UART/SPI/I²C** for peripheral communication, **timer-driven scheduling**, and **interrupt-driven actuation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home is a human body:

- The **microcontroller** is the **brain** — it receives signals, makes decisions, and sends commands.
- The **sensors** are the **five senses** — they perceive temperature, light, motion, and gas.
- The **actuators** (relays, motors, solenoids) are the **muscles** — they physically switch appliances ON/OFF.
- The **Wi-Fi module + cloud** is the **nervous system connecting to a doctor** — the homeowner sitting in an office can "feel" what is happening at home and remotely "instruct" appliances via a smartphone.

So, designing an IoT home automation system is essentially **building a small digital nervous system for a house**, where the microcontroller plays the role of the central processing brain, sensors gather raw data, actuators execute physical actions, and the internet acts as a long-distance communication channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A "microcontroller-based automation" system differs from a pure "IoT" system by the presence of an **embedded MCU running firmware**. IoT without an MCU is merely a remote-controlled device; with an MCU, it becomes *intelligent, programmable, and adaptive*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics and IEEE/IEC standards govern IoT home automation design:

- **Supply Voltage (V<sub>CC</sub>):** Typically **3.3 V** (ESP32/ESP8266) or **5 V** (Arduino UNO).
- **Wi-Fi Standard:** **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n** (2.4 GHz ISM band, ~**100 m** indoor range).
- **MQTT Keep-Alive Interval:** **60 seconds** (default per OASIS Standard).
- **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit** (Arduino UNO) → 1024 discrete levels.
- **ADC Reference Voltage:** **5 V** (default) → 1 LSB = 4.88 mV.
- **Relay Coil Voltage:** **5 V DC** for low-voltage signal switching; contacts rated at **230 V AC, 10 A**.
- **Power Consumption (ESP8266):** **70 mA** average (active), **10 µA** (deep sleep).
- **Cloud Latency Target:** **< 200 ms** for real-time control loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System-Level Functional Decomposition

An IoT home automation system is decomposed into **five tightly coupled subsystems**, each mapped to specific microcontroller peripherals:

| Subsystem | Hardware Element | MCU Peripheral | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Interface** | DHT11, LM35, PIR, MQ-2, LDR | **ADC** + GPIO | Digitize environmental parameters |
| **Actuator Driver** | Relay module, SSR, BJT/MOSFET, H-Bridge | **GPIO** + PWM | Switch AC/DC loads, control motors |
| **User Input** | Push buttons, rotary encoders, keypad | **External Interrupts (INT0/INT1)** | Local override and manual control |
| **Display** | 16×2 LCD, OLED (SSD1306), 7-seg | **I²C / SPI** | Show real-time status and IP address |
| **Communication** | ESP8266 (Wi-Fi), HC-05 (BT), nRF24L01 | **UART / SPI** | Wireless data exchange with cloud/mobile |
| **Timing/Scheduling** | RTC (DS3231) | **I²C** + Timer | Time-stamping and scheduled operations |

### 2.2 Operational Flow Logic (Step-by-Step)

The end-to-end operation of the system follows a deterministic **Sense → Process → Decide → Actuate → Communicate** cycle:

1. **Power-On Reset (POR):** MCU initializes clock (e.g., **16 MHz** crystal), configures GPIO directions, ADC channel selection, and UART baud rate (typically **115200 bps**).
2. **Wi-Fi Association:** MCU drives ESP8266 via AT commands (`AT+CWJAP="SSID","PASS"`) to join the local Access Point.
3. **Cloud Handshake:** MCU establishes a **MQTT** or **HTTP** connection to a broker (e.g., `mqtt://broker.hivemq.com:1883`).
4. **Sensor Polling:** On every **Timer0 overflow** (configurable, e.g., every 2 seconds), the MCU reads analog/digital sensors via ADC.
5. **Local Decision Logic:** Threshold comparison (e.g., if T > 30°C, switch ON fan relay) executes *immediately* — ensuring fail-safe local control.
6. **Cloud Publish:** Telemetry payload (JSON) is published to a topic like `home/livingroom/temperature`.
7. **Cloud Subscribe:** Incoming commands (`home/livingroom/fan/cmd = ON`) interrupt the main loop and trigger actuator updates.
8. **Acknowledgment:** MCU echoes the new actuator state back to the cloud for synchronization.

### 2.3 Communication Protocol Stack

The system leverages a **layered protocol model**, conceptually aligned with the simplified **TCP/IP stack**:

| Layer | Protocol Used | KTU 2024 Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | MQTT, HTTP/HTTPS, CoAP | Lightweight publish/subscribe vs. request/response |
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883 for MQTT, 80 for HTTP) | Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented |
| **Network** | IP (IPv4) | Logical addressing of MCU and cloud broker |
| **Link / Physical** | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), UART (TTL) | RF modulation at **2.4 GHz**, baud-rate framing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MQTT over HTTP?** MQTT uses a **persistent TCP connection** with a **2-byte fixed header** (minimum), reducing overhead by ~**80%** compared to HTTP. This is critical for bandwidth-constrained MCUs.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ADC Digital Output** | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | $D$ = digital code, $n$ = resolution bits, $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage | Volts, dimensionless |
| **ADC Step Size (LSB)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Quantization increment per bit | V / LSB |
| **Sensor to Engineering Units** | $T = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{(2^n - 1) \cdot 10\,mV}$ | LM35 scale: 10 mV/°C | °C |
| **Relay Switching Power** | $P_{load} = V_{AC} \cdot I_{load} \cdot \cos\phi$ | AC load apparent power | Watts |
| **MQTT Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Payload Size}}{\text{Round-Trip Time}}$ | Effective data rate | Bytes/s |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (Wi-Fi)** | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ = distance (km), $f$ = frequency (MHz) | dB |
| **RC Time Constant** | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Sensor filter / debounce timing | Seconds |
| **Baud Rate Divisor** | $BR = \dfrac{F_{osc}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | UART configuration (AVR/Arduino) | bps |
| **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Fan/dimmer control | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Power (Battery Life)** | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy budget for backup | Joules / Wh |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Significance

IoT home automation is the **flagship application domain** that justifies low-power MCU development. The global smart-home market exceeded **USD 100 billion** in 2024, and KTU graduates are expected to design systems used in:

- **Energy Management:** Smart meters (electricity, water, gas) reduce household consumption by **15–30%**.
- **Assistive Living:** PIR + fall-detection sensors for elderly care, integrated with GSM modules for SOS alerts.
- **Security:** Door sensors + IP cameras with cloud-based face recognition triggers.
- **Industrial Spinoffs:** The same architecture scales to **smart greenhouses**, **warehouse monitoring**, and **cold-chain logistics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners specifically test whether a student can *justify the choice* of MCU, protocol, and cloud platform — not just list them. Always link your design choice to a **constraint** (cost, power, range, latency).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Design Derivation: Sensor-to-Actuator Pipeline

The following step-by-step derivation demonstrates how a **temperature sensor (LM35)** signal is read by the MCU, scaled into engineering units, threshold-checked, and finally used to actuate a **fan relay** via a GPIO pin.

**Given Data (as per typical KTU 2024 design problem):**
- MCU: ATmega328P (Arduino UNO), $F_{osc} = 16\,MHz$
- ADC Reference Voltage: $V_{ref} = 5.0\,V$
- ADC Resolution: $n = 10$ bits
- LM35 Scale: $10\,mV/°C$
- Threshold: $T_{set} = 30\,°C$
- Hysteresis: $\Delta T = 2\,°C$ (to prevent relay chattering)

---

#### Step 1 — Compute ADC Step Size (LSB Voltage)

The quantization step of the ADC is derived from the reference voltage and the number of discrete levels:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024}
$$

$$
\Delta V = 0.0048828125\,V \approx 4.883\,mV
$$

**Logic:** Each increment in the ADC register corresponds to a real-world voltage change of ~4.883 mV. This sets the *minimum detectable temperature change* for the LM35.

---

#### Step 2 — Minimum Detectable Temperature Resolution

The LM35 produces 10 mV/°C, so the minimum temperature the ADC can resolve is:

$$
\delta T_{min} = \frac{\Delta V}{10\,mV/°C} = \frac{4.883\,mV}{10\,mV/°C}
$$

$$
\delta T_{min} = 0.4883\,°C \approx 0.49\,°C
$$

**Logic:** A 10-bit ADC on a 5 V reference with an LM35 sensor gives a temperature resolution of about **0.49 °C per LSB** — sufficient for HVAC control.

---

#### Step 3 — Convert ADC Reading (D) to Voltage

For an arbitrary digital reading $D$:

$$
V_{in} = D \cdot \Delta V = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1024}
$$

**Example:** Suppose $D = 307$ (a typical value at 30 °C):

$$
V_{in} = 307 \cdot \frac{5.0}{1024} = 1.4990\,V
$$

**Logic:** The ADC code is meaningless without this conversion; it must be re-mapped to a physical voltage.

---

#### Step 4 — Convert Voltage to Temperature (LM35 Linearization)

LM35 has a linear transfer function (no offset compensation needed):

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{10\,mV/°C} = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01\,V/°C}
$$

Substituting Step 3:

$$
T = 307 \cdot \frac{5.0}{1024 \cdot 0.01} = 307 \cdot 0.4883
$$

$$
T \approx 149.9 \cdot 0.4883 \approx 30.0\,°C
$$

**Logic:** The combined scaling factor $\frac{V_{ref}}{1024 \cdot 0.01}$ = 0.4883 °C/LSB is a **design constant** that is hard-coded in firmware for real-time execution.

---

#### Step 5 — Threshold Comparison with Hysteresis (Schmitt Trigger Logic)

To prevent relay *chattering* when the temperature oscillates around 30 °C, hysteresis is introduced:

$$
\text{IF } \quad T \geq T_{set} + \frac{\Delta T}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAN} = \text{ON}
$$

$$
\text{IF } \quad T \leq T_{set} - \frac{\Delta T}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAN} = \text{OFF}
$$

Substituting $T_{set} = 30$ and $\Delta T = 2$:

$$
T \geq 31\,°C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAN ON}
$$

$$
T \leq 29\,°C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAN OFF}
$$

$$
29\,°C < T < 31\,°C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{State Held (no change)}
$$

**Logic:** This implements a **software Schmitt trigger**, eliminating mechanical relay wear caused by rapid switching.

---

#### Step 6 — GPIO Actuation via Relay Driver

The MCU GPIO pin cannot directly drive a 5 V relay coil (current ~70 mA exceeds the 40 mA ATmega328P pin limit). A **BJT driver** (e.g., BC547) or **logic-level MOSFET** (e.g., IRLZ44N) is used:

$$
I_{base} = \frac{V_{IO} - V_{BE}}{R_{base}} = \frac{5.0 - 0.7}{1\,k\Omega} = 4.3\,mA
$$

With a current gain $\beta = 100$:

$$
I_{C(max)} = \beta \cdot I_{base} = 100 \cdot 4.3 = 430\,mA
$$

**Logic:** A 4.3 mA base current is well within MCU drive limits, while providing 430 mA collector capacity — **6× the relay requirement** — ensuring deep saturation and low $V_{CE(sat)}$.

---

#### Step 7 — Cloud Telemetry Payload (MQTT/JSON)

The MCU publishes data to a topic using JSON encoding:

```json
{
  "device_id": "HOME_LR_01",
  "timestamp": 1718901234,
  "temperature_C": 30.0,
  "humidity_pct": 65.0,
  "fan_state": "ON",
  "light_state": "OFF"
}
```

**Logic:** The JSON payload is human-readable for debugging and machine-parseable for cloud dashboards. A typical payload of **128 bytes** transmitted over MQTT at 115200 bps UART-to-Wi-Fi incurs **~9 ms** of airtime.

---

#### Step 8 — Total System Latency Budget

$$
t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{process} + t_{relay} + t_{network}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 100\,\mu s + 50\,\mu s + 10\,ms + 50\,ms \approx 60.15\,ms
$$

**Logic:** This is well within the **< 200 ms** cloud latency target, validating the design for *near-real-time* home control.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Design Decision | Option A | Option B | Selection Criterion | Chosen Option |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MCU Platform** | Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) | ESP32 | Wi-Fi built-in? | **ESP32** |
| **Wireless Protocol** | Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz) | ZigBee | Range vs. power | **Wi-Fi** (existing home router) |
| **Cloud Platform** | ThingSpeak | AWS IoT Core | Cost vs. scalability | **ThingSpeak** (academic projects) |
| **MQTT vs. HTTP** | HTTP REST | MQTT Pub/Sub | Overhead vs. reliability | **MQTT** |
| **Relay Type** | Electromechanical (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | Switching speed vs. cost | **EMR** (low cost) |
| **Sensor** | DHT11 | DHT22 | Accuracy vs. price | **DHT22** (better accuracy) |
| **Power Backup** | None | Li-ion + TP4056 | Uptime vs. cost | **Li-ion** (UPS for MCU only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** The matrix shows that every design choice involves a **trade-off triangle** — *Cost, Performance, and Power*. KTU answers that discuss these trade-offs score significantly higher than those that just list components.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End IoT Home Automation Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents a complete **block-level functional architecture** mapping all hardware/software interactions in the system. Each node is a functional block, with edges showing data/control flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENSORS["SENSOR ARRAY LDR DHT22 PIR MQ2 LM35"] --> MCU
    MCU["MICROCONTROLLER ATMEGA328P OR ESP32 FIRMWARE LOGIC"]
    BTN["MANUAL INPUT PUSH BUTTONS KEYPAD"] --> MCU
    RTC["RTC MODULE DS3231 I2C BUS"] --> MCU
    DISPLAY["OLED DISPLAY SSD1306 I2C"] --> MCU
    MCU --> RELAY["RELAY DRIVER BJT OR MOSFET"]
    MCU --> PWM["PWM OUTPUT FAN DIMMER SPEED"]
    RELAY --> LOAD["AC LOADS LIGHT FAN GEYSER"]
    PWM --> LOAD
    MCU --> WIFI["WIFI MODULE ESP8266 OR BUILTIN UART 115200 BPS"]
    WIFI --> CLOUD["CLOUD BROKER HIVEMQ OR THINGSPEAK MQTT 1883"]
    CLOUD --> MOBILE["MOBILE APP BLYNK OR CUSTOM"]
    CLOUD --> WEB["WEB DASHBOARD HTML DASH PLOTLY"]
    MOBILE -.COMMANDS.-> WIFI
    WEB -.COMMANDS.-> WIFI
    MCU --> BUZZER["BUZZER ALERT GAS LEAK INTRUSION"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **central column** is the MCU — every other block either feeds data *into* it (sensors, buttons, RTC) or is *driven by* it (relay, PWM, display, Wi-Fi).
- **Solid arrows** indicate physical sensor/control lines; **dashed arrows** indicate bidirectional internet traffic.
- The **Cloud** is the cross-domain bridge between the local embedded system and remote user interfaces.

### 4.2 Firmware Execution Flow (Main Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["POWER ON RESET INITIALIZE GPIO ADC UART I2C"] --> WIFI_CONN
    WIFI_CONN["CONNECT TO WIFI SSID AND PASSWORD"] -->|FAIL| RETRY["RETRY AFTER 5 SECONDS"]
    RETRY --> WIFI_CONN
    WIFI_CONN -->|SUCCESS| MQTT_CONN
    MQTT_CONN["CONNECT TO MQTT BROKER PORT 1883 CLIENT ID UNIQUE"] -->|FAIL| RETRY
    MQTT_CONN -->|SUCCESS| SUBSCRIBE
    SUBSCRIBE["SUBSCRIBE TO COMMAND TOPIC HOME DEVICE CMD"] --> LOOP
    LOOP["MAIN LOOP ITERATION EVERY 2 SECONDS"] --> READ_SENSE
    READ_SENSE["READ ALL SENSORS ADC POLLING I2C QUERY"] --> DECIDE
    DECIDE["APPLY THRESHOLD AND HYSTERESIS LOGIC"] --> ACTUATE
    ACTUATE["UPDATE RELAY AND PWM OUTPUTS"] --> PUBLISH
    PUBLISH["PUBLISH TELEMETRY JSON TO MQTT TOPIC"] --> LOOP
    LOOP -.INTERRUPT.-> CMD_HANDLER
    CMD_HANDLER["INCOMING MQTT COMMAND PROCESS CALLBACK"] --> ACTUATE
```

**Key observations:**
- The **main loop** runs periodically (every 2 s) using a **non-blocking millis()** approach, not `delay()`.
- **Cloud commands** arrive as **asynchronous interrupts** on the UART receive line, processed in the callback.
- The system is **fail-safe**: Wi-Fi failure does not halt local automation — local thresholds still drive the relays.

### 4.3 MQTT Topic Hierarchy (Publish/Subscribe Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["HOME"]
    ROOT --> LR["LIVING ROOM"]
    ROOT --> BR["BEDROOM"]
    ROOT --> KT["KITCHEN"]
    LR --> LR_T["HOME LR TEMPERATURE"]
    LR --> LR_F["HOME LR FAN STATE"]
    LR --> LR_L["HOME LR LIGHT STATE"]
    BR --> BR_T["HOME BR TEMPERATURE"]
    BR --> BR_M["HOME BR MOTION"]
    KT --> KT_G["HOME KT GAS ALARM"]
    KT --> KT_C["HOME KT COMMAND"]
```

**Logic of the topic design:**
- Hierarchical, slash-separated topics allow **wildcard subscriptions** — for example, subscribing to `home/+/temperature` (single-level wildcard) collects temperature from every room.
- This pattern is **QoS-aware** — clients can choose Quality of Service level 0, 1, or 2 per topic.

### 4.4 Hardware Wiring Schematic (Logical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC 5V"] --> LM35["LM35 PIN1 VCC"]
    VCC --> RELAY["RELAY MODULE VCC"]
    LM35 -->|VOUT PIN2| ADC["MCU PIN A0 ADC CH0"]
    VCC --> R1["RESISTOR 1K"]
    R1 --> BJT["BC547 BASE"]
    BJT --> RELAY_COIL["RELAY COIL"]
    RELAY_COIL --> GND1["GND"]
    MCU["MCU PIN D8 DIGITAL OUTPUT"] --> BJT
    RELAY -.->|COM AND NO| AC["230V AC LOAD"]
    AC --> NEUTRAL["AC NEUTRAL"]
    WIFI["ESP8266 VCC 3.3V"] -->|TX RX UART| MCU
    WIFI --> CLOUD["CLOUD SERVER"]
    MCU -.-> DISPLAY["OLED SDA SCL I2C"]
    MCU -.-> BUZZER["BUZZER PIN D9"]
```

**Reading this schematic:**
- LM35 is wired in its **standard 3-pin configuration** (VCC, VOUT, GND) with no external calibration components.
- The **BC547 BJT** operates in *saturation* when the MCU drives D8 HIGH, energizing the relay coil.
- The relay's **COM** (common) and **NO** (normally open) contacts form a switch in series with the 230 V AC load — this is the **galvanic isolation** boundary between the low-voltage MCU domain and the mains-voltage domain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Advisory (KTU Lab Note):** Mains-voltage wiring (230 V AC) must only be handled under faculty supervision with proper insulation, isolation transformers, and MCB protection. The MCU side must be **optically isolated** (using an opto-coupler like PC817) or relay-isolated from the AC side.
