## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define IoT. List any **four** sensors commonly used in IoT-based home automation systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects (*"things"*) embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies that enable them to collect, exchange, and act on data over the internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

Four sensors used in IoT-based home automation:

1. **DHT11/DHT22** — measures temperature and humidity.
2. **PIR (HC-SR501)** — detects human motion (security).
3. **MQ-2 / MQ-135** — detects gas leakage (LPG, smoke, CO).
4. **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — detects ambient light for automatic lighting.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four correctly listed sensors with one-line use. Full 3 marks require clarity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare **MQTT** and **HTTP** protocols in the context of IoT. Which is preferred for microcontroller-based systems and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HTTP | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Request/Response (Client-Server) | Publish/Subscribe (Broker-based) |
| **Header Overhead** | ~200–800 bytes | 2 bytes (minimum fixed header) |
| **Connection** | New TCP connection per request | Single persistent TCP connection |
| **Power Efficiency** | Low (connection churn) | High (long-lived socket) |
| **Push Support** | Polling required | Native push to subscribers |
| **Use Case Fit** | Web APIs, dashboards | Sensor telemetry, control |

**Preferred for MCUs: MQTT** — because MCUs have limited RAM (typically < 320 KB) and constrained bandwidth; MQTT's small header and persistent connection drastically reduce power and memory consumption. *(3 marks: 2 for comparison table + 1 for justified conclusion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Design an **IoT-based smart lighting system** for a home with the following requirements:
- Automatic ON/OFF based on ambient light (LDR) and human presence (PIR).
- Manual override via a push button.
- Real-time status monitoring on a mobile app.

Draw the **complete block diagram**, identify the **microcontroller and communication protocol** used, and explain the **firmware flow**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LDR["LDR VOLTAGE DIVIDER"] --> MCU
    PIR["PIR HC SR501"] --> MCU
    BTN["PUSH BUTTON MANUAL OVERRIDE"] --> MCU
    MCU["ESP32 MCU WIFI INTEGRATED ARDUINO IDE"] --> RELAY
    RELAY["RELAY MODULE 5V"] --> LAMP["230V AC LAMP"]
    MCU --> WIFI["WIFI 802.11 B G N"]
    WIFI --> APP["MOBILE APP BLYNK OR THINGVIEW"]
    APP -.COMMANDS.-> MCU
```

**Hardware Selection (2 marks):**
- **Microcontroller:** ESP32 (built-in Wi-Fi, dual-core, 520 KB SRAM, 12-bit ADC, low-cost).
- **Communication:** **MQTT over Wi-Fi (TCP port 1883)** — lightweight, persistent.
- **LDR Interface:** Voltage divider with 10 kΩ resistor → output to **GPIO34 (ADC1_CH6)**.
- **PIR Interface:** Digital output → **GPIO27**.
- **Relay:** 5 V coil, optically isolated, driven via **GPIO26** through BC547 BJT.
- **Manual Button:** **GPIO35** with internal pull-up, configured as **external interrupt (INT1)**.

**Firmware Flow (3 marks):**

1. `setup()` initializes serial (115200 bps), configures GPIO directions, sets ADC resolution to 12 bits, and connects to Wi-Fi.
2. ESP32 connects to MQTT broker `broker.hivemq.com` with unique client ID.
3. Subscribes to topic `home/livingroom/light/cmd`.
4. `loop()` runs every 500 ms:
   - Reads LDR via `analogRead(34)` and PIR via `digitalRead(27)`.
   - Computes lux proxy: $L \propto \frac{1}{ADC}$ (inverted).
   - **Logic:** $\text{IF } L < \text{threshold}_{dark} \text{ AND } PIR = HIGH \text{ AND } manualFlag = OFF \Rightarrow \text{LAMP} = ON$.
   - Manual button press toggles `manualFlag` (overrides automation).
   - Publishes JSON `{ "lux": X, "pir": Y, "lamp_state": Z }` to `home/livingroom/light/state`.

**Justification (2 marks):**
- ESP32 chosen over Arduino UNO + ESP8266 due to **single-chip integration**, reducing BOM and PCB complexity.
- MQTT chosen over HTTP for **persistent connection** and **push capability**.

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **MQTT publish/subscribe model**. List the **three Quality of Service (QoS) levels** in MQTT and state when each is used.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MQTT Model (4 marks):**
MQTT follows a **publish/subscribe** pattern with three components:
- **Publisher** — the MCU publishing sensor data.
- **Subscriber** — the mobile app or dashboard receiving data.
- **Broker** — central server (e.g., HiveMQ, Mosquitto) that routes messages.

**Example:** The ESP32 publishes temperature `{"T": 30}` to topic `home/livingroom/temperature`. The broker receives this and forwards it to all clients subscribed to that topic — for example, a mobile dashboard and a data logger. The dashboard does *not* need to know the ESP32's IP address; it only knows the broker.

**QoS Levels (3 marks):**

| QoS Level | Name | Delivery Guarantee | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget (no ACK) | Non-critical sensor logs |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | PUBACK handshake (duplicates possible) | Periodic telemetry |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | 4-step handshake (no duplicates) | Critical commands (door lock unlock) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of an IoT system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in the context of a home automation system.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The standard **3-layer (or 5-layer) IoT architecture** is presented below:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["PERCEPTION LAYER SENSORS ACTUATORS RFID"]
    L2["NETWORK LAYER GATEWAY WIFI ZIGBEE LTE"]
    L3["PROCESSING LAYER CLOUD EDGE COMPUTING"]
    L4["APPLICATION LAYER MOBILE APP DASHBOARD"]
    L5["BUSINESS LAYER ANALYTICS BILLING AI INSIGHTS"]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

**Layer-wise Roles (7 marks):**

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors/Actuators):** LDR, DHT22, PIR, MQ-2 collect environmental data; relays, motors, dimmers execute actions. **In home automation:** Light, temperature, and motion sensing; appliance control.

2. **Network/Gateway Layer:** Transmits data using Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, or cellular (4G/5G). **In home automation:** ESP32's Wi-Fi pushes data to a cloud broker; a ZigBee hub aggregates multiple room sensors.

3. **Processing Layer:** Cloud or edge computing for data storage, analytics, and decision-making. **In home automation:** AWS IoT Core processes rules ("if motion at night → turn on porch light").

4. **Application Layer:** User-facing interfaces — mobile apps, web dashboards, voice assistants. **In home automation:** Blynk app for switch control; Alexa/Google Home voice commands.

5. **Business Layer (Optional):** Higher-level analytics, billing, AI/ML insights. **In home automation:** Energy consumption reports, predictive maintenance alerts.

---

**(b)** Describe the **role of the ESP8266/ESP32** as a Wi-Fi-enabled microcontroller in IoT home automation. List **any four AT commands** used for Wi-Fi configuration and explain their functions.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of ESP8266/ESP32 (4 marks):**
The **ESP8266 (or its successor ESP32)** is a low-cost, system-on-chip (SoC) microcontroller with integrated **2.4 GHz Wi-Fi** and (in ESP32) **Bluetooth**. It operates at **3.3 V**, runs at **80–240 MHz**, contains built-in **TCP/IP stack**, and can be programmed via the **Arduino IDE**, **MicroPython**, or **ESP-IDF**. In IoT home automation, it acts as:

1. The **edge node** running sensor acquisition and local decision logic.
2. The **wireless gateway** connecting local sensors to the internet.
3. The **MQTT client** publishing telemetry and receiving commands.
4. A **soft AP** (Access Point) for direct mobile configuration when no router exists.

**Key AT Commands (3 marks):**

| AT Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `AT` | Tests module responsiveness; replies `OK` if functional. |
| `AT+CWMODE=1` | Sets station mode (client connecting to a router). |
| `AT+CWJAP="SSID","PASS"` | Joins the specified Wi-Fi network with password. |
| `AT+CIFSR` | Returns the assigned IP address of the module. |
| `AT+CIPSTART="TCP","broker",1883` | Opens a TCP socket to the MQTT broker. |
| `AT+CIPSEND=<length>` | Sends data of specified byte length over the socket. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):**
> 1. **Block diagrams without labels** lose 1–2 marks — always label all blocks and arrows.
> 2. **Listing components without justification** is penalized — explicitly state *why* you chose a protocol, MCU, or cloud.
> 3. **Skipping the ADC step-size calculation** when sensor interfacing is asked — KTU board examiners specifically test this numerical ability.
> 4. **Forgetting safety in mains-voltage circuits** — never omit the relay isolation and BJT driver discussion.
> 5. **No mention of MQTT QoS** when explaining the protocol — this is a high-weight KTU 2024 Scheme keyword.
> 6. **Hand-waving the firmware flow** — every "design" question requires a *concrete* `setup()` + `loop()` explanation, not vague descriptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Core Definition:** IoT home automation = **Microcontroller + Sensors + Actuators + Wireless Communication + Cloud + User Interface**, designed to remotely monitor and control household devices.

✅ **MCU Choice:** **ESP32** is the most common 2024 KTU answer for IoT projects due to built-in Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, dual-core, and Arduino IDE compatibility.

✅ **Three IoT Layers (Memorize):** **Perception → Network → Application** (extended to 5 layers with Processing and Business).

✅ **Communication Protocol:** **MQTT** for telemetry (lightweight, persistent TCP, publish/subscribe); **HTTP** for occasional web requests.

✅ **MQTT Components:** **Publisher, Subscriber, Broker, Topic, QoS (0/1/2)**.

✅ **Key Sensors:** **DHT22** (temp+humidity), **PIR** (motion), **LDR** (light), **MQ-2** (gas), **LM35** (analog temperature, 10 mV/°C).

✅ **ADC Formula (High-Weight):** $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ and Step Size $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.

✅ **Relay Driver:** MCU GPIO → **BJT/MOSFET** → Relay coil (never direct drive). Always add a **flyback diode** across the relay coil.

✅ **Hysteresis:** Use a **Schmitt trigger logic** (e.g., 29 °C–31 °C band) to prevent relay chattering.

✅ **JSON Payload Structure:** `{device_id, timestamp, sensor_value, actuator_state}` — standard IoT telemetry format.

✅ **Wi-Fi Standard:** **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n**, 2.4 GHz, typical indoor range ~**100 m**, throughput ~**50 Mbps** in 802.11n.

✅ **Power Budget:** Plan for **ESP32 deep-sleep (~10 µA)** to enable battery-backed sensor nodes lasting months.

✅ **Cloud Platforms (KTU Acceptable):** **ThingSpeak** (educational), **Blynk** (mobile-centric), **AWS IoT Core** (industrial), **Firebase** (real-time DB).

✅ **Safety First:** Mains-voltage (230 V AC) isolation via **relay/optocoupler**; never connect AC directly to MCU.

✅ **Exam Mantra:** Every KTU answer must include — **Diagram + Component Justification + Firmware Flow + Numerical Example**.
